Burch III, president of Massey Burch Investment Group Inc., was elected a director of this telecommunications services and engineering concern.  Article 2 *******************   



A WINNING BET. (HIGH TECH INVESTOR TOMMY DAVIS STARTS CATALYTICA ASSOCIATES INC.) 

By Thomas P. Murphy 
835 words 
14 January 1985
Forbes 
304 
English
Copyright Forbes Inc. 1985 

"I still don't understand what you are trying to do, but I know there is something there." 

With those words, Tommy Davis, dean of Silicon Valley's venture capitalists, launched two improbable partners in a high-risk-for-very-high-gain venture. The partners are a nice Jewish boy from Quito, Ecuador whose name is Ricardo B. Levy and a self-described Sicilian from Newark, N.J. named James A. Cusumano. The venture is called Catalytica Associates, Inc. 

What will Catalytica do? I sympathize with Davis' frustration, but let me try to explain. 

Levy, 39, and Cusumano, 42, both Ph.D. chemists (Stanford and Rutgers, respectively), believe that the world's chemical industry is on the brink of a revolution. Chemical process development, where Catalytica will concentrate, has always been based primarily on Edisonian research--if one thing doesn't work, try another. Edison experimented with thousands of different materials before he happened upon tungsten to make the filament for the electric light bulb. The world's chemical laboratories still proceed with a not too dissimilar approach. 

Levy and Cusumano think they can leapfrog this expensive and unpredictable process and take chemical research into what they call "molecular engineering." They think they can do this because, on a molecular level, both the theoretical understanding and the instrumentation are becoming available, just as a decade earlier theory and new instrumentation converged to create the biotechnology industry. 

Specifically, they are going after new catalysts for existing processes. Catalysts are the materials that create chemical reactions--the key to the rest of the process. According to Cusumano, they are looking for "knock out" economics. They want to develop catalysts that are so superior they economically obsolete whole plants and, coincidentally, send the big companies beating a path to Catalytica's Mountain View, Calif. door. 

None of this comes as startling news to researchers in the industry itself. In fact, Levy and Cusumano were both working at Exxon's big central research laboratory in New Jersey when one of them detected an entrepreneurial glint in the eye of the other. They quit Exxon and, in collaboration with Michele Boudart, professor of chemical engineering at Standford, set up a consulting business in 1974. It was pleasantly successful, and by 1980 the firm had 30 people and had finished 200 jobs all over the world. 

But solving other people's problems wasn't where it was at for Levy and Cusumano. The trouble was, where it was at was going to cost a lot of money. Enter Tommy Davis, founder of the Mayfield Fund of Palo Alto, Calif. But not directly. The introduction was arranged by one of Catalytica's board members, Paul Cook, who is founder and chairman of Raychem Corp. In fact, part of Catalytica's remarkable ability to raise money for a complicated and high-risk venture was the quality of its advisers, including four professors from Standford. One, Henry Taube, is a 1983 Nobel Prize winner in chemistry. 

Tommy Davis, then, had more to go on than the word of two conspicuously bright and eager men. Part of the chemistry establishment was firmly endorsing the people and their ideas when Davis agreed to back the new business. Eventually, along with Rothschild Inc. in New York, Davis invested about $3 million in 1983 and then, a couple of months ago, $2 million more in venture money, and up to $20 million more in a complicated joint venture arrangement with Lubrizol Enterprises, Inc. 

What happens now? According to Cusumano, the financings buy Catalytica four to five years of running time. They are not dismissing lightly the technical problems they face. 

Meantime, staff is growing. There are 50 people, half with Ph.D.s, and new instrumentation is on the way. A nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (try saying that four times without stuttering) is coming. That hardware, to put what things cost in perspective, is $500,000. 

What about competition? There is none, as far as Cusumano knows, from small companies. The big laboratories are on the same trail, but the two partners both have big lab experience and, in typical Silicon Valley fashion, believe they can beat them. Big labs are hobbled, they say, by bureaucracy, and the scientists working in them lack the incentive--the chance to make a lot of money. 

What will they have if they succeed in developing some knockout, quality new catalysts? Patents, primarily. In the electronics industry having only patents would scare a venture capitalist half to death. The chemical industry is different. Historically, patents have been upheld by the courts, and the industry has a reputation of really paying royalties for the use of them. Beyond that, the leverage of knockout improvement is awesome. Many chemical plants are $100 million-plus capital investments. Imagine replacing a few of those. 

It wouldn't be the first time Tommy Davis had won by basically betting on people.

 Article 3 *******************   



MAPPING THE ATOMIC WORLD ONE ATOM AT A TIME 

Roger Schulman in New York 
888 words 
2 September 1985
Business Week 
Pg. 72D 
Vol. Number 2910 
English
Copyright 1985 McGraw-Hill, Inc. 

It looks like a concoction straight out of Jules Verne: a stainless-steel contraption sprouting rivet-rimmed portholes and outsized knobs that seems more appropriate for Captain Nemo's submarine than for a modern electronics laboratory. Inside this ungainly housing resides a painfully delicate instrument--someone tiptoeing through the next room can throw it out of whack. Yet scientists are only too happy to put up with such exquisiteness. This is the most powerful microscope ever built, and it is fast uncovering fascinating new secrets of the microworld. 

''It's like turning on a light in a dark room,'' declares Marc H. Brodsky, a physicist at International Business Machines Corp.'s Thomas J. Watson Research Center in Yorktown Heights, N. Y. Called a scanning tunneling microscope, or STM, the instrument can magnify the surface of a material 1 million times. That's 20 times better than what the finest electron microscope can manage--and powerful enough to show individual atoms locked in atomic embrace. 

The world's first STM was built three years ago at IBM's lab in Zurich, Switzerland, culminating three years of development work. Since then, the microscope has rapidly become an essential tool for leading-edge research in microelectronics, and nearly a dozen more STMs have been hand-built at institutions as far-flung as Stanford University and the University of Madrid. Now, industrial research labs are starting to build tunneling microscopes as well--Ford Motor Co. is a case in point. OVERNIGHT SOLUTIONS. Scientists have already turned up some surprises with this new tool. With their homemade STM, researchers at AT&T Bell Telephone Laboratories in Short Hills, N. J., recently discovered puzzling, atom-size pits in the silicon crystals that are the foundation of modern semiconductor devices. Theoretically, the pits just shouldn't exist, says Venky Narayanamurti, a Bell Labs researcher. Consequently, ''the STM is completely revising our understanding of silicon,'' he adds. 

AT&T's STM also produced the firstpictures of the bond between siliconand germanium, a material used tomake microchips. ''We had debated about that endlessly,'' says Narayanamurti. ''The pictures simply solved the problem overnight.'' Knowing more about the atomic structure of semiconductor crystals may prove vital in designing microprocessors that are thousands of times more powerful than today's best computer-on-a-chip. 

Researchers are beginning to use the microscope to study biological molecules, too. The first fuzzy images of DNA and a common virus have helped confirm theories about the structures of these tiny organic chemicals. Such knowledge may one day enable genetic engineers to manipulate genes more precisely in order to develop new medical treatments. ''It looks like the STM could make major contributions in many fields,'' says Randall M. Feenstra, a scientist who works with one of the IBM microscopes. Unlike optical and electron microscopes, an STM uses no lenses of any kind. Instead, it ''traces'' the surface features of a material with a needle-sharp tungsten electrode. The tip of the needle is positioned an infinitesimally short distance from the surface under study. The gap is only 10 angstroms (0.00000004 in.) or less. TUNNEL VISION. At this minute distance, a phenomenon known as ''tunneling'' occurs: Electrons begin jumping from the tip of the electrode into the atoms that make up the material. The shorter the gap, the greater the electron flow. Computerized instruments monitor this flow and feed the information to a computer-controlled tripod that lifts and lowers the needle to keep the rate constant while the tip creeps ever so slowly across the surface of the material. 

An electronic plotter linked to the electrode draws a picture of the needle's journey, tracing the microcontours of the surface and revealing variations as subtle as one-tenth the diameter of an atom. Then, using image-enhancement techniques--similar to those that help spy satellites read license plates on cars from 20,000 mi. up--the line drawings are transformed into pictures of astounding clarity. Here are detailed maps of the plains, plateaus, and mountain ranges of the atomic world. 

''The trick,'' says Brodsky, ''is to get the thing to operate without the slightest vibration.'' To do that, the Zurich inventors devised an ingenious housing: a spring-cushioned platform wrapped in a magnetically isolated cocoon enclosed by a vacuum on a table protected by pneumatic shock absorbers. The vacuum not only insulates the mechanism from sound-wave vibrations but also maintains the surface cleanliness of the material being studied. The whole affair makes an STM perhaps the most stable object in the world. 

Ultimately, the technology may be adapted to convert the microscope into the world's smallest ''machine tool.'' Minute electrical charges delivered through the needle tip could, for example, trigger chemical reactions to create custom-tailored molecules. Or the needle could be used to etch semiconductor circuits less than an angstrom wide, many times smaller than those on today's tiniest chips. 

Meanwhile, technicians continue to improve the STM as a research tool. IBM is designing a ''pocket'' scope that is only about 10 in. high. ''We could probably design an STM that's easier to use,'' says Feenstra of IBM. ''But right now, we're more interested in doing the science and getting results.''

 Article 4 *******************   



Science & Technology A CRYSTAL-CLEAR VIEW OF THE WORLD'S TINY WONDERS --- A NEW MICROSCOPE PRODUCES AMAZINGLY DETAILED IMAGES OF ATOMS--USING ALMOST PRIMITIVE TECHNOLOGY 

Steve Dickman and Otis Port in New York 
1,204 words 
3 November 1986
Business Week 
Pg. 134 
Vol. Number 2971 
English
Copyright 1986 McGraw-Hill, Inc. 

For nearly 300 years, scientists have peered through lenses to peek into the microworld. Lensmakers refined the crude instrument invented by Dutch scientist Anton van Leeuwenhoek in 1674 into powerful optical microscopes that could discern bacteria. Then, in 1931, German scientist Ernst Ruska used a magnetic field as a lens to fashion the first of a generation of electron microscopes that would eventually produce fuzzy images of individual atoms. 

Now microscopy has made another leap. With a device called a scanning tunneling microscope (STM), scientists are viewing unprecedented vistas of the atomic structure of matter. Such a microscope recently astounded the scientific world by providing the first pictures ever of the electron bonds that hold atoms together. Incredibly, the lens is being shoved aside by a simple mechanical stylus that traces the contours of individual atoms the way a needle follows the grooves in a phonographic record. ''It's such a primitive instrument--a rusty nail that you scratch across the surface'' of some material, says co-inventor Gerd K. Binnig, a physicist at the IBM Research Laboratory in Zurich. ''When we told the people here in the lab what we wanted to do,'' says Heinrich Rohrer, the STM's other parent, ''they all said, 'You are completely crazy--but if it works, you'll get the Nobel prize.' '' Sure enough, in mid-October the Swedish Academy of Science awarded the Nobel prize in physics to the IBM scientists. The award came only five years after they built the first STM. Usually, the academy waits much longer. For instance, the two IBM researchers shared the physics prize with Ernst Ruska 55 years after his invention of the electron microscope. NEEDLE-SHARP. There seems little doubt that the STM is destined to become an indispensable tool in a wide range of disciplines. Other research labs have already built more than 60 copies of the microscope, and the number of STMs in operation is doubling every six months. STMs are now being used for basic research in biology, chemistry, and physics, as well as for applied work in bioengineering, metallurgy, and microelectronics. Says physicist Robert C. Jaklevic of the Ford Scientific Laboratory in Dearborn, Mich.: ''Someday, every lab will have one.'' 

The key to the STM's performance is keeping a needle-sharp stylus an excruciatingly constant distance from the surface of a sample. This is possible thanks to a mysterious phenomenon known as tunneling: Electrons at the probe's tip disappear and instantly reappear on the sample's surface, creating a minute electrical current. Because that current varies with the distance between the probe and the surface, lifting and lowering the probe to keep the current constant will produce a line tracing the peaks of individual atoms and the valleys between. Dozens of such traces at minuscule intervals provide a computer with the raw data to generate a three-dimensional image of the surface. 

That simple device can be astonishingly precise. A single atom is approximately 1 angstrom, or 2.5 billionths of an inch, in diameter. The STM can trace some details as tiny as 0.1 angstrom. Its average resolution is now 0.7 angstrom--twice as powerful as the world's best electron microscope. 'EXTREMELY EXCITING.' Researchers are using STMs to tackle problems that have been out of reach with existing microscopes. Scientists in the petrochemical and pharmaceutical industries believe that STMs may allow them to figure out--finally--why catalysts cause some chemical reactions to take place. Such information could open the door to ''designer catalysts.'' Biologists are looking to the STM to give them a new look at the structure of living organisms. While the electron microscope makes many details visible, samples are damaged by the electron beam and must be viewed in a high vacuum. With the STM, things can be examined in their natural state--even under water. Researchers at the University of Madrid have already mapped the surface of a virus. ''Ultimately,'' says Andreas H. Engel, a physicist at the University of Basel in Switzerland, ''the STM may show us the relationship between the structure and function of proteins.'' 

Although the technology of scanning tunneling microscopy is still in knee pants, some visionaries are eager to push it out of the laboratory and into practical applications. R. Fabian W. Pease, a professor of electrical engineering at Stanford University, believes that the STM idea can be modified to print the extremely fine lines on future integrated circuits. By ganging several STM probes together, he hopes to ''write'' circuit lines on multiple chips. These lines--less than a half-micron wide--are too fine to be seen by light and thus can't be printed photographically. ''Pease's work is extremely exciting and very innovative,'' says Donald J. Silversmith, a program director at the National Science Foundation, which is funding his research. NO FOCUS. Even entrepreneurs are catching STM fever. Quanscan Inc. was founded last February with the express goal of putting STM principles to work in a variety of fields. Quanscan's chief product, still under development, will adapt the STM technology to allow ultra-precise position control. Wilfred P. Charette, president of the Pasadena (Calif.) company, envisions applications in robotics, machining, and microsurgery. 

Already, the STM is spawning a whole family of lensless, essentially mechanical tools for attacking microscopic problems. Because an STM has no lens, it has no focus to adjust. So researchers are developing other types of probes to look at different sizes of microscopic features. IBM, for example, is pioneering the scanning thermal probe. It is similar to the STM, except that its probe is heated. The closer its tip comes to a surface, the faster it loses heat. And it has just the right resolution to examine submicron circuit paths on silicon microchips. ''If you look 5 to 10 years into the future,'' says Thomas H. Di Stefano, a physicist at IBM's Yorktown Heights (N. Y.) research lab, ''it's likely people will be using an arsenal of microprobes, including some we haven't found yet.'' 

All the excitement over mechanical microscopy has proponents of conventional techniques chafing to prove that lenses still have potential. At the University of Chicago, physicist Albert V. Crewe is working on an advanced lens system for an electron microscope. If it performs as expected, says Crewe, it will achieve resolutions down to 0.5 angstrom. He hopes to have a working model by yearend. 

No one, however, is betting that the resolving power of STMs and their mechanical cousins is anywhere near its limits. How much farther it will go is unknown, of course, but the performance of electron microscopes started out in the hundreds of angstroms. Even if STMs don't match that magnitude of improvement, ''the STM has pointed the direction for everybody else,'' asserts Clayton C. Williams, an IBM researcher. ''It's a momentous development.'' 

ROHRER AND BINNIG: COLLEAGUES SAID, ''YOU'LL GET THE NOBEL PRIZE.'' THEY WERE RIGHT 

THE PICTURE FROM THE STM: SILICON ATOMS IN CLOSE-UP

 Article 5 *******************   



Technology in the Workplace (A Special Report): Cover --- Frontiers of Science: Changing the Ways You'll Live and Work 

By William M. Bulkeley 
2,549 words 
10 November 1986
The Wall Street Journal 
English
(Copyright (c) 1986, Dow Jones & Co., Inc.) 

IN SOME U.S. LABORATORY, amid a clutter of vacuum chambers and thermoses of liquid nitrogen and computer screens, scientists are doing research that will win Nobel Prizes in the next decade. 

Some of that work will be esoteric computation with little immediate practical value. But much of it is likely to be applied quickly by engineers and technicians. A scientist who is now treading the frontiers of knowledge may change our lives as profoundly as the Nobel Prize-winning Bell Laboratories physicists who ushered in the computer age by inventing the transistor in 1947. 

Corporate, university and government researchers are working on technologies that will change the workplace of tomorrow. Major advances in materials science, genetic engineering, catalytic science, communications, computers and artificial intelligence are providing a foundation for changing the methods and even the kinds of work people will do in 20 years. 

Just as the transistor worked its way into numerous products and made possible things that weren't possible before, today's research will result in an array of products that people are beginning to talk about: aircraft that can ascend into space and travel from New York to Tokyo in three hours, genetically engineered plants that create their own fertilizer, synthetic diamond-film coatings that make nearly indestructible cutting tools, "designer" catalysts that don't occur naturally and that can cheaply turn natural gas into gasoline. 

Predicting exactly how scientific breakthroughs will have the greatest impact on the future is difficult. "How technology is going to be used is fuzzy in everyone's mind," says Joel I. Krugler, director of a Waltham, Mass., research laboratory of GTE Corp. "Despite all the strategic planning in the world, you're not going to anticipate the breakthroughs." Nevertheless, he says, "The general summary is fairly clear. The standard trend in my lifetime is faster, smaller and smarter." 

Driving that trend is scientists' growing ability to study the building blocks of matter. Advances in basic research are making it possible to "start with a need and then develop a material to meet it, atom by atom," says Joel P. Clark, a professor of materials science at Massachusetts Institute of Technology. 

As engineers grow familiar with atoms and biologists control genes, science is quickly being translated into technology. Close study of molecular reactions leads some to hope they can design catalysts that don't exist in nature. "New tools and information from new disciplines have created an age of molecular engineering. That has been the dream of chemists for decades," says James Cusumano, chairman of Catalytica Associates Inc., a Mountain View, Calif., research and development company. 

Other scientists are figuring out how to design computers that handle such routine human tasks as seeing, hearing and learning. They are developing a whole new science of surfaces and thin films that may explain and eventually help prevent unwanted corrosion, fractures, fatigue and separation. Such knowledge promises things like lighter, stronger car bodies and flat plastic computer screens that can be folded up and stuffed in a coat pocket. 

But imagination often outruns technology, and some of the research may take far longer than anyone thinks now. Futurists' forecasts in the 1960s often envisioned robots that moved around easily, saw where they were going and took over menial tasks. Today, however, it has become clear that human sight and movement take far more neurons than computers have transistors, and menial jobs such as cleaning an office stymie the best robots. 

On the other hand, scientists have produced expert computer systems that are better at playing chess or writing financial plans than all but a few humans. And sometimes events render technologies irrelevant -- at least for a while. Solar energy, for example, hasn't spread as expected for a number of reasons, the most important of which is that oil hasn't risen to $50 a barrel. 

Nevertheless, as basic research provides greater knowledge, engineers steadily apply it to existing problems. Such applications inevitably have broad impact, in some ways unforeseen. Some once-valuable commodities may become as irrelevant as whale oil. For example, the biological engineering that is likely to create grains that thrive in saline soils of the Third World will worsen the developed world's farm export problems. Engineering of the very pure glass needed for fiberoptic communications has already made copper cable obsolete in the long-distance telephone market. Genetic engineers' creation of "super cows" is already worsening milk surpluses. 

But, U.S. scientists say, pushing research is the only way for the U.S. and other developed nations to maintain their economies, because developing nations will inevitably learn to produce the less technically demanding materials and products. 

Little known to most consumers -- who seldom think much about the materials that go into the products they use -- is the revolution in materials science caused by burgeoning understanding of molecular structures. Those structures determine how heavy products are and how long they last -- indeed, even whether they can be produced. Instead of just mixing alloys, or discovering molecularly different materials by accident, scientists are increasingly setting out to make new materials -- and are succeeding in that task. 

The promise of some new materials is creating a "product pull" for other engineers, says Peter R. Bridenbaugh, vice president of research and development at Aluminum Co. of America. For example, he says, the potential for creating new types of fiber-reinforced ceramic mixtures that won't crack and that can survive 2,200-degree temperatures is driving aeronautical engineers to conceive of airplanes that zoom into space for quick transcontinental flights. Without such materials for turbine blades, the planes couldn't efficiently develop enough thrust. 

Ceramics scientists joke about a "new Stone Age" in which ceramics will be as important as steel. Though progress has been slow to date, their technology may yet provide the means to make a smaller, lighter car engine that runs at higher temperatures, doesn't need a radiator or lubrication and uses less gasoline. 

Ceramics tend to crack, though, and scientists are doing basic research on how to make blends of ceramics, metals and plastics that perform better than any known materials. The blending isn't easy, says John B. Wachtman Jr., director of the Center for Ceramics Research at Rutgers University. "You're on a scale where the chemical and electrical forces are comparable to the gravitational forces," he explains. "If you can't control it, the fibers all stick together." 

Still, it is much easier to create a material in a laboratory than it is to make use of it. "We know how to make very light, strong composites for auto bodies," says Richard K. Quisenberry, director of research at Du Pont Co. "We don't know how to put them together in a way that is competitive with stamping sheet steel." 

Solving such problems is vital, though. "Advanced materials, specialty polymers, ceramics, are the absolute core to advanced technologies in the future," says Kent Bowen, a professor at MIT. "Whoever wins that game has the jobs, sells the cars, the machinery, the electronics, because they can design far beyond people who have only plain vanilla materials," he says. And he worries that the Japanese are appropriating superior American science and applying it faster than American companies are. 

However, U.S. scientists themselves are chasing Japanese and Soviet scientists in another material science -- creating films of synthetic diamonds. Hydrogen and methane, the main components of natural gas, can be passed through a microwave that strips hydrogen atoms from the methane, leaving carbon atoms to form a diamond-lattice film on an object. Such films might make fantastically reliable computer parts -- or could be the basis of drill-bit coatings, long-wearing bearings, unscratchable lenses, even music speakers. 

Better understanding of atomic structures promises improved catalysts. Catalysts cause chemical reactions in other materials by breaking and reshaping molecular bonds. Under the right heat and pressure, they can turn vegetable oil to margarine and automotive pollutants to harmless byproducts. 

Mr. Cusumano of Catalytica predicts that his company will be able to devise a catalyst that can turn cheap natural gas into valuable gasoline in a simple process at the wellhead. That would eliminate the need to build high-pressure pipelines through remote areas or to liquefy natural gas and transport it on costly ships. Ordinary oil pipelines or tankers could do the job. Other scientists expect to design a catalyst that can convert natural gas into electricity in a fuel cell. 

In agriculture, genetic engineers expect to develop plants that can absorb nitrogen from air -- cutting the need for fertilizer -- and that will resist disease, lessening the need for fungicides. Grains that thrive in saline soils and dry conditions should improve food supplies in the Third World. 

Genetic engineering promises faster plant development and ever larger food surpluses. To achieve that, scientists are delving more deeply into how plants work. At chemical maker Du Pont, which spends 22% of its research and development budget on life sciences, Mr. Quisenberry, the research director, says scientists recently X-rayed the structure of the plant enzyme that is crucial to photosynthesis. "At its ultimate, understanding photosynthesis is the first step to regulating photosynthesis. You could find a way to produce a soybean that stores twice as much protein as now," he says. 

The most intensively studied of all materials is silicon, the heart of the computer. Researchers say that silicon chips can be improved for another decade. Beyond that, they look to gallium arsenide chips, which transmit electrons faster but are harder to work with. 

In one of the more remarkable recent achievements in the growing ability to understand the atomic structure of matter, Robert J. Hamers, a 28-year-old scientist at International Business Machines Corp.'s Watson Laboratories, took pictures that show the electron bonds between silicon atoms as well as the atoms themseleves. Working late at night when building vibrations were minimal, Mr. Hamers made the pictures with the help of a device called a scanning tunneling microscope, invented by IBM scientists in 1981. The device is so precise it can portray a surface from a distance of three atom widths. Someday, it may help IBM make faster computers. 

George Heilmeier, senior vice president and chief technical officer of Texas Instruments Inc., says that the company's scientists are studying different ways to design computer chips to limit the amount of wiring between transistors and increase speed. He says "in the distant future we might consider an array of devices talking only to their nearest neighbors" and relaying needed information. Such a design would require new ways of writing computer programs. 

Mr. Heilmeier says the world needs much more computer power. "Our industry produces the equivalent of one human brain per year," he says, calculating that the entire semiconductor industry produces as many bits of memory annually as there are synapses in one brain. 

Clever scientists are trying to redesign computers and to rethink software so that specialized machines can do tasks that only people now can do. Researchers are trying to develop a machine that can take dictation and type letters, or accurately translate languages. 

Many computer scientists are working on new designs for computers that will let thousands of small microprocessors be hooked together and share information, much the way brain cells have been shown to work. The Connection Machine, a massively parallel processor designed by Thinking Machines Corp., a small Cambridge, Mass., company, ties together 64,000 processors. Because each processor does a small portion of a project, it is much faster for some uses than current computers that solve problems sequentially. It is better at "seeing" than traditional computers because it looks at an entire photograph at once rather than starting in one corner and examining the photo bit by bit. It can quickly draw contour maps from airplane photographs. 

But before truly intelligent robots are possible, vastly more computer power is almost certainly needed. Hans Moravec, a senior research scientist at Carnegie-Mellon University in Pittsburgh, says, "Conventional artificial intelligence has addressed only surface-level thought. It's much harder to address the substantial amount going on subconsciously." 

Mr. Moravec is building mobile robots, and he keeps working to make them more human. He expects one will soon be able to be led through a space, gather information every few feet to build an internal map and then find its own way around the area by comparing information gathered by its sonar sensors to the internal map. He hopes the robot will evolve to the point of showing fear. "If it detects signs of danger" -- a stairwell, say -- "it starts acting in a different mode. It will start sensing a lot more, and that's the right thing for it to do. Sometimes it will be frightened by shadows." 

But, he laments, human vision systems involve 100 billion computations a second, 1,000 times more computations than today's fastest computer can do. "We need one trillion operations per second to do human intelligence," he says. "We should have that rather cheaply -- in 40 years." 

--- 

Mr. Bulkeley reports on computers and technology from The Wall Street Journal's Boston bureau.

 Article 6 *******************   



Developments to Watch 

EDITED BY SANA SIWOLOP 
963 words 
9 March 1987
Business Week 
Pg. 103 
Vol. Number 2988 
English
Copyright 1987 McGraw-Hill, Inc. 

PUSHING COMPUTERS TO RUN WITH SUPERCONDUCTING CHIPS When International Business Machines Corp. decided in 1983 to scuttle its 20-year, $300 million investment in Josephson junctions, lots of people also wrote off the idea of using those superconducting circuits to build ultrafast computers. But not IBM researcher Sadeg M. Faris, who left Big Blue to found Hypres Inc. On Feb. 19, Faris' Elmsford (N. Y.) startup passed a milestone by unveiling the world's first commercial system based on Josephson junctions, which switch on and off roughly 1,000 times faster than semiconductor transistors. 

What Hypres has built is a fairly modest computer, a $120,000 machine for analyzing electronic signals. But the Hypres PSP-1000 also contains the key to more sophisticated systems. Faris says his team solved the puzzles that stumped IBM. For example, Hypres does not immerse the chips in liquid helium to cool them to the ultralow temperatures required for superconductivity. It simply sprays liquid helium on one corner of the chip where all the logic circuits are located. The ultimate payoff, Faris predicts, will be computers that work at close to the speed of light. ''We've launched the third age of electronics, after vacuum tubes and transistors,'' boasts Faris. THE STRENGTHENING CASE AGAINST ADDING DRUGS TO ANIMAL FEED Feed low doses of antibiotics to animals and they grow up faster and healthier. But evidence that those drugs produce resistant strains of bacteria that can infect people is mounting. In 1984 researchers at the federal Centers for Disease Control in Atlanta provided the first direct evidence that meat contaminated with drug-resistant bacteria caused an outbreak of salmonella food poisoning. Now the CDC says it has collected more data in a further study, which appears in the Mar. 5 issue of the New England Journal of Medicine. 

The new findings will fuel efforts to ban drugs and some additives from animal feed, a $2 billion market in the U. S. The Food & Drug Administration first proposed such a ban in 1978, but Congress sided with the drugmakers who contended that the evidence was inconclusive. Now several lawmakers, including Senator John H. Chafee (R-R. I.), say they will reintroduce bills to control agricultural use of antibiotics. A 'TWEEZER' THAT CAN PICK UP ATOMS Imagine being able to reach into the atomic structureof substances and reshuffle the atoms to produce precisely the properties you want. Doing that routinely is still years away, but researchers at AT&T Bell Laboratories have taken the first step: They recently transplanted a clump of just 10 atoms. The ''tweezer'' the researchers used was the supersharp tip of a scanning tunneling microscope. An STM is so remarkably sensitive that it can actually trace the contours of individual atoms (BW--Nov. 3). 

While studying the surface of germanium crystals, Russell S. Becker and two co-researchers noticed that the STM's tip sometimes seemed to pick up atoms here and deposit them elsewhere. They found they could control this to a degree by adjusting the electrical potential between the needle and the surface. If the technique can be perfected, scientists may be able to change the position of single atoms. That could lead to a new generation of tailor-made semiconductor materials. THESE PLANTS SOP UP METAL POLLUTANTS Jimson weed ranks high among pesky plants. It has a foul smell and is toxic to grazing animals. But even jimson weed may have a use: It has a novel capacity to sop up metals. While most plants will die in soil contaminated with copper or cadmium, some types of jimson weed flourish. 

That's why the Energy Dept. is interested. Jimson weed and a few other plants such as monkey flower may be just the thing for greening old strip mines or producing ''crops'' of metal from industrial wastes. Other metal-loving plants might be better able to withstand acid rain, which leaches toxic aluminum from rocks. Biologists at the University of California at Los Angeles have one candidate: a shrub related to the coffee plant that concentrates up to 800 times the normal level of aluminum in its leaves. 

Researchers at Los Alamos National Laboratory in New Mexico have discovered why some plants thrive on metals. The secret is a protein that latches onto metals to prevent them from entering cells. The researchers are now trying to find the gene that codes for the production of the protein so that it can be transferred into other species. Eventually, the protein itself could be used in industrial pollution control. WHY THE EYES VOTE 'NAY' ON SODIUM-VAPOR LAMPS In some cities, they're known as ''crime lights,'' and their yellow-orange glow is familiar around parking lots and along highways. But high-pressure sodium lamps are also used indoors, in schools and offices. And many office workers and students are complaining of ills from eyestrain to nausea. 

Samuel M. Berman, director of the lighting research program at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory, found a reason for the complaints. The problem is that people don't see as well with sodium lamps as they do with incandescent lights of equal brightness. The proof lies in the size of the eye's pupil. When the pupil contracts, it means the available light is bright enough for optimal vision. ''The smaller the pupil, the better your acuity,'' Berman explains. 

But Berman found that pupil size is also affected by the type of light used, regardless of brightness. And under sodium lights, subjects' pupils were larger than with incandescent light of the same intensity. Berman believes the eye was straining because it was uncomfortable with the sodium color spectrum. The problem with sodium light? It's ''the flagrant absence of blue,'' Berman says.

 Article 7 *******************   



DANCING ON THE HEAD OF A PIN. (ATOM-SIZE BREAKTHROUGHS IN SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY) 

By Gail Bronson 
1,043 words 
6 April 1987
Forbes 
152 
English
Copyright Forbes Inc. 1987 

Dancing on the head of a pin 

At the National Nanofabrication Facility in Ithaca, N.Y., industrial and academic researchers are patiently pursuing ways to design communications and data processing components down to the size of ten atoms--a nanometer, to be precise. 

Using silicon and gallium arsenide wafers, scientists are styling transistors so tiny they today exist only in the imagination of manufacturers. However, once these devices move from the laboratory into production, the limits of technological innovation will become virtually boundless. 

Faster processing of greater quantities of information and crystal-clear satellite transmissions will be only teh initial result. Further out, scientists foresee the ability to communicate more easily with interplanetary space probes as well as to record and interpret what for now remain unintelligible signals from distant stars. And, astounding though it sounds, somewhere down the road engineers may well be building computers composed of living, biological organisms rather than electronic components. 

All of this will be made possible by the microelectronics industry's progress toward not just smaller and smaller components but also those of radically different design and conception. "Right now people are making the same sorts of transistors that they've made from the very beginning, just smaller,' says Gregory J. Galvin, Nanofabrication lab's deputy director. "Somewhere that has to change to something fundamentally different from transistors and yet somehow analogous.' 

That shift may well ensue from research using the Nanolab's prized electron beam lithography machine. This highly sophisticated system allows scientists to construct transistors containing electron pathways as narrow as 0.02 microns--roughly one ten-thousandth the diameter of a human hair--an infinitesimal size compared with current electronic component dimensions. Transistors are the tiny structures built on a thin silicon or gallium arsenide "semiconductor' base; the devices collectively channel electrons that power electronic products. As scientists are able to pack more transistors into each square millimeter of a semiconductor chip, the smarter, faster and more powerful the chip becomes. 

The ability to fabricate electronics in such extreme microscopic sizes makes the lab invaluable to visiting scientists. A handful of companies, such as AT&T's Bell Laboratories and Raytheon Inc., have bought or built similar machines. But only at the Nanolab, funded by the National Science Foundation, Cornell University and corporations, can outsiders use equipment that costs $2 million to $3 million to acquire. 

The standard industry process for making silicon wafers, optical lithography, uses a mask or stencil as a template through which patterns may be etched on semiconductor wafers photographically. The process is fast but not terribly precise when compared with the tedious but elegant inscriptions made possible by the Nanolab's lithographic machine. The latter procedure is slow going because the entire surface of the chip cannot be exposed to the narrow electron beam at once. Instead, the beam writes the pattern line by line, guided by a computer. One wafer may take an hour to etch. 

Although the Nanolab research is time-consuming, results are starting to trickle out. McDonnell Douglas researchers soon will announce a new sculpting technique to etch patterns on the faces of laser crystals no bigger than grains of sand; the resulting devices will improve laser-based communications systems. 

"Such systems will eventually replace microwaves,' predicts McDonnell Douglas senior scientist Peter Tihanyi, who foresees much demand for these devices by the military. "The transmissions from them cannot be decoded and diverted in the way that other forms of communications currently can be.' 

General Electric, too, has used the Nanolab. GE used the Ithaca facilities to design a 0.25-micron transistor for the Defense Department. Now the contractor is working at the lab to fabricate even faster 0.1-micron gallium arsenide transistors. "This lab is an important strategic planning tool for us to determine our future direction,' says Alan Swanson, manager of General Electric's materials and devices laboratory. 

Although AT&T Bell Labs has its own electron beam lithographer, it has used the Ithaca facility's as well. At the Nanolab, in October 1985, AT&T researchers created what at the time was the world's fastest semiconductor device. It was capable of switching an electronic signal on and off in 5.8 trillionths of a second-- important in the designing of everfaster computers. 

Additional Nanolab research runs the gamut from biology to condensed particle physics. University of Rochester researchers are exploring how blood cells grow and mature in the body's bone marrow and then move into general circulation. The apertures from the marrow into the blood stream are of micron size. Without tiny lab-built models from the Nanolab, it would be impossible to conduct various studies that may provide clues to how cancer and other diseases affect blood cells. 

Plant research by Cornell University biologists could lead to savings of anywhere from $20 million to $250 million in bean crop damage annually. By exposing bean rust fungus to a microscopic wafer that mimics a leaf's surface, the scientists have been able to determine that the fungus navigates, using a sophisticated sense of touch. From that insight the biologists hope to develop ways of halting the blight's spread (see picture on opposite page). 

Physicists at the University of Pennsylvania are using the lab to study the basic properties of exotic materials that are not found in nature. Electron-beam-designed models allow scientists to "trick' electrons into performing on a submicron scale in ways impossible to engineer at an atomic level. 

Companies in Japan, the U.K. and the Netherlands produce electron beam lithography machines, as does Perkin-Elmer in the U.S. But the devices carry seven-digit price tags, and when the use of the same equipment at a nominal cost is readily available at the Nanolab, why buy one? For now, at least, it looks as if the Nanolab will continue to have all the work it can handle. 

Photo: Bean rust fungus 

Understanding crop diseases could save millions. 

Photo: Nanofabrication lab engineer Richard Tiberio and (left) magnification of chip he is holding 

The smaller, the smarter. 

ILLUSTRATION: photograph - chart CAPTION: Diagram of a transistor.

 Article 8 *******************   


IBM SETS OFF THE STAMPEDE TO SUPERCONDUCTING. (J. GEORG BEDNORZ AND K. ALEX MULLER) (BUSINESS PEOPLE OF THE YEAR) BIOGRAPHY 

By Anthony Ramirez 
1,799 words 
4 January 1988
Fortune 
39 
English
Copyright Time Inc. 1988 

TWO PHYSICISTS, working in a modest Swiss hilltop laboratory in 1986, made a breakthrough that may change the world. They discovered how to give certain substances properties that could make possible economical transmission of electricity over vast distances with virtually no loss of current. In time that discovery could lead to magnetic trains that reach 300 miles an hour, ultrafast computers, far more efficient power transmission, and clean, plentiful energy from nuclear fusion. With unusual swiftness, the two International Business Machines researchers won the ultimate recognition of their work: The Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences awarded J. Georg Bednorz and K. Alex Muller the 1987 Nobel Prize in physics. 

Superconductivity had long been scientifically fascinating but commercially barren because it worked only at temperatures so low that supercold and superexpensive liquid helium had to be used as the refrigerant. That was until Bednorz and Muller fashioned an exotic ceramic that worked as a superconductor at warmer temperatures than any material had before. The achievement set off a race that dominated the scientific news of 1987 and culminated in the discovery by U.S. researchers of a material that may conduct electricity almost perfectly at a mere -54 degrees F. Since then others have reported evidence that superconductivity could be possible at room temperature and even well above it. 

In a reflective moment, Muller says that success in science, a quintessentially rational endeavor, requires something of the irrational -- ''the internal fire,'' in his phrase. That fire burns bright in both Bednorz and Muller, who make a couple as odd as the substance they created. Bednorz, 37, a German school principal's son, is a cheery laboratory whiz who pads around in white socks and sandals, an ash-laden cigarette dangling from his lips. Muller, 60, a Swiss whose grandfather founded Grison, a chocolate maker, is the picture of a tweedy, absent-minded professor, an intense intellectual who reads Jung, listens to Bach, and rarely appears in the lab, preferring to do much of his work at home. Apart from physics, Bednorz and Muller seem to have just one thing in common: Both like to fix old cars. 

Together, doer and thinker, they hit upon a paradoxical approach to making superconductors: fashioning them out of insulators, which by definition conduct little or no electricity. Strong atomic forces bind their electrons tightly to their nuclei, impeding the flow of current. Good conductors have electrons that don't bind so tightly, enhancing the flow of current. By tinkering with its molecular structure, Bednorz and Muller were able to use an insulator's strong atomic forces to pull electrons from atom to atom, thus coaxing the insulator into superconductivity. Fired as a ceramic, Bednorz and Muller's creation resembles the materials used in some kinds of cookware. 

As relative novices in a field that seemed near a dead end, Bednorz and Muller had nothing to lose by trying the unconventional. The staff of their IBM laboratory in Ruschlikon, near Zurich, includes at least five other scientists with doctoral degrees or other research experience in superconductivity. Among them: Gerd Binnig and Heinrich Rohrer, winners of the 1986 Nobel Prize in physics, who built the scanning tunneling microscope that can picture objects as tiny as individual atoms. Bednorz sees a similarity between his and Muller's work and Binnig and Rohrer's in microscopy, where they too were novices. Says Bednorz: ''They had a certain degree of ignorance. They simply ignored the judgment of people who had worked on the problem before and started with their own ideas, which were not very obvious.'' 

SINCE 1911 scientists have known that electrical conductivity increases as temperature decreases. At ordinary temperatures, even in a good conductor like copper, electrons waste some of their energy through scattering. As the temperature drops, electrons scatter less and conductivity rises. At -459.7 degrees F., absolute zero, electrons stop scattering completely. Just before that theoretical point, electrons in a properly cooled superconductor can glide effortlessly, without resistance. 

Cooling substances to extremely low temperatures is cumbersome and expensive. So for decades scien tists sought superconductors that would work at temperatures significantly above absolute zero. By the 1970s the quest had largely been abandoned. But not in the mind of Alex Muller, who was intrigued by the idea that nonconductors -- insulators -- might somehow superconduct. 

To find the right compound, Muller needed someone with greater gifts in the laboratory than his. A specialist in crystals that carry an electric charge, Muller picked Bednorz, who had shown promise as a crystal grower while still an undergraduate in IBM Zurich's summer program. He was a newly minted Ph.D. from the nearby Swiss Federal Institute of Technology (known by its German initials, ETH). Following Muller's theories, Bednorz used a simple mortar, pestle, and oven to mix and temper some 60 different compounds. He fired them as ceramics because they would be quicker and easier to work with than unstable, hard-to-prepare crystals. 

By autumn 1985, Muller and Bednorz were fresh out of leads. Then Bednorz, looking through the literature in the IBM library, came across an article by researchers at the University of Caen in France. Describing their search for new catalysts to speed up chemical reactions, the Caen scientists mentioned a compound of copper and oxygen, with traces of barium and lanthanum. They hadn't tested it for superconductivity. Says Bednorz with a large grin: ''I realized that this material would fulfill all our requirements.'' 

In January 1986, working alone in his ground-floor lab, Bednorz chilled the copper oxide the French had described and found that resistance dropped sharply at -440 degrees F. Bednorz was elated. Testing the substance again the following morning, he found the resistance drop was no fluke. Over the next few months Bednorz meticulously varied the barium concentration and the oven temperature, reducing the substance's resistance to zero and raising the superconducting temperature to a record-breaking -406 degrees F. 

But a problem remained. Two tests are required to establish superconductivity: zero resistance and the so-called Meissner effect, the repulsion of a magnetic field much as one magnet repels another. IBM's magnetometer for measuring the Meissner effect was still on order. Wanting to publish but uneasy about lacking the second test, Bednorz and Muller submitted a cautious paper in April 1986 to a German journal, Zeitschrift fur Physik. They would at least be on record. Only the Japanese snapped to attention. In December a group at the University of Tokyo published exhaustive Meissner effect data on the Bednorz-Muller compound, confirming the breakthrough. 

AN INTERNATIONAL RACE was on to improve superconductors, perhaps to the point where they would need no coolant at all. A University of Houston group raised the superconducting temperature of a compound similar to Bednorz and Muller's to -54 degrees F., high enough to use cheap liquid nitrogen as a refrigerant, and another IBM lab in Yorktown Heights, New York, pushed current- carrying capacity up to commercially useful levels. While some difficulties have yet to be resolved -- mainly the brittleness of the material -- scientists agree that Bednorz and Muller's basic compound could well make possible the most significant advance in electronics, and perhaps other industries, since scientists at AT&T's Bell Laboratories invented the transistor in 1947. 

The intellectual development of the man who set off the superconductivity revolution began with a ham radio. Fascinated by the radio's electronics, the teenage Muller wanted to become an electrical engineer. A high school teacher encouraged him to go into physics, where he could also study electricity. (The radio was confiscated just before the end of World War II as a suspected spy apparatus.) 

After getting his Ph.D. in physics from ETH, Muller went to work in 1959 as a project manager at the Geneva lab of the Battelle Memorial Institute, a U.S. research foundation. He showed a knack for management. ''You were like a little king, you were,'' Muller says wryly. In 1963 he joined IBM's Zurich lab, where he again showed managing talent, helping to hire Binnig, for example, after a single 15-minute conversation. 

BUT MULLER isn't all brains and business. He has been an avid skier since age 7 and an amateur photographer since he was 11. Married for 32 years, he and his wife, Ingeborg, the sister of an ETH colleague, have two grown children, a dentist son and a daughter who is a kindergarten teacher. Muller loves to drive with dispatch along the winding mountain roads between his suburban Zurich home and the IBM lab. When he has time, he plays grease monkey underneath his 1979 Daimler Double-Six and a 1975 Plymouth Valiant, both burgundy colored. 

Bednorz also loves to fix cars, another expression of what Muller calls ''his gift for handling materials.'' Working on his doctorate at ETH, Bednorz astonished his colleagues with his ability to improvise equipment from spare parts in the lab, according to Herman Wuest, a friend and fellow ETH researcher. He showed the same facility with a convertible 1953 Fiat Topolino, although friends joke that it is probably the world's most expensive car for all the work and new parts he has put into it. 

Above all, his colleagues were taken with Bednorz's sunny and self-effacing personality, says Hanns Arend, the materials scientist who supervised Bednorz's Ph.D. ''The people who lust for the Nobel Prize, they don't get it,'' says Arend. ''They are horrible people. They just want to climb up on the shoulders of their colleagues. Bednorz was never like that.'' His pleasures remain simple, says Wuest. Bednorz loves to cook Chinese and German food for his friends. He and his girlfriend, a manager at a Swiss watch company, like to go hiking in the Alps, where they drink wine and cook sausages. 

Bednorz and Muller have been elated and dismayed by the hoopla surrounding their Nobel Prize. Bednorz, especially, was stalked by the aggressive German press for photographs and interviews. Muller was lucky enough to be out of town during the initial hubbub. Says Bertram Batlogg, Muller's friend and a top physicist at Bell Labs: ''Alex is weighed down by the burden of winning the Nobel Prize. More people listen to his words and take them as God-given.'' Explains Muller: ''I want to avoid people believing that now that you have understood superconductivity, you know everything about everything.'' 

Still, says Muller, ''I am astonished what our creation has wrought.'' Eventually the fruits of his and Bednorz's work could astonish the world. 

ILLUSTRATION: portrait CAPTION: K. Alex Muller and J. Georg Bednorz.
