 Article 70 *******************    



Science & Technology: Materials 

THE BIG SQUEEZE IN THE LAB How extreme pressure is creating exotic materials 

By Ruth Coxeter, with Peter Coy, in New York 
1,067 words 
13 February 1995
Business Week 
76 
Number 3411 
English
(Copyright 1995 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

In 1987, C.W. (Paul) Chu of the University of Houston was racing scientists from Moscow to Long Island for a breakthrough in high-temperature superconductivity. On a hunch, he prepared a bit of copper, barium, lanthanum, and oxygen smaller than a BB. Then he worked high-pressure alchemy, squeezing it under pressure 18,000 times as great as that at sea level. 

Bingo: When electrodes were plugged in to the blend, it conducted electricity with zero resistance. Chu then guessed he could achieve the same effect at ordinary pressure by substituting yttrium for lanthanum. The substitution worked--and earned Chu worldwide fame for a material that superconducted at a record high temperature: at -180C. 

Chu's work highlighted how extraordinarily high pressures can strong-arm elements into yielding their secrets. The high-pressure manufacture of synthetic diamond (by squeezing carbon) is already a near-billion-dollar industry. Today, researchers are using pressures exceeding those at the center of the earth to break and re-form chemical bonds in everything from hydrogen to beach sand. ``Even by a conservative estimate, we could triple the range of materials we now know just by squeezing them,'' says Robert M. Hazen, a research scientist with the Carnegie Institution's Geophysical Laboratory and author of a 1993 book on the topic, The New Alchemists. 

If gases can be compressed into stable metallic crystals they might superconduct even at room temperatures. Superhard glass made from silicon dioxide--or quartz--could serve as windshields for rockets. A metallic form of hydrogen could be an incredibly dense form of stored chemical energy. ``It would be something like 30 times more efficient than any existing rocket fuel,'' Hazen says. 

The laboratory equipment required to achieve these awesome pressures, a diamond anvil, is no more than 8 inches tall and can be screwed tight by hand. It works by amplifying arm power through gears and concentrating all of it into an extremely small area. That's done by placing a tiny sample between the tips of two cut diamonds. A plate with a hole in it corrals the sample. 

Research on such a small scale has big drawbacks. The products are too tiny--about a millionth of an ounce--to be useful for anything except research. Many materials revert to normal once the pressure is off. And high-pressure science can be hazardous. Having witnessed one explosion, David Mao and Russell Hemley of the Carnegie Institution now operate experiments from behind a steel wall. 

With real money probably years off, most work on exotic high-pressure materials is going on in universities, not companies. Cornell, Harvard, and the University of California at Berkeley have substantial programs. Three years ago, the National Science Foundation established a Center for High-Pressure Research, pooling efforts at the Carnegie Institution, Princeton, and the State University of New York at Stony Brook. HARD LUCK. High-pressure research got a jolt last May when 11 Russian and French researchers claimed in the journal Physics Letters that they had created a material harder than diamond from ``buckyballs,'' or carbon-60. But similar claims have arisen before, and some scientists argue that the samples were too small to be reliably tested. 

Squeezing materials does more than harden them. Compressing gases such as hydrogen liberates their electrons, turning them into excellent electrical conductors. Helium, ordinarily inert, will bond with nitrogen when sufficiently compressed. Oxygen is ordinarily colorless--but under extreme pressure it forms crystals with facets of red, yellow, and blue. UCLA professor of physical chemistry Malcolm F. Nicol speculates that sulfur crystals could store information, with different colors of light serving to write, erase, and read data. 

The quest for a superconducting metallic hydrogen is taking scientists into realms of pressure never experienced on earth--and into heated rivalries as well. Researchers at Harvard University and Carnegie Institution disagree over what happens to hydrogen at 1.5 million atmospheres, or megabars, at which point its molecules line up like watermelons. Carnegie brags it was the first to reach 2.5 megabars. No one has surpassed 3 megabars, where scientists speculate hydrogen becomes fully metallic and superconducting. The big question: Will metallic hydrogen remain metallic once the pressure is off? MICROPOPS. Commercialization is just one goal. High-pressure researchers also seek to understand the composition of stars and planets and the sources of earthquakes. UCLA's Nicol has even recreated conditions of the early earth to form chains of hydrogen cyanide, which he believes are precursors to DNA. One favorite for research is ikaite, a watery calcium compound. Its behavior under high temperature and pressure sheds light on how veins of gold, silver, and other ores are formed. Raymond Jeanloz, a physicist at UC Berkeley, and graduate student Charles Meade proposed a new seismic theory after pressurizing serpentine, a greenish, waxy mineral. A microphone attached to a diamond anvil picked up a popping sound--perhaps a miniature version of a quake that occurs when water is forced out of serpentine deep underground. 

Doubts about the commercial value of exotic high-pressure materials have kept away mainstream producers of synthetic diamonds, such as General Electric, Sumitomo, and De Beers. ``We're a materials supplier. Just creating high-pressure phases doesn't make you any money,'' says William F. Banholzer, manager of engineering for GE Superabrasives in Worthington, Ohio. But in labs like Paul Chu's, the big squeeze is paying off. Chu recently found a superconductor that he hopes could be produced in industrial quantities by depositing the materials in vapor form. That's the kind of advance that keeps the pressure on. 

HIGH-PRESSURE ALCHEMY 

I t's not just making diamonds from graphite: All kinds of ordinary materials take on startling characteristics when subjected to extremely high pressure. 

MATERIAL POTENTIAL USES HYDROGEN, OXYGEN Fuel cells, superconductors CARBON-60, OR BUCKYBALLS Coatings for computer disks, abrasives SILICON DIOXIDE Superhard glass, lightweight car components IKAITE Indicator of how mineral deposits form HELIUM NITRIDE Aid to research on inert gases DATA: CARNEGIE INSTITUTION, BUSINESS WEEK

Developments to Watch 

LURING FACTORIES TO LANDFILLS 

EDITED BY NEIL GROSS 
203 words 
6 March 1995
Business Week 
58 
Number 3414 
English
(Copyright 1995 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

NOBODY WANTS A GARBAGE dump in their backyard. But when it comes to luring industrial parks, communities compete aggressively with tax breaks. Playing on that, scientists at Stevens Institute of Technology are tackling the problem of solid waste by turning landfills into a new breed of industrial park. Manufacturers who locate there would get free energy and free raw materials in exchange for converting, say, discarded jars into glass fibers. These would be used to reinforce plastic composites made from scrap shampoo and soda bottles. 

The idea could solve a basic economic problem: If resources mined from a landfill have to be transported to remote factories and reprocessed using commercial power, ``then recycling just can't compete,'' says George P. Korfiatis, director of Stevens Institute's Environmental Engineering Center. Better to do it on the spot, using methane from decaying garbage for power. Stevens will build a $2.3 million pilot recycling center next to a landfill in New Jersey. It will also use molecular engineering to create new polymers that make it easier to manage the total life cycle of plastic products--currently more than one-third of all solid waste.

Developments to Watch 

NANOTECH TOOLS FOR TOMORROW'S TEENY ROBOTS 

EDITED BY OTIS PORT 
212 words 
1 May 1995
Business Week 
128 
Number 3422 
English
(Copyright 1995 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

A DECADE AFTER BIG BLUE'S 1981 invention of the scanning tunneling microscope, or STM, IBM researchers demonstrated that an STM's pointy tip can do more than just trace the contours of individual atoms. The microscope can also pick up and move separate atoms. And now, scientists at Cornell University have made an ultrasmall STM that could be the forerunner of minuscule machine tools for the nanotechnology era. 

Nanotech visionaries predict that tiny robots and computer circuits will one day be built atom by atom. Such molecular-scale robots could probe and repair DNA or hunt down cancer cells in the bloodstream. But today's STMs aren't practical production tools because the business end of the system, including the little motors that drive the tip, is about the size of your thumb. 

To make drastically smaller STMs--smaller than pinheads--Cornell's Noel C. MacDonald, a professor of electrical engineering, has harnessed the technology used in manufacturing semiconductors and built transistor-size silicon motors. So future silicon chips could be packed with batteries of STMs, turning chips into nanotech factories. Cornell is already working with TMS Technologies Inc. in Ithaca, N.Y., to license the technology. 

Document bw00000020011024dr51001b2


More Like This

 Article 73 *******************    



Developments to Watch 

TRUTH THAT'S STRANGER THAN FRICTION 

EDITED BY OTIS PORT 
211 words 
1 May 1995
Business Week 
128 
Number 3422 
English
(Copyright 1995 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

RESEARCHERS AT OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY HAVE MEAsured the friction of one atom rubbing against another--and it's not what textbooks say it should be. At the atomic level, explains Bharat Bhushan, head of OSU's microtribology work, the classical theory of friction doesn't hold up. So for tomorrow's nanotechnology products, which will get stitched together atom by atom, a new theory of friction will be necessary. A paper co-authored by Bhushan in the Apr. 13 Nature outlines the implications--not all of which are in the distant future. 

Within five years, Bhushan believes the new theory of friction could yield far smaller computer hard drives. His team has tested supersmooth aluminum surfaces, similar to the platters used in hard drives, that were lubricated with a film only one molecule thick. The film resisted abrasion 80 times better than predicted. Thus, a hard drive's read/write head could be reduced to a fraction of its current size and moved closer to the disk. This would decrease the size of the area needed to store bits of data, increasing capacity by as much as 400 times. 

Photograph: IT'S A DRAG: Graphite surface after abrasion 


Document bw00000020011024dr51001b3


More Like This

 Article 74 *******************    



"Show me a piece."(investing in technological advances; 1995 Money Guide: Technology) 

Philip Ross 
1,066 words 
19 June 1995
Forbes 
230 
Vol. 155, No. 13, ISSN: 0015-6914 
English
COPYRIGHT 1995 Forbes Inc. 

REMEMBER THE FUSS over high-temperature superconductors? They won a 1987 Nobel Prize for two IBM scientists. Then the talk started about what this discovery meant. Superconductors were going to change our lives by floating trains on magnetic fields and ferrying excess power from Canada to Florida without losing a watt to resistance. In early 1987 a weekly business magazine gushed about a revolution in the making, then cautioned that it "will probably be 1990 before full-fledged products show up." 

The turn of the decade came and went without any full-fledged superconductor products showing up. No semi-fledged ones, either. Only now are the first niche products coming on stream. Talk of multibillion-dollar markets has subsided. 

Investors who jump at the latest and most exciting scientific discoveries should listen to Rustum Roy, the founding director of Pennsylvania State University's Materials Research Laboratory. Roy, a 70-year-old native of India, worked on superconductors himself but remained skeptical about their prospects. "I have a simple test: Show me a piece," he says. 

Roy's second rule of thumb is publicity. "If it's really [commercially] interesting, would you talk about it?" he asks. "[Synthetic] diamonds were interesting in the 1950s, and General Electric kept the secret for years." 

Diamonds, in fact, offer a good example of how technological advance and investment success do not always go hand in hand. Synthetic diamonds cannot be brushed aside as scientifically intriguing but commercially useless, like such inventions as fullerenes (atomic cages made of carbon). Far from it: Diamonds, says Roy, are stupendously useful--"tops in every property-- hardness, transparency, conductivity of electricity and of heat, refraction of light." 

In 1993 Roy and his Penn State colleagues found a way to make industrial-grade diamond crystals at low pressure, using a microwave oven, powdered carbon and metal, and hydrogen gas. Their energy-saving process makes it potentially the biggest advance since GE's 1950s-era method, which requires very large presses. 

Roy got his share of gee-whiz headlines in the science sections. But he isn't trying to raise venture capital. 

The reason? Commercially, low-pressure diamonds have been a huge disappointment. "We cannot sell them," says Roy. The market for the most attainable stuff--diamond grit for industrial grinding--has collapsed. It seems that the Australians and the Russians discovered new diamond fields, and the Chinese invested heavily in the old, high-pressure synthetic method (whose labor- intensive nature makes it economical for them but not for us). As a result, the cheapest diamond grit has dropped from a dollar per carat in the mid-1980s to a measly 12 cents a carat now. 

As recently as 1990, many in the industry predicted the market for artificial diamonds would reach $5 billion to $10 billion by 2005. Roy says his own skeptical estimate was $1 billion--and now, five years later, he expects it to be lower still. "I was disappointed, too," he says. "But I was wrong in a smaller way than most others. I held my romantic impulses in check." 

Romantic impulses can take you a long way on Wall Street. Stanford Ovshinsky, founder of Energy Conversion Devices of Troy, Mich., has made a career of hyping new materials, especially amorphous silicon--which his company really does make into solar cells. When superconductivity fever hit in early 1987, Ovshinsky's company jumped on the bandwagon. "ECD has now demonstrated the ability to synthesize materials that can be essentially 100% superconducting," the company trumpeted. 

The stock jumped 11/4 points when that press release came out. Two months later, the company claimed it had achieved superconductivity at the stupendously high temperature of 9 degrees Fahrenheit, kicking up the stock another point. 

Where are the working pieces? The sales? Ovshinsky's company has discontinued research into high-temperature superconductors. 

Energy inventions never cease to dazzle gullible investors. In 

1989 two professors announced a process for cold fusion that promised to wring virtually limitless energy from seawater. Within three weeks, a key ingredient of the new process, palladium, had jumped 27%. Today there's still no demand for palladium from commercial fusion reactors. 

In 1988 Nucell, a subsidiary of Peripheral Systems, announced a technical development that would lead to miniature fission reactors--household power plants. That announcement helped push Peripheral's stock from 2 to 6. Where is desktop fission today? Nowhere. The stock was delisted three years ago. 

In 1980 the old Gulf & Western Co. made headlines with a new battery that G&W said would make the electric car economical. G&W's stock jumped 1 3/4 over the next 11 weeks. Fifteen years later, how many electric cars do you see on the streets? 

Mining a similar vein, Valence Technology pushed up its stock price from 8 in the spring of 1992 to 19 a year later by claiming that its lithium/polymer battery would cost less than the nickel/cadmium standard and outperform it in several ways (FORBES, Mar. 1, 1993). The battery is still in development. Valence's stock: a recent 2 1/2. 

Roy says other way-out science continues to get more respect than it deserves. He cites as an example biomimetics, which aims to reproduce the extraordinary properties of natural materials-- such as the spider's silk or the elephant's trunk--by copying their structures. Another example is nanotechnology, which in its most extreme form imagines bacteria-size "assemblers" that would build any product you desire atom by atom, at a cost no higher than that of the atoms themselves. Researchers are busy hustling venture capital to explore these fascinating fields. The next step would be a public stock offering to the kind of investors who liked 

Valence. Avoid investing, says Roy, until the big talkers have a working model. 

Don't get us wrong. Big money has been and will be made on Wall Street from technology. But it tends not to be the sort of technology that wins oohs and aahs from science journalists. Wal- Mart made a fortune by skillfully marrying inventory management systems to cash registers. No Nobel Prize there. 

Put it this way. If someone invents a slightly improved mousetrap, consider investing in it. If he invents an antigravity machine, stay away. 

illustration photograph

 Article 75 *******************    



Developments to Watch 

NANO-NEEDLES TO TAILOR MOLECULES 

EDITED BY OTIS PORT 
220 words 
3 July 1995
Business Week 
81 
Number 3431 
English
(Copyright 1995 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

IMAGINE DIPPING AN ULTRAFINE QUILL PEN INTO PLATINUM ink, then using it to transform the chemical structure of individual molecules. In essence, that is what chemists at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory Materials Sciences Div. have just done. Their feat signals the dawn of ``nanocatalysis''--a technology that could allow chemical reactions to be triggered molecule by molecule. 

Until now, most research in nanotechnology has centered on tracing the atomic surface of materials, using a new breed of microscope with a supersharp needle that can pick up the contours of atoms like a phonograph's stylus following the grooves in a record. The instrument used by the Lawrence Berkeley scientists is called an atomic-force microscope. It's like other such microscopes, except for one thing: Its point is coated with platinum, a common catalytic agent. 

When the researchers touched the needle to an azide molecule, the platinum catalyzed a reaction, turning the azide into an amine molecule. Says Peter Schultz, the senior staff chemist who's leading the research: ``Now we can do chemistry on surfaces instead of just looking at them.'' Developing nanochemistry to work with other catalysts and molecules, says Schultz, could point the way to custom-made materials with unique industrial applications, including new semiconductor materials. 

Document bw00000020011024dr730020x

 Article 76 *******************    



Developments to Watch 

NEWS FROM THE WHITE-NOSE FRONT 

EDITED BY NEIL GROSS 
149 words 
14 August 1995
Business Week 
79 
Number 3437 
English
(Copyright 1995 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

FOR SUN LOVERS CURSED with fair and sensitive skin, there's good news: a new sunblocking lotion that relies solely on the reflective properties of titanium dioxide (TiO2). It's already widely used in sunscreens. But the molecules tend to clump together, weakening their ability to deflect rays. To compensate, sunscreen makers add light-absorbing chemicals such as PABA, which can irritate skin and eyes. 

By refining TiO2, researchers at Nanophase Technologies in Burr Ridge, Ill., say they have eliminated the need for other chemicals. They vaporize the metal into tiny, ultrapure spheres that distribute themselves more evenly in lotion than the bulkier TiO2 molecules do. Nanophase will market its sunblock, called Nanoguard, later this summer. Meanwhile, it's developing new TiO2 products to protect paper, ceramics, and household fabrics, such as carpeting, from damaging rays.

 Article 77 *******************    



Science & Technology: CYBERSPACE 

WELCOME TO THE WORLD WIDE LAB By providing instant access to data, the Web is changing how research is done and shared 

By Joan O'C. Hamilton in San Francisco, with Heidi Dawley in London 
1,617 words 
30 October 1995
Business Week 
66 
Number 3448 
English
(Copyright 1995 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

Dr. Ingrid E. Akerblom's research at Incyte Pharmaceuticals Inc. could be crucial to developing a new generation of superior medicines. But this biologist never peers into a microscope, squints at a lab rat, or draws blood from a wincing patient. 

Rather, Akerblom sits at a computer all day, analyzing genetic information that will be part of a giant database program linked to information sources around the globe. Her job is to make those data sing. With a few mouse clicks, she can zip from her Palo Alto (Calif.) headquarters to Switzerland, Washington, and St. Louis, amassing clues about disease pathology, for example, that just a few years ago would have taken months to harvest from traditional biology experiments and days to cross-check in the bowels of a library. 

Although she is glued to a powerful workstation, Akerblom bears little resemblance to the ``digital scientists'' who drive the world's fastest supercomputers to model cosmic string theory and other exotica. While these pioneers rule the theoretical reaches, Akerblom is part of a more grassroots phenomenon: scientists using the ever expanding graphical and software links of the World Wide Web as a new way to do science. 

The potential of the Web, created by physicists less than five years ago to speed navigation of the global Internet, is tantalizing scientists of nearly every persuasion and changing the way they run experiments, compare data, and publish papers. It's also leveling scientific hierarchies by giving researchers around the planet access to the most advanced scientific tools and databases. The Net may not be ready for prime-time commerce, as recent security breaches suggest. But it is becoming a surprisingly powerful platform for scientific discovery. Says San Diego Supercomputer Center computational ecologist Richard Marciano: ``The scope and magnitude of problems being tackled is at a level never reached before.'' 

Incyte's crown jewel is Lifeseq--a proprietary database holding tens of thousands of gene sequences extracted from tissue libraries. Trouble is, each sequence is just a long stream of raw genetic code--alphabet soup, to the naked eye. Incyte and its pharmaceutical partners, including Upjohn, Novo Nordisk, Pfizer, and Hoechst, seek answers to specific questions, such as which genes are turned on in cancer, or what effect a chemical has on genes linked to arthritis. NATURE'S CODE. To create signposts, Akerblom and other Incyte researchers ``annotate'' gene data as they arrive and use HTML (hypertext markup language)--the link-building programming language of the Web. By using the Netscape Communications Corp. Web browser, scientists can explore connections, say, between lists of genes functioning in healthy vs. diseased tissue. And they can step seamlessly from Incyte's private domain to public databases. The possibilities that open up, Akerblom says, ``have blown my scientific mind.'' 

The exponential increase in data from the Human Genome Project is one force driving biology to the forefront of Web-based science. DNA is as close as nature gets to pure software code and is readily adapted to computer analysis. As a result, experimental biology has evolved from in vitro and in vivo to ``in silico.'' GenBank, a public, Bethesda (Md.)-based gene repository, lets Web-wise scientists compare new sequence data against a huge database. The number of ``intellectual queries'' from the Web has soared from zero in 1992 to 12,500 a day now. Earlier this year, researchers using this query approach to study the so-called ataxia gene were thrilled to uncover a similar gene in yeast--one that has been studied extensively. That provided hints to the human gene's function. In a matter of seconds, says GenBank Director David Lipman, the Web lets scientists ``make discoveries that nobody who created the databases ever dreamed of.'' 

Physicists, who have strong stomachs for the arcane codes of the Internet, were among the first to cruise databases and public servers. They set up the first ``pre-print servers'' to make scientific papers available long before they are physically published--a trend that's now spreading to other disciplines. And physicist Philip Berners-Lee invented the Web and HTML. 

At first, researchers at the Stanford Linear Accelerator Center (SLAC) in Menlo Park, Calif., and elsewhere used Web pages for ``housekeeping'' chores, such as maintaining phone directories. Then some began putting up their workbooks, giving other high-energy physicists almost real-time access to notes on cutting-edge experiments. BAY WATCH. When scientists in other fields saw how easy it was to navigate databases using HTML, lightbulbs went on. At the San Diego Supercomputer Center, for example, ecologist Marciano is dusting off 20 years of Landsat data sitting in tape reels and getting them Web-ready so researchers grappling with global warming, land-use patterns, or water-storage problems will be able to analyze measurements over time. 

Another effort, the ``San Diego Bay Project,'' is linking disparate databases full of measurements on sediment chemistry, bird species, and vegetation growth. Using new software tools such as Virtual Reality Modeling Language, researchers will be able to move through 3-D representations of these data. 

Such powers have attracted major scientific journals, virtually all of which have gone online or are thinking about it. Neither the review boards nor most readers are in a hurry to weaken the peer review process. But scientists on the Web get used to instant communication. 

One effect may be to breathe new life into disciplines such as archaeology. By its very nature, explains Michael P. Heyworth, deputy director of the Council for British Archaeology, research in this field is destructive: Sites can only be dug up once. By next August, however, the council hopes to have a new journal on the Web that will display measurements and images and allow readers to review sites from the angle of their choice. Heyworth hopes readers of the Web journal will be able to simulate a walk through, say, Stonehenge on a midsummer's day, showing the sun and shadows from the astronomically correct angles. 

And since cyberspace is not bound by physical page lengths, scientists can put up their complete data so colleagues can perform their own analyses. These data are neither better nor worse in quality than they have ever been. They're simply easier to obtain. Says organic chemist Henry Rzepa of London's Imperial College of Science, Technology & Medicine: ``We're trying to strip off the grime'' of misinterpretation that can occur when data are marshalled to support a theory. Collaboration also takes on new resonance. Rzepa recently hosted a chemistry ``conference'' exclusively on the Web, including presentations, papers, and discussions--at almost no cost. 

Such efficiencies are critical in a time of shrinking budgets. More and more conferences include an electronic repository for the pounds of written materials and papers scientists used to collect and lug around. And if a meeting is hot, says Marciano, ``discussion lives on long after the meeting.'' 

Even bigger cost-savers may lie ahead. Last March, a group of fusion scientists joined a novel experiment at Massachusetts Institute of Technology. They sat well beyond the requisite 70-foot safety distance from MIT's Tokamak magnetic-chamber plasma device--3,000 miles beyond it, in fact, since they operated the Tokamak remotely from California. PLASMA DONATION. For 12 hours, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory scientists controlled plasma bursts in the $30 million Tokamak over the Internet, while watching the results and swapping ideas through live audio and video feeds. Scientists are studying Web-based interfaces for similar experiments. And since most major labs are facing dire budget cuts, such collaborations will likely be used to stretch scarce funding, says Ian Hutchinson, director of the Alcator Fusion Project at MIT's Plasma Fusion Center. In theory, nothing would prevent sites around the country from simultaneously viewing a fusion experiment and taking turns ``driving'' it. Scientists could share other resources, such as accelerators and radio telescopes. 

Scientists face some of the same hurdles as business executives who embrace the Web, such as slow access times and vulnerability to corruption or sabotage. Maintaining databases is also expensive, so the best ones are often overloaded. 

What's more, the sociology of science is changing. ``Access is no longer a function of where you are or what your facilities are,'' says Bebo White, the ``Webmaster'' at Stanford's SLAC. By simply clicking to the home page of Pedro M. Coutinho, a chemical engineering graduate student at Iowa State University, anyone can browse a treasure trove of biotech resources and find pointers to powerful gene-analysis programs. 

It's not surprising that such a long list of issues would arise from such a fundamental development. From astronomy to zoology, scientists are putting their imaginations to work on the Web--and in the process, blowing their minds. 

Download a version of this story with Web links from Business Week Online on America Online 

How Scientists Are Working the Web 

REMOTE CONTROL Scientists could one day ``drive'' IBM'S scanning tunneling microscopes remotely to build atomic landscapes. But technicians would still load samples by hand 

NEW VIEWS Projects in chemistry, physics, and medicine exploit the Web's dazzling 3-D graphics. The National Library of Medicine sponsored digital training videos linked to these icons 

COMMUNICATIONS Organic chemist Henry Rzepa and Britain's Royal Society of Chemistry collaborate in electronic publishing experiments to bring 3-D renderings of molecules to life 

Photograph: GENE-CRUNCHING: Web links give Akerblom data from St. Louis to Switzerland ROBERT HOLMGREN

INNOVATIONS 

EDITED BY OTIS PORT

-- Just days apart, two teams of biotech researchers announced they had found the gene that regulates flowering. Scientists at the Salk Institute in La Jolla, Calif., and the University of California at San Diego say that the results promise to boost crop yields by coaxing plants to bloom faster and to shorten the time it takes to cross-breed new hybrid strains. They could even be used to inspire rose plants to sprout flowers where leaves would normally grow. -- Snapshots of atomic structures have led to many new insights in materials science. But the static images that are produced by a scanning tunneling microscope (STM) don't illuminate what happens while one atom is reacting with others. Now, physicist Brian S. Swartzentruber at Sandia National Laboratories has developed a new STM--one that's so sensitive it can track a moving atom. It should yield new understandings in semiconductor physics. 

-- Grinding the high-quality lenses used in cameras, fax machines, and smart-bomb ``eyes'' has always been more art than science. Until now. Researchers at the University of Rochester have devised a program that predicts the results of grinding various glass formulations. It's a ``significant breakthrough'' in controlling factory-floor operations, says Harvey Pollicove, director of Rochester's Center for Optics Manufacturing.

 Article 80 *******************    



Chips triumphant. (history of microprocessor industry and technology) (includes related article on deconstruction of semiconductor industry)(Forbes ASAP) (Industry Trend or Event) 

Michael S. Malone 
6,378 words 
26 February 1996
Forbes 
S52 
Vol. 157, No. 4, ISSN: 0015-6914 
English
COPYRIGHT 1996 Forbes Inc. 

THE PERSONAL COMPUTER FIRST MADE THE INTEGRATED circuit chip real to most of us. Now, despite the fact that it is the most complicated consumer product in history, the average person knows an amazing amount about the microprocessor and its use in the PC. Packard Bell found out die hard way this past Christmas. Buyers shunned its underpowered PCs. Customers wanted powerful 10-megahertz Pentiums, not the cheaper 75s. Maybe you can go broke by underestimating the sophistication of the American buyer. 

Unfortunately, this expertise has come at the expense of knowing about the rest of the semiconductor industry. In fact, at about $50 billion in annual sales, microprocessors represent only one-third of the $150 billion worldwide semiconductor market. Another 40% is for computer memory. The rest is composed of tiny devices, many selling for no more than a nickel, that are nonetheless critical to our modern work: amplifiers, rectifiers, diodes, optoelectronics and scores of other mechanisms. 

How many chips are out there? Perhaps 200 billion. A staggering figure. And more than 10 billion of these are microprocessors and their cousins, microcontrollers. 

Where are all those chips going? What does this vast invisible chip world mean to your business? 

How can you strap your business to the rising chip rocket? 

Or, perhaps you ask, should you care? 

The average American, it's said, knows more about the atomic bomb than about the integrated circuit; though the former remains (happily) all but unused in a half century, while the latter defines almost every corner of our daily lives. It is high time we close this knowledge gap. If we are going to prepare for the new millenium, we need to start with a common, basic knowledge of chips. 

For that purpose we've created this special issue of Forbes ASAP. We hope you'll learn from it and then save it for future reference. A by that, we mean for the rest of your life because, as surprising as it may sound, the greatest breakthroughs of the semiconductor revolution may still be ahead. 

"IT'S AS SIMPLE AS THIS," says BRIAN F. WILKIE, general manager of Motorola's advanced microcontroller division. "If there is any point in your production process or in the use of your product that is electrically controllable, then you should immediately assume that one of your competitors is using chips to make that product or process faster, cheaper, more reliable and more user-friendly. 

"And that's just the start. If you can figure out any way to add electrical control to manufacturing, production or even distribution of your products, you should do it. Why? Just so you can then put in the chips." 

From children's apparel to currency arbitrage, chain-store retailing to machine tooling, the smartest and best are exploiting chips. If not within products, then in their manufacture, their distribution, their retail sale, their service and support, their marketing or their use by the customer. Today there is at least one semiconductor circuit connected with just about every electrical device or motor on earth. And new product designers, following Wilkie's dictum, have raced to stick the computer intelligence of microprocessors and microcontrollers in the most amazing places: lightbulbs, basketball shoes, ski bindings, bodies irons, credit cards, pet collars, greeting cards, even human bones, to list just a handful of the thousands of recent uses. 

This panoply of applications in the industrialized world is matched only by the sheer volume of chip usage that is anticipated for the developing world. In such places as China, planners are looking to leapfrog the expensive, traditional infrastructure of copper wire and go right to wireless fiber optics--a prodigious leap that will be propelled by scores of billions of semiconductor chips. 

Sometimes it seems as if the best solution is simply to abandon your fears and leap into an embrace with high tech. Buy all the chippacked gizmos you can get your hands on, put robots on the manufacturing line, give your salespeople laptops and hire a platoon of EEs--electrical engineers--to reconfigure your product line. 

But then there are the sobering counterexamples. Just ask Denver International Airport, the Internal Revenue Service or the London Stock Exchange. 

Nearly 50 years of working with semiconductors and 25 years with the microprocessor itself have taught us three critical rules for success in the chip age. You'll find bits and pieces of these rules in many of the popular business books-but never together as in the summary that follows. 

RULE NO. 1: LISTEN TO THE TECHNOLOGY 

"The simple truth is that chips will only become more pervasive," says Charlie Carinalli, senior vice-president and chief technical officer at National Semiconductor. "It's all happening so fast that nobody--not even we in the industry--can look out five years into die future. Often the best we can do is try to be moving in the right direction for next year." 

Each of the 170 million or so microprocessors turned out annually is the result of a lab process that has been compared in complexity to the Manhattan Project. 

Why is all this important to know? Because, in the world of semiconductors, function follows form. Every advance at the chip even in capacity, price, speed or new feature ripples all the way down the computer chain to the IBM Thinkpad in front of you, the Sony Playstation in your kid's room and die dashboard on your car. 

An example: In the early 1980s, as it began designing the Macintosh, Apple Computer switched from the microprocessor it had used for Apple II and III to a new chip, the Motorola 68000. The new chip allowed the Mac to have a different sort of display, called bit mapped, that was clearer and more precise. Bit mapping, in turn, made possible cheap computer graphics, which transformed the entire computer industry. But more than that, bit mapping also lent itself to composition--and, when combined with the new laser printers, to desktop publishing. The result was a brand-new, multibillion-dollar industry. Software companies like Adobe Systems that recognized the power of bit mapping early got rich; typesetting shops that didn't got nuked. 

The same thing is happening today. The Mpact multimedia processor from Silicon Valley upstart Chromatic Research performs seven tasks, including modem/ fax, voice mail and the acceleration of graphics (two- and three-dimensional), audio and video. 

This type of "category elimination," in which entire markets disappear after the arrival of new technology, will be increasingly common in die years to come. The only way to survive it is to see it coming (and there won't be much time), usually from the most unlikely direction. Then, get your company ahead of the curve--or out of die way. 

Luckily, the same process will also lead to more category creation as well. If you've ever wondered why a dozen fast-growing companies always seem to be popping up out of nowhere in places like Silicon Valley, that's the reason. America's high-tech enclaves are really company factories, bringing together venture capitalists, entrepreneurs, suppliers and consultants who wait for the latest chip generation to arrive and then pounce on the new product opportunities it presents. In a hot new market, such as the World Wide Web, 100 or more new companies may jump on the bandwagon. 

The microchip is pulling that wagon. To succeed in the information age, you need to know what new wonders are emerging at the chip and system levels, which companies are developing products using the latest chips, when those products will appear, and how you can use them. 

For those of you who aren't Ees, this may sound daunting. Fear not; the process itself is systematic. The information is there. You just need the will and the time to wade through it. 

RULE NO. 2: MASS-CUSTOMIZE 

Everything you do with chips should serve this larger goal: creating customers for life. This is what chips enable; inexorably, this will be the next competitive battlefield. Companies that learn to produce in volume while using chips to adapt each product to the needs of single customers will have a nearly insurmountable advantage. 

"Mass customization" sounds like an oxymoron. And even those who understand it often assume that it will be possible for only a limited number of products, such as the much heralded Motorola pager fine, which produces millions of units each year, all of them different--manufactured, in Motorola's phrase, "in lots of one." Not so. Some celebrated mass customizers like lighting-control manufacturer Lutron Electronics, Andersen Windows and freezer-maker Baffy Refrigerated Boxes essentially backed into mass customization as they retooled their companies to solve specific competitive challenges. 

Don't assume that mass customization works only for complex products. You really can mass-customize dog food and potato chips. Consider the work of the George Group, manufacturing consultants in Dallas who have developed a model for what they call microproduction. 

According to Francis X. Buckman, formerly senior vice-president of the George Group, large consumer companies, Ming to meet shorter lead times, reduce inventory and still satisfy customer demand for greater variety, have moved to ever-more-centralized mass production. But this, says Buckman, is only a short-term solution, "because eventually the infrastructure--shipping, mailing, etc.--begins to work against them. Centralized facilities have a built-in lead time they cannot overcome. 

"Meanwhile, thanks to new chip-based manufacturing and control technologies, you no longer need to make, say, a 5,000-gallon drum of detergent. Now you can make a small jar of the stuff just as cost-effectively." 

"Microproduction takes advantage of this latter trend through what Buckman calls "mass deployment": moving production out to local sites, even to the retailer. Translation: It is now possible to make fresh potato chips or dog food in small machines right at the supermarket or pet food store, with the exact ingredients, product size and volume the customer desires. Want half the usual caffeine in your Coke? Prefer your Doritos in an 8.5-ounce bag? Does your Pekingese eat only small-sized kibble? Soon, stores will be able to fill your custom orders. The George Group is already working with Hill's Pet Nutrition to begin mass deployment of microproduced dog food in Europe. 

If mass customization is the future, why aren't more people doing it yet? 

Two reasons. The first is technical. Only in die last five years have chips become powerful enough, small enough and cheap enough to do many of the tasks demanded by mass customization. You don't want to put a $500 Pentium in a lightbulb to help it conserve power. But the same Pentium five years from now will cost about 10 bucks, and five years after that, I 0 cents. And when the brain of die most powerful Pc becomes less expensive than shoestrings, imagine the possibilities. Conversely, die new $500 microprocessor of a decade from now will have all the power of today's Cray supercomputer: animation, modeling, speech recognition, pattern recognition, etc. Just imagine what you can do with one of those wired into one of your factory robots. 

Indeed, use your imagination. Failing to do so is the second reason limiting the general acceptance of mass customization. Despite Wilkie's dictum, few manufacturers or service providers can yet imagine the reality of mass customization and how quickly die little chip will get us there. But it will. And some of those companies may be your current or (dropping in seemingly from nowhere) your future competitors. Often, the first to market will quickly own that market, leaving room for perhaps one more competitor. So the real question to ask yourself is: Will my company be one of those two that survive? 

RULE NO. 3: RIDE THE SILICON LEARNING CURVE 

This third and final rule is embodied in the first two. If you fully understand the power of the semiconductor chip, then you know the chip is not a one-time implementation, but a process that must become a continuous part of running your business. The bad news is that since the ENIAC computer of 1946, the efficiency of information technology, especially at the chip level, has jumped by 32 orders of magnitude--that's 100,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000, or about 100 octillion times--not only the greatest single improvement in productivity in human history, but a leap to the very limits of human experience with the universe. And that is how big of an opportunity you've already missed. 

But the good news is that most estimates put us only halfway through the advances of the chip. The greatest are yet to come. So, though you may have missed one universe of opportunities, it is not too late to get on board for the next one. The Chip Revolution has only just begun. 

Just what is a chip? At the core is a switch, an idea that stretches back to the first wooden gates on corrals and herding pens. A gate has a binary function: Open it and animals pass through; close it and they cannot. Translate this to a mechanical system--say a lock or a railroad track--and you have a mechanism that was known to the Romans: a mechanical switch. Run electricity through it and you have that ubiquitous mechanism of modern life, the wall light switch. 

But you've still got a problem. The mechanical switch is a powerful tool, but it has real limitations. For one thing, you need someone on hand to throw it. Somebody has to lock die gate or the cows get out; and some yardmaster had better run down the tracks and swing the big switch arm or two trains will collide. 

So now take that mechanical switch, the one with electricity flowing through it, and add a second electrical line, this one passing through a coil of wire that is wrapped around the metal arm of the switch itself. If you send a current through this second line, it will create a magnet and throw the switch--from afar. Thus, you have an electro-mechanical switch, a key invention of die 19th century and the heart of telegraphy (and, until recently, telephony). 

So far so good. What followed was the idea of using one flow of electricity to control another. No moving parts--a real breakthrough!--but the feat could be done only in a vacuum tube. Then, in the late 1940s, two physicists at Bell Labs in New Jersey, Walter Brattain and John Bardeen, experimenting with a crystalline structure called a semiconductor, came up with this idea: Why not build an electronic switch out of solid materials without the need for fragile glass vacuum tubes? Bardeen and Brattain took their idea to another scientist at Bell Labs who happened to have one of the greatest minds of the century: William Shockley. Intrigued, Shockley offered some critical suggestions . . . and in 1947, two days before Christmas, the team built die first working transistor. 

The transistor, eventually sold in the billions and stuffed in everything from jet fighters to cheap radios, revolutionized electronics. It was very fast, small and used little power. By the end of die 1950s, the solid-state revolution was well under way. Transistors and other semiconductor devices quickly supplanted traditional tubes in almost every application, from radios to computers, and enabled even the rockets of the space age itself. 

All of this presented a terrific opportunity for clever entrepreneurs willing to dive into the semiconductor market. One such player was William Shockley himself, fresh from earning a Nobel Prize for the transistor. Moving back home to Palo Alto, Calif., he gathered the brightest young men in electronics and solid-state physics and set out to take the transistor market by storm. 

Shockley, however, was a miserable businessman, and his young engineers, henceforth known as the Traitorous Eight, soon grew disenchanted and quit to found their own company, Fairchild Semiconductor, the mother finn of Silicon Valley. 

At fairchild, a team led by the Eight's leader, Robert Noyce, set out to find a new, more effective way to manufacture semiconductors. Noyce had the idea (coincidentally also being pursued by a scientist at Texas Instruments, jack Kilby) that the transistor could be created using a lithographic process as a series of etched and plated regions on a tiny sheet of silicon. Moreover, he thought this process, like printing, would be almost infinitely repeatable. And that in turn meant a semiconductor manufacturer could replace a bank of transistors soldered to a printed circuit board with an array of interconnected circuits, digital or analog, printed on a single sliver of silicon. 

In other words, a solid-state system, an integrated circuit. The integrated circuit, following die Noyce/Kilby model and made practical by the "planar" process devised by another of the Traitorous Eight, Jean Hoerni, was a masterstroke, an invention that proved even greater than die triode or the transistor. That's because with the IC, semiconductors stepped onto an economy-of-scale path unlike any the world had ever seen. Using the most common of elements (silicon, oxygen, common metals), chip manufacturers could now construct devices worth hundreds of times their raw material costs. And the photolithographic process enabled the manufacturers to continue to reduce the size of the circuits themselves until they became so microscopic that chipmakers could put tens of millions of them on a single eight-inch silicon wafer. 

Thus were planted the seeds of Moore's Law, the very principle that governs the information age. 

By the mid-1960s, two types of digitals had emerged: logic and memory and, within the latter, two forms developed as well, random access memory or RAM for blank storage, and read-only memory or ROM to hold prepared software programs. These memory chips would in time be further modified into different types (dynamic or static RAM, electronically programmable Rom, nonvolatile memory, etc.) for distinct applications. This division in use was not unexpected, given the computer industry's need for ICs that could replace current processor and memory hardware. 

In 1968, two of the Traitorous Eight, Noyce and Gordon Moore, left Fairchild to start a new company, Intel, dedicated to making memory chips using a new process called metalic oxide semiconductor (MOS). The company was just getting on its feet when an order came from a Japanese calculator company, Busicom, for a new design that could put all of a calculator's functions on just a handful of chips. 

The founders of Intel hesitated, but one of the company's engineers, Marcian "Ted" Hoff, who had been studying the new minicomputers and thought their architectures could be transferred to silicon, finally convinced them to try. Soon a team formed that included Hoff, Intel engineer Stan Mazor, Busicom's top engineer Masatoshi Shima (who soon joined Intel) and a famous silicon designer who'd just joined from Fairchild, Federico Faggin. 

The four-chip set they designed, the Intel 4004, was the world's first microprocessor. Within three years, with the model 8080, the chip's performance had improved eightfold. 

The microprocessor may well be the single most important invention of the late 20th century. Its real breakthrough is that it takes the process of integration to the next step. In the microprocessor, logic, memory and certain discrete functions are all combined into a complete system, a "computer on a chip." 

Since the introduction of the 8080, the microprocessor has embarked on a steady march through generation after generation of improvements, in the process pulling along all electronics, but especially the personal computer, in its wake. The modem Pentium or PowerPC is 1,000 times as powerful as the 8080 and contains three million transistors. This relentless progress, outstripping any comparable technological advance, was first described by Intel's cofounder, Gordon Moore. Moore's Law, celebrated as the defining rule of the modern world, states that the computer chip performance doubles every 18 months. 

If thanks to the computer chip, continuous, unremitting change is our fate, how do we plan for the future? How can we conduct our businesses--our lives--with any certainty? 

The best place to look for answers is in the chip business itself After all, it was the first to experience the future shock of microelectronics, and it has coped the longest. 

So, how do the Intels, Texas Insumentses and Motorolas deal with, and thrive, in such an apparently unpredictable business reality? And how do the fast-moving new companies, which have to attack new markets first to survive, know what to target and which emerging technologies to pursue? 

The answer lies within Moore's Law and its implications. And it would not be exaggerating to say that every businessperson and engineer in Silicon Valley, phoenix, Austin and the rest of the world's tech enclaves works with one eye always on the ticking clock of this law. The smart businessperson, no matter what his or her industry, would be well advised to do the same. 

"Moore's Law is important because it is the only stable ruler we have today," says Tim Bajarin, president of San Jose-based industry watcher Creative Strategies. "It's a sort of technological barometer. It very clearly tells you that if you take the information processing power you have today and multiply by two, that will be what your competition will be doing 18 months from now. And that is where you too will have to be." 

A little background: In 1965, Dr. Gordon Moore was invited to write an article for Electronics magazine, in which he formulated the theory now known as Moore's Law. Moore had not only helped build the first integrated circuits but also had cofounded Fairchild Semi-conductor and his current company, Intel. More anyone in the industry, then or now, he possessed an intuitive sense of the nature of semiconductors, and of how their advances in capacity, size, speed and price seemed to be accelerating. 

On a hunch, Moore took out a sheet of logarithmic graph paper and plotted the performance of past and current memory chips, as well as those he knew were still being developed. To his amazement, the points made a straight line. It seemed that every 18 to 24 months the complexity of those memory chips doubled. Extending the line outwards he predicted that it would take only 20 years to grow the 1,000-bit DRAM memory chip of 1970 to one million bits. 

The very idea of a million-bit DRAM was outrageous when Moore made his prediction. Yet, it proved true. More remarkably, Moore's Law held up for other chips, such as microprocessors, as well. And, with a few adjustments, it also has described the performance improvements of nearly every box those chips have gone into, from personal computers to fax machines. 

Working with Moore's Law, the semiconductor industry discovered some interesting things. For example, you could now predict with some certainty what the chips of not only the next generation, but three or four generations out, would be like--and start designing for them. Thus, it has become a business that focuses less on what is, than on what will soon be. 

As simple as Moore's Law is, its implications are profound. For instance, it suggests you can hold one or more of the variables constant and vary all the others. Thus, instead of track--the latest and most powerful chips as they march into new applications, you can also track a given chip model as its price plummets over the years and it brings its capabilities to whole new markets. You can even look ahead: Thanks to Moore's Law, if you know the pace of change and what level of intelligence you want in your product, then you can predict when that technology will be affordable and plan for it now. 

As you might imagine, making just such predictions is an obsession in electronics. After all, the next equivalent of the video game business or the laptop computer or the CD-ROM may be just around the comer waiting to bestow forward thinkers with almost limitless rewards ... and punish the shortsighted with oblivion. 

A survey of some of the best thinkers in the high-tech industry revealed a wide range of potential future scenarios--and even more potential market opportunities. The irony of Moore's Law is that every one of these predictions, no matter how wild, may at some joint prove true. So may yours. And if the past is any indication, all of the opportunities we can think up will be but a fraction of those that eventually appear. 

For Avram Miller, Vice-President of Corporate Business development for Intel, the big story of the present--personal computers--will remain so, far into the future. "The personal computer," he says, Will be to the 21st century what the automobile was to the 20th. It will reorganize the way we live, play and spend our lives. 

"How it will affect a particular business will vary with the business. But one thing appears obvious--some of the hottest places for doing business will not be places at all, but will exist in cyberspace. There will be a whole new world of virtual products and services that live only in this other world, which we will create as we go along. 

"Say you and I went to college together but we live far apart and we want to get together to watch our old team play. We could do that on our computers--and not only that, we could add to the experience," says Miller. "For example, if I want to stop the action--freeze frame--I could replay that on both our machines, commenting as I went. So could you. So could five of us. Or 10,000. Perhaps we could buy a ticket for an event that exists only on the Internet. Five years from now, wearing 3D glasses similar to the regular glasses we wear now, hearing 3D sound, we could attend that event in a cyberstadium, with the computer acting as our liaison, tracking our location, adjusting our experience. Think of the new businesses and services that could grow from this. 

"I know it sounds wild. It's as hard for us to understand as the airplane was in the 19th century. Yet now we fly on planes every day. Cyberspace will be a world 21st-century man will feel at home in." 

If Miller is looking at consumer electronics, Federico Faggin is, not surprisingly, studying the chip itself The builder of the first microprocessor (see page 96), Faggin is today CEO of Synaptics, a company that researches how chips process sensory information. 

"We tend to predict the future of microprocessors as heading in a single direction: Pentium, Pentium Pro, P7, P8 and so on into the future. And I have no doubt that will occur. But I think there is a second path that's now emerging as well. 

"Call it point-of-need hardware, PON. It arises from the fact that computers are coming down in price and being used by people who don't know anything about computers. We need computers that are more like us, that can talk to us, adapt to our needs, learn from us," says Faggin. 

"What we want are computers that are like people, that share many of the features of our brains--like the ability to learn from experience rather than needing to be told in excruciating detail--without all the complications of emotions and biological requirements. We want computers to be intuitive and have common sense; most of all, we want them to be able to interact with us naturally, through sight, sound and touch. 

"Today's microprocessor architectures just can't do that. But there are other ways. We have a whole body of programmable chips--'plastic hardware," you might call them--like field-programmable gate arrays and field-programmable interconnect components that could be linked together into powerful systems that could be rewired and reprogrammed as you need it. This brings us into a world of 'appropriate hardware.' Suppose you wanted to make a cellular phone call," he suggests. "Then this chipset--or eventually single chip-would configure itself as a cell phone. A few minutes later, you might tell it to become a global positioning system. Or a fax machine. Or a laser printer. 

"What we're talking about is a nondifferentiated set of circuitry that can become what you want. If you want more performance, you buy it, like buying fabric or flooring. "How far off is this? Twenty years probably. Fifty years for biological computers. In the meantime, a convergence of computers and communication, wireless, Internet. That's what will impact you tomorrow." 

Perhaps no one in the world has a better sense of this than Motorola's Brian Wilkie. His division is the world's largest producer of microcontrollers. "Chips have quietly, almost invisibly, changed changed the world around us," Wilkie says. "Your telephone was an electromechanical device, now it is electronic. And the cell phone in your car has more user-friendly features than the ones on most desks. Your hotel door lock has an electronic key that can tell the room to wait five minutes then turn off the fights. 

"What many businesspeople don't realize is that the real design challenge was moving from mechanical to electrical, adding the switches and the little electrical motors. The next step, to intelligence, requires an incredibly small incremental cost increase. That's the great opportunity but also the great risk, because you need to worry about whether your competitors will beat you to it. 

"And no business is immune," he says. "Even if I was designing children's clothing in Indianapolis," he says, I'd be thinking hard about whether I should add intelligence to my designing, cutting, manufacturing, even to the clothes themselves. A few years from now kids could be wearing leather jackets with built-in displays." Wilkie, a Scotsman, laughs, "But what I'm waiting for is for someone to come up with the smart golf tee. It would register your swing as it comes down, then lean left or right to find the sweet spot on your club." 

Moore's Law is THE VERY EMBODIMENT OF the old fable about the Chinese emperor who agreed to the terms proposed by a craftsman--to get paid in a quantity of rice that would double with each square of a chessboard. Halfway down the squares, the emperor realized these terms had committed him to give the clever man all the rice in the world. 

As described by Moore's Law, the electronics industry undergoes the equivalent of the great Industrial Revolution every two years. And it isn't slowing. As Tim Bajarin notes, " Moore's Law may actually be accelerating, such that we may see by the end of the century three or four times' jumps in performance every 18 months." 

Eventually, of course, it has to end. As Moore noted in Forbes recently, if the semiconductor industry will soon represent I of the world's economy, by the middle of the 2 1 st century, it would total 100% of it, which seems impossible. 

There are also technical questions. As the features on the surface of the chip continue to miniaturize into submicron widths, they collide with the rules of quantum physics. The walls of the pathways literally become bumpy with molecules. The miniature circuit sizes throw off ever-stronger electric fields that interfere with the chip's performance. Heat, too. Contemporary chips throw off as much as 10 times the heat as a comparable cookstove surface. Electrons start tunneling through the insulating walls, threatening to short the circuit. Apparently, the chip is racing toward an unbreachable physical wall. 

Six years ago, Dr. James Meindl, former provost of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, proposed a Chip Performance Index CPD to measure how much time was left before the chip reached this barrier. For current designs he guessed the limit of miniaturization was [10.sup.25]. He estimated the current CPI is [10.sup.18]--suggesting that semiconductors have only (!) a 10 million times' jump in improvement left. A huge amount, but small compared with what's happened so far. If Moore's Law is right, that limit is only about 20 years away. If Bajarin is right, even sooner. 

So, there's some consolation: Around 2020 the madness will end and we (or our children) can catch our breath and assimilate the extraordinary events of the preceding half-century. 

But wait, not so fast. Last April, at a National Semiconductor technology conference, Meindl, now with Georgia Tech, listed all of these technological obstacles and then reminded the chip designers that these problems are no greater than the ones the industry has overcome in the last two decades. Human ingenuity will always prevail, he said; the semiconductor revolution will continue indefinitely. 

Craig R. Barrett, Coo of Intel and chair of SIA's Technology Strategy Committee, thinks so, too. Working with the Semiconductor Industry Association, the trade group for U.S. chip makers, Barrett created the National Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors, which identifies potential roadblocks and ways to avoid them. How? No one knows the answer. Future chips may not be silicon but gallium arsenide. They may be cubes floating in liquid nitrogen. The millions of transistors may be gone, aced by arcane structures such as quantum dots or Josephson junctions. Or the chip may be a crystal lattice or a skin of living nerve cells. 

Whatever form the chip takes, it will continue its phenomenal trajectory well into the lives of our grandchildren. We have tied ourselves to a comet whose journey has just begun. 

RELATED ARTICLE: PENTIUM AT A GLANCE 

* Estimated total cost to produce the Pentium: roughly $1.5 billion. 

* Approximate cost for design and development: $200 million. Time spent: three years. 

* Cost to build fabrication plants used to manufacture the Pentium: $1 billion to $1.5 billion each; 75% of the cost is for equipment. 

* Average cost to produce a Pentium processor: approximately $50 Average price of the Pentium: $300 

* Number of Pentium chips produced in 1995: 32 million. The Pentium was developed more quickly than any other generation of Intel microprocessor. Also, it took less time to reach a million units shipped than any other generation-it happened within the fist year. (It usually takes at least year and a half or longer.) 

* Fab plants designed to produce the Pentium can be used in that capacity for only about two years. By that time, the equipment is outdated and the fab has been completely amortized. To get its money's worth, Intel has to run the plant 24 hours a day, seven days a week. Employees work shifts of four days on, three days off, which switches every other week to three days on, four days off. Older fab plants are used to manufacture less-complicated chips such as flash memory or 16-bit microprocessors. 

An estimated $20 of the $50 spent to produce each chip goes into engineering and design; $20 goes into capital-equipment expense and depreciation; 410 goes into chemicals and materials. 

* A 100-megahertz Pentium costs $118 to manufacture and sells for $300. The faster Pentiums cost the same to build but are sold for more. Chips are manufactured, tested and then stamped according to their evaluated performance speed. 

* A 120-megahertz Pentium sells for $357; a 133-megahertz for $520. 

* Estimated salaries of design team: Key architects: $150,000 (for senior people maybe $200,000 maximum with stock options, can make over $500,000) Software programmers: $60,000 Circuit designer: $80,000 to $120,000 Testers: $60,000 to $100,000 Manufacturing workers: $30,000 to $60,000 

RELATED ARTICLE: GLOSSARY 

ASIC 

(application-specific integrated circuit) Devices that perform a specific task using a custom blueprint of logic circuits, which can be mass-produced at low cost. Used in everything from video cameras to microwave ovens. 

Clock speed 

The number of cycles of operation, measured in hertz (cycles per second). The 8088 microprocessor used in the first IBM PC had a clock speed of 4 megahertz. Today's Pentiums run in the range of 75 to 160 megahertz. 

CPU 

(central processing unit) The microprocessor governing the operation of PCs, servers and workstations. 

DRAM 

(dynamic random-access memory) The primary type of RAM used in PCs. It is dynamic because its contents can be changed at will by application running on the system. 

Embedded processor 

A cheap microprocessor built into products like greeting cards and toys. 

Fuzzy logic 

The application of vagueness and approximation to computer logic, allowing computers to respond to less clearly defined input. 

Instruction set 

A set of binary codes that directly manipulate the registers, memory locations and other devices of the microprocessor. These codes are also referred to as microcode. 

Microcontroller 

A stripped-down microprocessor, designed to perform a specific function such as controlling an automotive fuel-injection system or a laser printer. Generally has fewer memory-management capabilities and a smaller instruction set than general-purpose microprocessor. 

Microprocessor 

A CPU on a single chip. Contains the logical elements for performing calculations carrying out stored instructions. 

Microprocessor learning curve 

As more companies employ microprocessors at the heart of their products and services, they will have to improve their products and services at the rate of chip evolution. 

MIPS 

(million of instruction per second) Measures performance of microprocessors. 

Neural networks 

The application of microprocessors and other integrated circuits simulating the architecture of the brain. Neural nets are used for pattern and voice recognition, and other applications requiring cognitive skills. 

Transistor 

An electronic capable of amplifying electronic signals similar to the vacuum tube but made from a semiconductor material such as silicon or germanium. Today's Intel Pentium contains more than 3 million transistors.
