 Article 81 *******************    



Science & Technology: ELECTRONICS 

SCIENCE'S NEW NANO FRONTIER 
The quest: To build supercomputers molecule by molecule 

By John Carey in Washington, with Heidi Dawley in London 
1,772 words 
1 July 1996
Business Week 
101 
Number 3482 
English
(Copyright 1996 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

`Anyone who is not shocked by quantum theory has not understood it,'' said Niels Bohr, one of the 20th century's premier physicists. In the quantum world--the world of atoms, electrons, and other very tiny things--cherished notions of reality collapse. Bits of matter can exist in more than one place at the same time. Electrons tunnel through seemingly impenetrable walls. And some say quantum theory allows the possibility of multiple parallel universes. 

All of this strange quantum behavior, which once mattered only to theoretical physicists, has major implications for the electronics industry. Semiconductors have shrunk to the point where the behavior of individual atoms and electrons will soon begin to matter. Quantum effects are starting to take over, changing all the rules. Unless chipmakers learn to harness quantum physics, growth in computer power and everything else that depends on chips will come to an abrupt end. 

``FUN TIME.'' Around 2010, the width of lines etched into semiconductors will be narrowing to less than one-tenth of a micron. Electrical signals running through those circuits will have so few electrons that adding or subtracting a single one could make a difference--putting chipmakers squarely in the quantum world. ``At that point, we better have a new technology ready to go into production,'' says Gary A. Frazier, manager of nanoelectronics at Texas Instruments Inc. 

That's why scientists around the world are rushing to probe what University of Wisconsin materials scientist Max G. Lagally calls ``the last great frontier in solid-state physics.'' Lagally and others have made tiny structures called quantum dots that can hold single electrons. They are so small that billions could dance on the head of a pin. Researchers have used quantum dots to fashion transistors that switch on or off with movements of a single electron. And they've concocted clever arrangements of quantum dots that could serve as the guts of tiny, powerful computers. ``It's a fun time,'' says Yale University electrical engineer Mark A. Reed. ``The field is exploding, and there's a lot of good work going on.'' 

The current research involves long-range, basic science. ``We have to understand the new physics, new properties, and new devices that might be possible at these smaller scales,'' explains IBM chemist Christopher B. Murray. Indeed, the quantum devices being built or contemplated today may never prove economically feasible. But the notion of building tiny structures molecule by molecule, incorporating advances in chemistry, physics, and materials science, is ``bound to have a tremendous payoff,'' says University of Notre Dame physicist James L. Merz. The promise has led companies such as Texas Instruments, IBM, Hewlett-Packard, and Motorola to back major research efforts. 

The main goal of such research is to control the movement of very small groups of electrons without running afoul of weird quantum effects. Quantum dots could make that possible. These dots are clumps of matter less than 20 nanometers wide--that's 20 billionths of a meter, or about the length of a string of 60 silicon atoms. If such a clump is engineered from atoms with just the right properties, it can turn a free-spirited electron into a caged canary. The electron can't escape without a precisely sized boost of energy from the outside. 

This ``quantum confinement'' gives rise to some interesting phenomena. It makes possible the small, highly efficient lasers already in use in many CD players. These so-called quantum-well lasers are made of an ultrathin layer of semiconductor material sandwiched between two layers of another material. Electrons in the middle are trapped in a quantum flatland, able to move in only two dimensions. That makes it easier to pump energy into them in the way needed to produce laser light. The result: more light produced from less power. 

At Bell Laboratories, owned by AT&T spin-off Lucent Technologies Inc., researcher Loren Pfeiffer is delving even deeper into quantum research. He lops off one more dimension from the flatland and makes lasers based on so-called quantum wires. Inside, electrons can move in only one direction. Quantum-wire lasers can emit light at power levels beyond the practical limits of quantum-well lasers. ``That could prove an overwhelming advantage for communications,'' Pfeiffer says. These higher-power lasers may reduce the need for the expensive repeaters installed every 50 miles or so on telecommunications lines to regenerate laser pulses, which degrade as they travel through optical fibers. 

Scientists expect more gains by moving from quantum wires to quantum dots. But the quantum-dot lasers that researchers have built haven't lived up to expectations. For various reasons, including the difficulty of making these clusters exactly the same size, ``the dots are not as useful as people thought they would be,'' says researcher Gilberto Medeiros-Ribeiro at Hewlett-Packard Co. 

The first challenge is to find a way to mass-produce quantum dots of nearly identical size. Two leading methods are to etch pillars in semiconductors and to deposit clusters on top of them (diagram, page 101). Some researchers are spurning these techniques in favor of chemistry. For instance, Louis E. Brus, a physical chemist at Bell Labs, has pioneered a method that grows quantum-dot crystals in a test tube, molecule by molecule. Using this technique, researchers have built light-emitting diodes that can be tuned to different colors. 

Even more exotic are the quantum structures made from single organic molecules in James M. Tour's chemistry lab at the University of South Carolina. This approach offers the tantalizing prospect of packing trillions of molecule-size devices onto a square millimeter. That single millimeter would contain 10,000 times more transistors than now found in a PC. 

The ability of quantum dots to grab and hold electrons is essential if computing functions are to be performed by movements of single electrons. Physicist Konstantin K. Likharev of the State University of New York at Stony Brook has constructed a model memory chip with quantum storage dots. Single-electron transistors ``read'' the contents of the dots by sensing the electrical field of a trapped electron. On paper, his design could store a terabit of data--that's a trillion bits, or 15,000 times more than existing chips can store--on a chip about the same size as those used today. 

A number of research teams have crafted the single-electron transistors needed by Likharev's model. In 1993, for example, a Hitachi-funded lab at Britain's Cambridge University announced it had constructed an experimental single-electron memory device. Last July, it unveiled a single-electron logic structure. 

Most quantum devices work at extremely low temperatures. Even the slightest heat makes electrons too rambunctious and swamps the tiny quantum effects. However, by carefully altering materials to give quantum dots a tighter grip on their electrons, a few electrical engineers, including Kazuhiko Matsumoto of Japan's Electrotechnical Laboratory and Stanford University's James S. Harris, have recently built single-electron transistors that work at room temperature. ``When we were working at 1 Kelvin [minus 458F], most of the world thought we were really crazy,'' says Harris. ``Now, the work is stimulating a lot of research.'' 

Many problems remain to be solved. Switching speeds can be slow, and a single electron is easily diverted from its intended path by stray electrical energy. So instead of trying to design quantum imitations of today's computers, most scientists are hard at work concocting radically new approaches. 

SQUARE DANCE. At the University of Notre Dame, Craig S. Lent and fellow theorist Wolfgang Porod have come up with a scheme in which the basic building block is a square containing four quantum dots. When two electrons are added, they back into opposite corners. So the square has two possible configurations: electrons in the top left and bottom right corners, or in the top right and bottom left. This is just what's needed for a switch--and it can be flipped back and forth by the movements of electrons in neighboring squares. That means these squares can be arranged to duplicate all the logic functions needed for computing. So far, Lent's team has managed only to build pairs of dots to test the physics. But the initial results are ``stunning,'' says Lent. ``I was prepared for them not to work as well as they do.'' 

Whatever technique proves to be the best way of building quantum chips, years of painstaking engineering lie ahead. ``Quantum computing is so far out that, right now, it's just something fun to scratch our heads about,'' says R. Stanley Williams, head of Hewlett-Packard's new quantum effects lab. Still, researchers foresee a day when trillions of quantum dots could be stacked in layers on otherwise conventional slabs of silicon. That promises a supercomputer on a pinhead--making these exotic structures part of the hottest boomtown on the quantum frontier. 

HARNESSING QUANTUM DOTS

Quantum dots are the smallest electronic structures scientists can imagine. In the 21st century, they may lead to vastly smaller, yet far more powerful, chips and lasers. Here's how researchers are making quantum dots today: 

GROWING CLUSTERS Coat a semiconductor wafer with gallium arsenide. Shower the wafer with molecules of another semiconductor, such as indium arsenide. The falling molecules bind to the gallium arsenide--and act as seeds. As more molecules rain down, they attach to the seed molecules, forming cannonball piles. 

ETCHING PILLARS Print metal squares on the surface of a semiconductor wafer that has an ultrathin quantum layer of gallium arsenide sandwiched between two layers of silicon. Etch away the silicon, using ions that won't erode the metal squares, to expose the quantum layer. 

PRECIPITATING CRYSTALS In a solvent such as toluene, dissolve the ingredients for a two-part semiconductor--say, cadmium and selenium. Add a reagent to prevent the solvent from contaminating quantum dots. Heat carefully so that only a few atoms combine. Result: crystals small enough to be quantum dots. 

PHOTOGRAPH: NOW THAT'S PORTABLE SINGLE-ELECTRON TRANSISTORS HAVE ALREADY BEEN CRAFTED THAT, IN THEORY, MAKE IT POSSIBLE TO STORE 15,000 TIMES MORE DATA ON A CHIP THAT WOULD BE NO LARGER THAN THOSE PRODUCED TODAY PHOTOGRAPH: NOW THAT'S PORTABLE SINGLE-ELECTRON TRANSISTORS HAVE ALREADY BEEN CRAFTED THAT, IN THEORY, MAKE IT POSSIBLE TO STORE 15,000 TIMES MORE DATA ON A CHIP THAT WOULD BE NO LARGER THAN THOSE PRODUCED TODAY PHOTOGRAPH COURTESY OF LAGALLY GROUP/UNIVERSI

 Article 82 *******************    



LEISURE & ARTS 
Bookshelf 

The Illusion of Progress 

By Jim Holt 
929 words 
5 September 1996
The Wall Street Journal 
A14 
English
(Copyright (c) 1996, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

Who is Darwin's most distinguished successor today? Most nonbiologists would probably say Stephen Jay Gould -- public scientist, prolific essayist, scourge of the creationists. Among Mr. Gould's peers, however, his stock seems to be alarmingly low. "The evolutionary biologists with whom I have discussed his work tend to see him as a man whose ideas are so confused as to be hardly worth bothering with," says John Maynard Smith, a leading British theorist. Another blunt assessment is furnished by E.O. Wilson, a colleague of Mr. Gould's at Harvard: "Steve uses the squid tactic. When attacked, he escapes in a cloud of ink." 

There are indeed irritating things about Mr. Gould's books: his cloying cuteness of style, his dithering on whether he is truly a Darwinist or not. "Full House: The Spread of Excellence From Plato to Darwin" (Harmony, 244 pages, $25) opens with the usual cataract of cliches from Shakespeare and the Bible and closes with a bit of speculative bluff. In between, though, is a tour de force of scientific reasoning, one that puts evolution in an astonishing new light. 

Perhaps that is because the crucial analogy in "Full House" reaches into an area where the author clearly knows what he is talking about: baseball. Why, Mr. Gould asks, haven't we seen a .400 batter these past 50 years? The disappearance of .400 hitting has been much examined by baseball pundits. Ted Williams, the last player to clear the mark, blames it on increasing mediocrity at the plate. Others cite the introduction of the sliding pitch, the bullpen, defter fielding, even night games (you can't hit what you can't see). 

The assumption shared by all these hypotheses is that hitting has not kept up with other aspects of the game. But if this is so, it's odd, as Mr. Gould observes, that the mean batting average in the major leagues has been "rock-stable" at .260 during the past 100 years. Something else must be going on here. 

It was while recuperating from a bout with cancer some years ago that Mr. Gould resolved the paradox. Propped up in bed with the "Baseball Encyclopedia," he calculated that a steady shrinkage in the variation of batting averages had taken place since the sport's beginning. In other words, the distance between the best and worst hitters had diminished, and in a surprisingly lawlike way. 

Thus the extinction of the .400 hitter, far from implying a slippage in standards, is a "necessary and predictable consequence of general improvement in play." As the bell curve of batting averages pushes against the upper limit of human ability, the curve's far right tail -- representing otherworldly averages like Ted Williams's -- gets squeezed out of existence. 

But what does this have to do with Darwin? Therein lies the genius of "Full House." The mystery of the disappearing .400 hitter turns out to be the mirror image of another mystery: Why does evolution seem to show a trend toward complexity? Natural selection is about survival, after all, and simple, tiny things are generally more durable and prolific than big, complicated ones. Though Darwinians have always grasped at the principle of progress, it is far from obvious why microscopic blobs should want to become artists and arbitrageurs. 

Well, they don't, really. What we are seeing, Mr. Gould demonstrates, is not a thrust toward complexity but a random increase in variety of form. Evolutionary spread looks like it has a direction because it is blocked on one side: You can't get much simpler than a bacterium. The progress of life is an illusion, like the degeneration of batting. Each arises from taking an extreme item -- the .400 hitter in one case, Homo sapiens in the other -- as a surrogate for the "full house." 

So far, so good. But then Mr. Gould takes a gratuitous whack at humankind's pedestal. It is very well for him to say that evolution has brought us not to the Age of Man but to the Age of Bacteria; these single-cell wonders, found in riotous profusion everywhere from our guts to (perhaps) Mars, may well outweigh all the other life in the universe. But it hardly follows that we humans are just a Darwinian fluke, an improbable accident. Mr. Gould has been making this claim of "radical contingency" for years. Whenever he does, a little ink cloud is not far off. 

It is true that a replay of evolution probably wouldn't produce a species quite as winsome as H. sapiens. But if we are talking more generally about intelligent, language-using, self-aware creatures -- creatures with minds -- then there are plenty of reasons to believe that natural selection will reliably deliver the goods. And most of these reasons are nicely set out in Daniel C. Dennett's "Kinds of Minds" (BasicBooks, 184 pages, $20). 

Having already grappled with the mind-body problem in "Consciousness Explained" (1992), the Tufts philosopher here offers a brisk survey of animal minds and proto-minds. Starting with the molecular "robots" from which all life is descended, he charts a series of small information-processing improvements that would lead inexorably to creatures able to reflect upon their beliefs and desires. 

And consciousness? That strange Cartesian substance disappears from Mr. Dennett's story of mind, much the way progress does from Mr. Gould's story of evolution. Which goes to show, I guess, there's more than one way to rewrite "Hamlet" without the Prince.

What's News 
World-Wide 

World-Wide 

661 words 
10 October 1996
The Wall Street Journal

GORE AND KEMP STRESSED economic policy and tax-cut plans in their debate. 

Republican Kemp asserted that the U.S. economy "is overtaxed, over-regulated, and too many people are suing each other." He presented the case for Dole's tax-cut strategy, claiming it would boost economic growth. Gore discussed the administration's limited tax-cut proposals, saying they would balance the budget without hurting popular programs such as Medicare and Medicaid. 

Gore repeated Clinton's pledge "to mend, not end" affirmative action. Kemp said the country must eliminate all racial quotas, which he implied were inherent in affirmative-action programs. 

--- 

Both parties were accused of "massive violations" of campaign-finance laws. Common Cause, a watchdog group, urged the appointment of an independent counsel to pursue criminal charges. At issue are the "soft money" accounts used to solicit large donations from corporations, unions and wealthy individuals. 

--- 

Burnings of black churches in the South are evidence of an alarming rise in racial tensions in U.S. society, federal civil-rights officials said. Officials of the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, which held community forums on the church fires in six southern states, said the inquiry has shown that the incidents reflect a deeper racial problem. 

--- 

Doctors who treat AIDS patients said that nine months after widespread use of protease "cocktail" therapies for AIDS began, the use of expensive in-patient health services for AIDS seems to be dropping. It appears that the new drugs, while costly, may generate health savings that could offset their costs. 

--- 

Mexican investigators unearthed a body on the ranch of former President Carlos Salinas's brother Raul. They speculated that the remains may be those of Mexican congressman Manuel Munoz Rocha, who disappeared in 1994. Prosecutors allege that Munoz and Raul Salinas plotted the 1994 slaying of a ruling party official. 

--- 

Clinton signed stricter airline safety legislation aimed at protecting travelers from terrorism. The law calls for upgrading bomb-detecting luggage scanners, requires background and fingerprint checks for airport workers, increases the number of FBI agents assigned to counterterrorism and boosts air-cargo inspections. 

--- 

The Nobel Prize in chemistry was won by two Texans and a Briton for discovering the the family of carbon molecules informally known as "buckyballs." The Nobel physics prize went to three U.S. scientists for discovering that at extremely low temperatures a form of helium can flow without losing energy to friction. 

--- 

Mideast negotiators began a fourth day of talks at the Erez crossing between Israel and Gaza. The chief Palestinian negotiator said his instructions were "not to listen to any Israeli proposals that go beyond what had been agreed upon." Clinton's envoy at the talks, Dennis Ross, acknowledged that no progress had been made. 

--- 

Troops loyal to the ousted Afghan regime halted the advancing Taleban army, broke through its lines and pinned down some of its forces in fierce fighting north of Kabul, witnesses said. But officials for Taleban rebels, who captured the capital two weeks ago, said they were holding their ground. 

--- 

A Yugoslav opposition leader quit just 11 days after becoming head of the Zajedno coalition, which had overtaken President Milosevic's government in public opinion polls. Former national bank governor Avramovic said he was resigning for health reasons. His action came less than a month before national elections. 

--- 

Five African nations have expressed willingness to provide troops for a U.S.-organized force to intervene in humanitarian crises there, U.S. officials said. Separately, hundreds of African peacekeepers headed for western Liberia to clear out rebels blocking relief operations. 

--- 

The U.S. temporarily has moved more than 100 Asian immigrants from a crowded freighter to the U.S. naval base at Guantanamo Bay in Cuba. The Xing Da was found adrift last week off Bermuda. 

--- 

Died: Walter Kerr, 83, New York drama critic who won a Pulitzer Prize in criticism, in Dobbs Ferry, N.Y., after a long illness.

 Article 84 *******************    



The road from serfdom: after being almost strangled by nationalization, France's Rhone-Poulenc is back on its feet as one of the world's great chemical companies.(includes related article) 

Howard Banks 
2,239 words 
21 October 1996
Forbes 
156 
Vol. 158, No. 10, ISSN: 0015-6914 
English
COPYRIGHT 1996 Forbes Inc. 

ONE OF THE MANY blunders inflicted upon the French economy by Socialist President Franois Mitterrand in the early days of his electoral triumph was the 1982 nationalization of Rhone-Poulenc S.A. Before nationalization, Rhine- Poulenc hadn't been doing very well. After nationalization it almost expired. 

By 1985 Rhone-Poulenc's sales had stagnated at around $7.5 billion a year. Earnings were just $250 million, a 3% net margin in an industry where well- run companies earn twice as much. There was talk at the Bourse that Rhine- Poulenc might celebrate its centenary by declaring bankruptcy. 

The Socialists didn't need that kind of embarrassment. To bail themselves out, in 1986 they brought in a new man to run Rhone-Poulenc: Jean-Ren Fourtou. Age 47 when he got the call, Fourtou was a nonpolitical businessman who was running Frances largest management consultant, Bossard et Michel. His clients included several U.S. and European pharmaceutical firms, but he had never run one himself. He was, however, acceptable to the unions that represented virtually all of Rhone-Poulencs 80,000 workers. 

The Elyse wanted Rhone-Poulenc salvaged and fast. It was a tall order, yet Fourtou has pulled it off. Sales have increased 2.5 times since Fourtou took over, to $17.3 billion last year. Profits have climbed over threefold, to $780 million. Three years ago things were going well enough that the government was able to re-privatize Rhne- Poulenc. 

Turnarounds like this don't happen by magic. Fourtou succinctly sums up his impression upon arrival: It was a disaster, he says, and that's probably an understatement. 

Long a heavily centralized and bureaucratized company, under the Socialists Rhne-Poulenc became even more so, with even minor decisions pondered at length by a rigid Paris-based corporate bureaucracy. Worker productivity was abysmally low. But top priority was preserving jobs, not increasing productivity. 

Heavily dependent on two lines of business commodity chemicals and textile fibers - Rhone-Poulenc was a prisoner of Europe's economic cycles. Yet there was no effort to break out of this prison. Little was spent on research and development, and not much on new investment. 

Fourtous first move was to push Rhone-Poulencs products into new geographical markets. Gallic to the core, Rhone-Poulenc then did only about 3% of its business in the all-important U.S. market. France alone accounted for over 40% of sales, the rest of Europe for much of the rest. 

In late 1986, less than six months after taking over, Fourtou bought Union Carbides agrochemicals business for $575 million. (The deal indemnified Rhone-Poulenc against any fallout from the 1984 explosion of Carbides plant in Bhopal, India.) The deal gave Rhone-Poulenc a solid U. S. marketing team. It also added insecticides, filling an important gap in Rhone-Poulencs product line. 

Perhaps most important, there was a psychological benefit from the Carbide deal, too. It was decisive, says Fourtou in his precise, fluent English. It gave me confidence for future acquisitions in the U.S. 

In 1990 he paid $1.7 billion for 51% of Collegeville, Pa.-based Rorer Inc., which made Maalox, the U.S. leading antacid. In a complicated pooling of interest deal this was later expanded to a 68% stake. The business has been renamed Rhone-Poulenc Rorer (RPR), but Fourtou was determined that the American company continue to exist as an independent firm with its own board of directors and headquarters in Collegeville. 

Fourtou came under intense pressure from the Socialists to headquarter Rhone- Poulenc Rorer in France, but he stuck to his guns. Maintaining the drugmaker as a separate company, he says, helped me a lot in decentralizing the rest of the group. Today 32% of RPRs outstanding stock trades independently on the New York Stock Exchange, where Rhone-Poulencs ADRs also trade actively (see box, .p. 158). 

Thanks to Carbide, RPR and a score of other, smaller, U.S. acquisitions, the U.S. now accounts for 21% of Rhone-Poulencs sales and this on a much higher sales base than in 1990. Meanwhile, sales in France have dropped to 22% of the total from 40% in 1986. The rest of Europe accounts for 32% (versus around 55% a decade ago). Latin America accounts for 10% and Asia around 7%. 

In broadening Rhone-Poulencs geographical base, Fourtou also diversified its product mix. When he arrived, over 80% of the company's business was done in commodity chemicals and textile fibers. Today chemicals and fibers together account for 45% of sales and 20% of profits. 

Replacing low-margin chemicals and fibers at Rhone-Poulenc are higher-margin pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals. Drugs for animals as well as humans now make up over 45% of sales and 70% of earnings. Their share will grow with the acquisition last year of British group Fisons for $2.9 billion-and with the continued growth at highly profitable Rhone-Poulenc Rorer, which earned $400 million last year on sales of $5.1 billion. 

Another jewel in the health products group is Pasteur Mrieux Connaught, the $1.05 billion (estimated revenues) world leader in vaccinations. PMC has around 32% of total world sales, according to Jean-Jacques Bertrand, chairman of PMC; its vaccines are used on 400 million people a year around the world. It's a growing business. Children will soon get a five-part vaccine, covering diphtheria, tetanus, whooping cough, polio and haemophilus infections. Future possibilities include vaccination against Lyme disease. AIDS, the holy grail in this sector, is getting one-fifth of PMCs research money. 

Under Fourtou, R&D spending has grown relentlessly, both in absolute dollars (to $1.5 billion last year) and as a share of revenues to 8% last year, up from 5% a decade ago. As a result of this investment in the company's future, Rhne-Poulenc has several new drug products, and more in advanced medical trials. Some examples: 

RPRs anticlotting drug, Lovenox, for use after hip replacement or knee surgery, has been in use in Europe for some time and is now available in the U.S. Annual sales could reach $500 million within three years. The Fisons acquisition adds $1 billion a year in sales in asthma treatments in what is a $12 billion-a-year world market. 

In partnership with Novo Nordisk, RPR is developing a hormone replacement therapy for menopausal women. The product will compete with Ciba-Geigys Estraderm and should do very well as female baby boomers pass 50. 

In a list of cancer treatment drugs, potentially the most important new product from RPR is Taxotere, designed to treat breast cancer (it is also designed to treat such things as lung cancer). Philippe Desmarescaux, Rhne- Poulencs group president of chemicals and research, and a Ph.D. chemist, says Taxoteres main rival drug, Bristol-Myers Squibbs Taxol, takes at least 3 hours and up to 24 hours to be injected, meaning discomfort and requiring expensive treatment in hospitals. 

Taxotere, says Desmarescaux, is more soluble as a result of work done by group chemists not part of the pharmaceutical operation. So it takes a one- hour injection in a doctors office and trials suggest it works better. Desmarescaux adds that Taxotere is aimed at a big market: Taxol sold $580 million in 1995. 

Drugs are not the only key to Rhone-Poulencs future. Also moving up in importance are agrochemicals. These currently compose 12% of Rhne- Poulencs total sales and nearly 20% of profits. 

Among the most promising agrochemical products: Fipronil, an insecticide for use in rice farming; and Balance, a highly effective herbicide aimed at U.S. corn growers. Rhne- Poulenc will market Balance in partnership with Monsanto or Ciba-Geigy. Alain Godard, president of RPs agro sector, predicts that these deux blockbusters and other new products will boost Rhne-Poulencs agrochemicals sales by nearly 15% over the coming five years, to nearly $9 billion. He predicts that the divisions profitability will nearly double, to a return on invested capital of 25%. 

In an important sense Fourtou does not see Rhne-Poulenc as a pharmaceuticals company or an agrochemicals company or a textile fibers company. He sees it as a company that spends a lot of money on basic chemical research that may lead to exciting new products that cannot easily be pigeonholed. The important thing here is creating technical partnerships with key customers, says Desmarescaux. Its different from traditional customer-supplier relationships. 

Some such partnerships and product breakthroughs are already at the commercialization stage. Take the so-called green tire developed with Michelin. This uses a new form of silicate to replace carbon in tires, while giving the same life but better gas mileage. Michelin has a three- or four- year lead with the new silicate, but Rhone-Poulenc can sell the material to other tiremakers. Now the group is working with Colgate to develop this same silicate technology to produce a better abrasive for toothpaste. 

Like most chemical firms, Rhone-Poulenc has for many years made titanium dioxide white pigment for paint and paper. Now, working with glassmaker Saint-Gobain, it has come up with a nanoparticle version. These ultratiny particles are transparent and, applied to glass, break down the bond of organic material when exposed to sunlight. So when a pigeon leaves his signature, a bit of sun and the next rain, the windows automatically clean, says Desmarescaux. 

These nanoparticles also block ultraviolet light, so they are also being developed to create a new form of sunscreen. Advantage: Because the material is inorganic, there is no risk of a skin reaction. 

When Rhone-Poulenc was reprivatized in 1993, the ADRs began trading at around $25 a share. Despite the huge moves made by the stocks of competing companies like Monsanto, Dow Chemical and Britains ICI, Rhone-Poulencs ADRs today trade at around $28-less than 14 times this years likely earnings. Partly this unimpressive performance mirrors the performance of the Paris Bourse, but it also suggests that investors have been disappointed in how long it has taken Fourtou to reinvigorate Rhne-Poulenc. Yes, its taken a long time, Fourtou says. But when you are betting on research and development, on innovation, it does take time. I believe we are just starting to see the payoff. 

What will the ultimate payoff be? Fourtou looks out his window on the banks of the Seine near Paris La Defense area. He starts talking about how much more cosmopolitan Rhne-Poulenc will become in the next ten year-show the company's French managers will interact with their American, British, Chinese and German colleagues. We face bigger cultural changes ahead than those that are behind us, he concludes. 

Then he answers the question. 

By 2002, Fourtou predicts, Rhne-Poulencs market capitalization will be five times greater than today's $9 billion. Is that possible? Compound annual growth of 22% a year for six or seven years? Fourtou replies: When I look at the potential from the new products like Taxotere, its not dreaming. I dare not really say it, but I really think it could be much more. 

His colleagues have every incentive to help Fourtou meet that goal. All senior managers at Rhone-Poulenc and at Rhone-Poulenc Rorer must own stock in their companies with a value equal to at least one years pay. 

Very un-French. Totally un-Socialist. 

RELATED ARTICLE: Two Ways To Play 

THERE ARE TWO convenient ways Americans can invest in the turnaround Jean-Ren Fourtou is forging at Frances Rhne-Poulenc. One is to buy New York Stock Exchange-listed Rhone-Poulenc Rorer, the Collegeville, Pa.-based pharmaceuticals maker. Rhne-Poulenc bought 68% of this $5.7 billion (likely 1996 sales) company in 1990. The other 32% of the stock, some 45 million shares, trade actively on the Big Board. Recent price: 733/4, giving the company a market value of $10 billion. Rhone-Poulenc Rorer (symbol: RPR) earned $2.53 a share last year, but higher-margin new products are expected to boost earnings to $3.60 a share this year and $4.10 a share next year. 

The other option is to buy Rhone-Poulencs ADRs, also on the NYSE. Currently $28 a share, the ADRs are priced at 14 times this years estimated earnings of $2 a share and pay a 2% dividend (the 15% French withholding tax can probably be recouped by most U.S. taxpayers as a foreign tax credit). Stock symbol: RP. 

Which is the better buy? Heres some math. Behind each Rhone-Poulenc ADR stands 0.43 of a share of Rhone-Poulenc Rorer. The Rorer operations account for about 45% of the French parents' earnings. So if you buy a Rhone-Poulenc ADR for $28, you, in effect, get $21 worth of Rhone-Poulenc Rorer plus other assets that have an earning power of about $1.10 per ADR. At current market multiples, these assets are probably worth at least $10 per ADR. The extra value you get when buying the ADRs should more than cover against foreign exchange fluctuations, or even a few riots in the streets of Paris. U.S. institutions had boosted their holdings from 27 million shares to 41 million by July.

Developments to Watch 

INNOVATIONS 

EDITED BY OTIS PORT 
288 words 
4 November 1996
Business Week 
199 
Number 3500 
English
(Copyright 1996 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

- In a modern version of the stained-glass arts of the Middle Ages, researchers at Oak Ridge National Laboratory are making stained wafers--colorful disks of silicon, sapphire, or glass. The colors are a result of tiny ``nanocrystals,'' or clusters of a few hundred atoms of gold or other metals, that are planted in the wafers with an ion-beam system. Researcher Clark W. ``Woody'' White says the technology may pay off in the form of optical transistors and chips for a new breed of optical computer, promising speeds 1,000 times faster than electronic computers. 

- All the hoopla surrounding the Information Superhighway is more than just hype, judging from a study by the Center for Applied Special Technology, a think tank in Peabody, Mass. CAST compared the performance of fourth and sixth graders in 28 schools in seven cities, from Oakland, Calif., to Chicago and Miami. The schools were split evenly between those with and without Internet access. At the end of a course on civil rights, the students attending schools with online links scored significantly higher. 

- A multinational research effort involving the U.S. Agriculture Dept. and scientists in three other countries has scored a major victory. The team has found a gene in rye that could be transferred to wheat to produce a new strain able to grow on billions of acres worldwide where it can't today--because of aluminum in the soil. ``No plant likes aluminum,'' says plant geneticist J. Perry Gustafson of the Agricultural Research Service's Columbia (Mo.) lab. But rye has a gene that enables it to tolerate levels of aluminum that kill wheat. Researchers are working to transfer it to wheat.

The Property Report 

Workspaces: Nobel Niche 

By Terzah Ewing 
353 words 
6 December 1996
The Wall Street Journal 
B16 
English
(Copyright (c) 1996, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

A look at the places where businesspeople work 

-- WHO: Richard Smalley, 53-year-old professor of chemistry and physics, Rice University, and one of this year's winners of the Nobel Prize for chemistry. 

-- WHERE: 6100 Main St., Rice University, Houston. 

-- WHAT YOU SEE: At first, your typical academic's cubbyhole, its walls lined with titles like "The Most Beautiful Molecule." But closer inspection reveals a precise theme: homage to the buckyball, a carbon molecule no one knew existed until 1985. Dr. Smalley and his colleagues that year made the discovery that enabled them to snag the ultimate prize in science. 

Behind his desk rest several pastel-colored plastic tinkertoy-like models of buckyballs. A green and orange origami version, sent by an admiring stranger, adorns his computer. One bookshelf supports a champagne bottle containing 65-million-year-old buckyballs captured and bottled for Dr. Smalley by a geologist friend. 

Notably absent from among his assorted awards and degree certificates are signs of his latest honor. He'll get his Nobel Prize on Dec. 10. Dr. Smalley hasn't figured out where in the office he'll put the leather-bound certificate and a copy of the gold medal he'll receive. The real medal is destined for a safe-deposit box. 

-- WHAT HE SEES: "These days almost all of my time is spent in here, not in the lab. See that pile? That's unread mail, and I don't know if some of it will ever be answered. I look around and realize that the first thing I was supposed to do today, which was write a letter of recommendation, hasn't been done. 

"This office is the home of the discovery of the buckyball. The structure was conceived here back in 1985; we sketched it out on a chalkboard that used to be on that wall. We didn't know then that this molecule would be the harbinger of a whole new class of molecules. It's hard for me to believe sometimes how many other people are now researching this."

 Article 87 *******************    


INFOTECH SPECIAL REPORT 

WHAT IT'S REALLY LIKE TO BE MARC ANDREESSEN SURE, NETSCAPE'S 25-YEAR-OLD CO-FOUNDER IS THE MULTIMILLIONAIRE POSTER BOY OF THE INTERNET AND A GEN-X ICON. BUT YOUTH MAY BE NO ADVANTAGE IN A BUSINESS DOMINATED BY A 41-YEAR-OLD NAMED BILL GATES. HAS ANDREESSEN GOT WHAT IT TAKES TO COMPETE? 

RICK TETZELI REPORTER ASSOCIATE SHAIFALI PURI 
5,968 words 
9 December 1996
Fortune Magazine 
136+ 
Issue: DECEMBER 9, 1996 VOL. 134 NO. 11 
English
(Copyright 1996) 

To get a sense of New Lisbon, Wisconsin, all you need to do is drive up the Interstate from Milwaukee, turn off at the exit marked by the town's water tower, and read the signs. "Welcome to New Lisbon, the Friendly City!" exclaims the first billboard. The next is even more auspicious--an ad for one of the town's dozen or so churches proclaiming that "Jesus Saves!" 

New Lisbon (pop. 1,491) is a typical Midwestern town--small, friendly, slow. The passage of time seems measured less by season than by sport: football, basketball, baseball, and the wait for football again. On a Tuesday night in October, at least 150 people show up to watch the junior high football team play Bangor junior high. Sure, it turns out to be an exciting game--the hometown Rockets win in overtime after tying the score on the last play of regulation--but that's not the reason one-tenth of the population is here. "What else would we do?" asks one fan. "There's nothing else to do in New Lisbon." 

Nope--as in so many small towns, if you want to make it big, you split. New Lisbon's success stories are all somewhere else--Linda Balgord is starring in the road show of Sunset Boulevard, and Tammy Richmond is a Los Angeles physical therapist with the Oakland Raiders and other professional sports teams. 

Then there's Marc Andreessen, who seems to be everywhere but in New Lisbon. An Alta Vista search on his name in cyberspace turns up 7,488 links, including one to Kay's Bed-Cam, where Kay says, "I'm dreaming of marrying Marc Andreessen! (It used to be Bill Gates)." In the real world, Andreessen probably gets more press than anyone in the computer industry but Gates himself. He made the cover of Time at 24, a prime example of "The Golden Geeks." 

Just four years out of college, Andreessen is the main man behind the most dramatic shift in computing since the advent of the PC: the rise of the Internet. With his buddy Eric Bina, Andreessen wrote the first version of Mosaic, the browser that made the World Wide Web accessible. Then he and billionaire Jim Clark founded Netscape Communications. 

But now Andreessen, like Netscape, is at a turning point. The company took off by giving away its Navigator browser, an easy-to-use piece of software that makes the World Wide Web available to anyone, including computer neophytes. This strategy worked brilliantly: Spending little on advertising and marketing, Netscape placed Navigator on 45 million PCs and reaped tons of mind share. But the company's success led to an industry makeover. Sun, Oracle, Intel, and, yes, Microsoft began redirecting resources to create software based on Internet technology. 

According to Andreessen, Netscape, which made $7.7 million on sales of $100 million in its last quarter, is aiming for revenues of $5 billion by 2000. Given the competition, it will need a lot more than good PR to get there. It plans to grow by selling more software that runs in the guts of corporate computer networks, the so-called intranet infrastructure. The software will make it easier for employees to do such things as fill out medical forms, update a database of sales contacts, or collaborate on a presentation. Software, in other words, that's useful but not sexy. 

So it seems fair to ask sharper questions about Andreessen: Is this 25-year-old programmer who never took a business course in his life an asset to his company? Can he help Netscape continue its phenomenal growth at a time when its market has been invaded by Microsoft? Is he anything more than a one-hit wonder, the Golden Geek of the moment? 

The best way to answer these questions is to look back, both at Andreessen's past and at the computer industry's. Andreessen's career so far mimics the careers of two earlier Golden Geeks: Gates, of course, and Steve Jobs. Each worked with a partner to create a product that changed the course of the industry: Jobs and Steve Wozniak, a more technically sophisticated engineer, designed the Apple I and II; Gates and Paul Allen propagated the PC operating system; Andreessen collaborated with Bina, whom he credits with doing the grunt work on Mosaic. The partners are all on the sidelines now: Wozniak is out of the computer industry altogether; Allen owns an intriguing mishmash of companies but is hardly a central figure; and Bina, a Netscape employee, calls himself "just a computer programmer." 

Andreessen, then, is the partner who moved beyond the technology to seek a splashy business career. Whose path will he follow? Will he become another Jobs, who was never an adroit manager but who had the opportunistic touch and marketing sense to make himself hundreds of millions of dollars? Or does Andreessen have the single-minded drive to match Gates and make Netscape a software supercompany? 

The comparison Andreessen hears all the time, given the very public competition between Netscape and Microsoft, is the one with Gates. It's an analogy that leaves him nonplussed. He has never met Gates, and isn't sure he wants to. "Think about it," he says outside a Palo Alto coffee shop. "What would we talk about? His stock? My stock? He's got more. He'd tell me about his house. I'd tell him I've got a house too. Mine's a normal house." 

COUNTRY BOY 

Gates is a product of wealthy parents, private schools, and, briefly, Harvard. Andreessen comes from the middle class, public schools, and the University of Illinois. Gates's father is an influential lawyer, and his mother (who died in 1994) was a teacher and prominent community leader. Andreessen's father is a retired seed salesman. His mother works for Lands' End, the retail catalogue company. Gates grew up in suburban Seattle, in a culture where achievement was expected. Andreessen grew up in a little town where the quarterback was king. 

Unlike most of his friends, Andreessen had no interest in sports. "A sure sign," he says, "of someone who's going to leave and move to California." So he turned to the most interesting thing he'd read about--computers. He taught himself Basic by reading a book at the local library. He didn't actually get his hands on a computer until sixth grade, when he began using the school library's PC. Andreessen's first program was a virtual calculator that could do his math homework. But there was a problem. When the janitor closed up at the end of the day, he turned off the power in the building, wiping out the program. Andreessen's parents solved that prob-lem by giving him his first computer, a Commodore 64, in the seventh grade. 

"Marc had an intellectual capacity that could intimidate people," says Ken Adams, who was principal of New Lisbon High when Andreessen attended. Yet teachers and classmates say Andreessen also displayed qualities that to this day distinguish him from the average geek: a keen sense of humor, a likable combination of brashness and humility, and a curiosity that extends well beyond computer languages and operating systems. By all accounts, he was a good all-around student who did well in the subjects he cared about--computing and math, of course, but also history and English. Says classmate Clay Haschke, who is studying for a bachelor's degree in nursing at Viterbo College in La Crosse, 40 miles west of New Lisbon: "Marc just seemed to have a broader sense than the rest of us. Say we were talking about God or something. He would talk about it in a more complete way, with more than one view. And we'd sit back on our heels and say, 'Wow, oh yeah.'" 

THE FIRST STEP 

Andreessen has a voracious appetite. The thing he likes least about Palo Alto, his home now, is that "everything closes down around ten o'clock. It's hard to find a restaurant that's open. Your choices are Denny's: Denny's on El Camino or Denny's at Rengstorff." Andreessen is not credible when he complains about either Denny's: This, after all, is a guy who lives on French fries, chocolate malts, and canned delicacies like SpaghettiOs. More interesting than his penchant for junk food is his appetite for less tangible fare. A conversation with Andreessen jumps across a whole range of ungeekish subjects, including classical music, history, philosophy, the media, and business strategy. It's as if he has a hypertext brain, which helps explain why he got so involved with computers at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. That's where Andreessen first discovered the Internet, the medium that enabled him to travel so far beyond his small-town roots. 

It's easy to forget how cumbersome the Internet was just four years ago. A British academic named Tim Berners-Lee had invented the World Wide Web, allowing documents to be embedded with hypertext links that connected them to other documents around the globe. But the Web was not manageable. Users had to learn a variety of functions: FTP allowed you to download documents from another computer; Gopher helped you find documents; Telnet allowed you to log on to someone else's computer; and so on. "Internet software was ten years behind the hardware," says Andreessen. "I realized that we could pull the software forward a few years." In November 1992, he approached Eric Bina with a proposal--why not write a program that hid all those functions behind an attractive user interface? 

Andreessen and Bina worked for the National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA), the University of Illinois's world-class computer research center. Bina, who had already received undergraduate and master's degrees in computer science from Illinois, was a salaried employee developing a program called Collage. Andreessen was an undergraduate who started out earning $4.50 an hour writing code for a graphics application called Polyview. Working out of small offices in the basement of the school's Oil Chemistry Building, the two wrote Mosaic at night and on weekends, whenever they could. Says Bina: "Sometimes we would work through the night and into the next day, and sometimes we'd work for three or four days in a row and then crash." 

Bina was the master programmer, while Andreessen, for whom classes became an afterthought, played team leader, bringing in other programmers to write versions of Mosaic for PCs and Macs (the original was for Unix-based workstations). "I really liked programming," says Bina. "Marc always had higher aspirations. He liked being in charge and directing other people, and he liked being the idea man. He told me that he'd be really upset if he was still programming by the time he was 30." Says Andreessen: "The only thing programming gets you is RSI, from all the typing." 

Andreessen and Bina completed the first version of their browser in six weeks. Its official name was NCSA Mosaic, and since both men were university employees, the code belonged to Illinois. It was a wisp of a program, a mere 9,000 lines of code, but Mosaic's simplicity was its charm. Suddenly the World Wide Web was accessible to everyone. Neither Bina nor Andreessen had any idea that the browser would, in essence, re-create the software industry. NCSA director Larry Smarr, who says he met Andreessen at the first demonstration of Mosaic in January 1993, thought otherwise. "When I saw it," he remembers, "I said, 'This will change the world.'" 

A ROUGH PATCH 

Mosaic was an immediate hit. Between January, when the first version was released on the Net, and December, when Andreessen graduated with his BS in computer science, millions of copies were downloaded by users all over the world. Andreessen talked with Smarr about how he could make a business of the browser. Smarr explained that while the university was glad to license code its employees had written, it did not have a system in place to incubate commercial companies. He told Andreessen he'd have to find his entrepreneurial niche outside academia. 

But the niche wasn't obvious to the 22-year-old. Back in 1993, the Internet seemed little more than a buzzword. The real money, experts said, was in interactive television being developed by companies like Oracle, Time Warner, and Bell Atlantic. "The Internet was a toy then, right?" Andreessen says. "That was when the whole interactive TV thing was peaking, one of those frenzies the industry indulges in when it loses sight of what's happening in the real world. So I wasn't sure about the browser." 

The only thing he did know for sure was that Mosaic was his ticket out of the Midwest. The boy from New Lisbon had grown disenchanted with Champaign. "Coming from Wisconsin," he says, "I thought Champaign was the big city. I was wrong. Champaign is cornfields and pig farms. If Champaign has a positive attribute, it's that it's so isolated and cut off that all you can do is work." Andreessen headed for--where else?--Silicon Valley, to write programs for Enterprise Integration Technologies, a small software company that had recruited him because of his work on Mosaic. Enterprise, which designed security software for Internet transactions, never made much of a splash; it was acquired a year ago by VeriFone. But Andreessen was free of Champaign. At this point his life resembled that of an average 22-year-old college graduate. He rented a cheap apartment. He bought a Ford Mustang. And he met his fiancee, Elizabeth Horn, a commercial real estate broker who had studied cognitive neuroscience at Pomona College. 

But then he received an E-mail message from Jim Clark, who had recently left Silicon Graphics after losing a fight over the direction the company he had co-founded was to take. Clark invited Andreessen to his house, just to talk. Then he took him for a sail on his yacht to talk some more. Clark was enamored of the idea of creating software for interactive television, and he thought the browser might serve as the interface for interactive TV subscribers. But as Andreessen explained the World Wide Web to him, Clark decided they should focus instead on browsers and servers for the Internet. Shortly after that first E-mail, Clark made Andreessen an offer he couldn't refuse: Let's start a company together. 

It's a moment that has taken on the status of Silicon Valley myth, even though Netscape, founded in April 1994, is less than three years old. Yet before the first version of Navigator could claim 75% of the market, before the IPO could make Clark the first Internet billionaire and increase Andreessen's net worth by $100 million or so, the two had to get through a rough patch. 

"You know," says Andreessen, "I try to keep emotions out of things, and I generally can, but this Illinois thing..." 

The Illinois thing is the bitter legal battle that he, Clark, and Bina (one of Netscape's first employees) fought with the University of Illinois. Officials there maintained that Netscape--originally named Mosaic Communications--was improperly using a name created at the university. The dispute was settled out of court when the university accepted a settlement worth far less than the tens of thousands of Netscape shares it had been offered at one point. The fight is over, but it's not forgotten. Says Andreessen: "They basically tried to shut us down. They started a campaign in the press to make us look like thieves. It was bizarre." 

Larry Smarr at NCSA professes no bitterness. He remembers Andreessen as "one of a long series of very bright students that NCSA has hired." Smarr says he looks back on the creation and spread of Mosaic with pride. "We changed the world," he says. "Our programmers formed a core team at Netscape. And it was our software, licensed by Microsoft, that allowed them to come to market so fast with Internet Explorer. The technology transfer from NCSA created the market, as well as the No. 1 and No. 2 competitors. We preheated the market by giving away Mosaic. There has never been a more successful example of technology transfer, in terms of creating a new market in the short term. We think of it as a model for the future." 

Maybe he should think twice--or three or four times. "There's a level of donation and contribution from me and from Clark and from others that {the University of Illinois} will never see," says Andreessen. "Over the next five years they will miss out on what would probably have been in excess of $100 million worth of contributions. That's a pretty big hit." 

MAKING IT 

If Andreessen seems a mite satisfied with how he's not spending his loot, he doesn't crow about what he does spend. "I was pretty comfortable before," he says. "I'm just sort of more comfortable." Let's define comfort: Over the past eight months, Andreessen has sold at least 500,000 Netscape shares worth $28 million. He still has 1.5 million shares, worth $82 million, in which he is 60% vested. And he brings home a salary and bonus, which he declines to disclose. 

He's not living ostentatiously, though--at least not in Valley terms. He bought a house to replace his apartment; a Mercedes to replace his Mustang; two bulldogs; a fancy stereo; a microwave; a new computer; hundreds, if not thousands, of classical CDs; and a couple dozen Polo shirts. Even with the new shirts, his attire is best described as frat-party ready. And, for the record, his eating habits remain pedestrian--he takes his French fries extra-crispy and prefers a chocolate malt to Chardonnay. Andreessen is incapable of what most people call cooking. "I can do Campbell's Chunky Soup, SpaghettiOs, Pillsbury cinnamon rolls, and cereal. The last time Liz went away, I couldn't find the can opener. So I went, like, five or six days and couldn't eat at home." 

Andreessen has yet to get the hang of a life of leisure. He keeps an engineer's schedule, waking around 10 a.m. and finally sacking out at 3 a.m., with a break from work every afternoon to walk his bulldogs: 2-year-old Lily, and Clayborne, a 6-month-old with "a destructive streak and a slight potty-training problem," says Andreessen. At night, after he and Elizabeth somehow navigate dinner, they might rent a video. Andreessen's into Steven Seagal, Steve Buscemi, bad B movies, and Jackie Chan-style Hong Kong flicks. He spends the hours after midnight reading and returning hundreds of E-mail messages. 

He's taken two vacations since starting Netscape--if you count a two-day trip to New Zealand last fall as a vacation. He and Elizabeth didn't check out weather reports, arrived during the rainy season, and flew back to California the next day. This year they stayed closer to home, renting a bungalow at a fancy hotel in Santa Barbara. Bob Dole happened to be staying there too. "I walked outside," says Andreessen, who worked most of the vacation, "and the Secret Service guys are hitting on Liz. I said, 'Aren't you supposed to be guarding the candidate?'" 

THE EDUCATION OF MARC ANDREESSEN 

Sometimes it's a struggle to remember that Andreessen is a businessman, responsible for providing the guiding vision for a $400-million-a-year company. He's supposed to be devising ways to beat back the evil empire in Seattle, and instead he's imitating the way his bulldogs pant, or explaining why techno-journalist/visionary George Gilder is like Friedrich Nietzsche, or telling you about the homoerotic motifs in the work of Hong Kong action-film director Ringo Lam. Sure, you asked him about this stuff, and sure, you asked for all this time with him, but...aren't industry leaders more restrained than this? Did Bill Gates ever behave this way? Is this 25-year-old really an asset to a company in need of discipline? 

To understand why the answer to that last question is yes, it helps to know what Andreessen does--and does not do--at Netscape. He is not a programmer; he hasn't written a line of commercial software code since late 1994. He is not a manager, since the only person reporting to him is his administrative assistant. And he does not hang out in an office; he doesn't even have his own cubicle at Netscape's Mountain View campus. His title is senior vice president, technology. He reports to CEO Jim Barksdale, 51, who defines Andreessen's role as Netscape's "principal technology visionary. Marc articulates in practical, pragmatic terms how his vision can become a real product. He's also a spokesman and an evangelist, and he does lots of sales calls and public presentations." 

Andreessen and Barksdale complement each other. According to Neil Weintraut, a Silicon Valley venture capitalist, "Barksdale is a mentor, providing strategic moderation. He has the credibility of the wise old man. It's not just that he has more gray hair; he also has a style that's very calming." Andreessen, on the other hand, is the Energizer Bunny. He does whatever he can to push Netscape products out the door as fast as possible. "Marc wants more sooner," says Debby Meredith, 37, who heads development of new versions of the Navigator browser. "He pushes. He'll say, 'Debby, the next version's gotta have this feature. You'll be a hero if you get this in.' And I'll tell him that it will impact the delivery date. And he'll say, 'Yeah, but you'll be a real hero if you get it in on time with that feature.' And I'll say, 'Yeah, Marc, and then there are the laws of physics.'" 

He can be just as pushy when it comes to marketing and strategy. His midnight E-mail often discusses tactics designed to stymie Microsoft. Says Netscape chief technology officer Eric Hahn, 36: "Marc is on the aggressive side, especially when it comes to Microsoft. He always wants us to announce products earlier than we do, his argument being that Microsoft is doing just that. They're announcing three- and five-year road maps, and we're announcing six-month road maps." 

Executives like Barksdale, Meredith, and Hahn are considered adult supervision at Netscape. The company has grown from 300 to 1,250 in a year, and many of the new hires have come directly from places like Stanford, Berkeley, MIT, and, yes, Illinois. Even though Andreessen spends most of his time with the old folks, he is one of the kids. His energy and accessibility make him an ideal bridge between the youths and the adults. Atri Chatterjee, 34, head of server product marketing, says, "I try to get everyone who's managing a product to meet him. He's very unassuming. I had him down to meet a bunch of new hires, and one of them came up to me afterward and said, 'Who was that? He looks really familiar.'" 

That's partly because Andreessen has performed so spectacularly in his role as Netscape's primary marketing image. True, Barksdale now gives the company an air of respectability--in this summer's rash of "browser war" stories, it was Barksdale, not Andreessen, who faced off with Gates. But Andreessen is the guy who made Netscape cool, and who continues to make the company's name synonymous with the Internet. Says Jerry Michalski, editor of industry newsletter Release 1.0: "Marc's a sort of folk icon among Gen X-ers and other young people turned on by the Web. It doesn't hurt that he made a fortune. He mixes youthful idealism with the capitalism that's in now." 

Andreessen clearly enjoys his press, even calling himself a bit of a "media whore." But he doesn't seem carried away with his celebrity. "The press has been a big part of our ability to market ourselves, and our ability to build a brand image," he says. "It's extraordinary that with hardly any advertising we've been able to do this much this quickly." 

A lot of what made Andreessen a phenomenon is the simple story of a kid who got rich quick. But those who have watched him closely at Netscape see an increasing sophistication. Says Bill Gurley, an Internet analyst at Deutsche Morgan Grenfell: "Calling him a geek or Webhead or poster boy is selling him way short. He's not married to technology the way a pure tech guy would be. He's into the business strategy, including marketing, positioning, and figuring out the best way for Netscape to win. He also has the curiosity that you see in many brilliant people. When Marc doesn't know about something he thinks he needs to understand, he gets a book and talks to people and learns. The guy has a knowledge base that's just incredible." 

While Andreessen didn't take any business courses at Illinois, he's been in a crash course ever since. "The stuff that interests me is business strategy and customers--how people are using the technology," he explains. "There's a triangulation between what's possible with the technology, what business needs people have, and what's actually practical. You triangulate on that, and that's where the sweet spot is for making a successful business and driving it further. That's the most interesting part." 

Andreessen's reading list consists mostly of books about business strategy and trade magazines he catches on the Web. Talking about Netscape's success, he recalls three books that an obscure government agency published on the growth of the electronics, railroad, and telephone industries. "There's very little here that's new in terms of the mechanics of growing a company," he says. Later, in a discussion about whether Internet stocks will flame out, he says, "You should check out Roger McNamee's article on the history of all this in The Red Herring {a technology magazine for investors} this month. He says that every major new wave of innovation in America has this frenzy of speculation." 

Andreessen's favorite subject is Netscape's strategy, which has changed radically since he and Clark got started. "It turned out that there are two primary opportunities for Netscape--one is Internet, one is intranet," says Andreessen. "Over the long term they become the same thing. But for the near term, the amount of money going into the intranet far outweighs what's going into the Internet--by three or four orders of magnitude." Netscape now positions itself as an intranet infrastructure company, seeking to provide software that runs inside your corporate network. That pits its products against Microsoft's Back Office suite of server software, and against IBM's Lotus Notes. "In the next five years every business in the world will be picking one of these three companies," says Andreessen. "We won't get all of it. My theory is that Microsoft and Netscape will be the dominant vendors, and Notes will have a niche." He predicts Netscape's share of the $10-billion-a year market in 2000 will be 50%. 

The strategy makes sense, but it doesn't make for good copy: A plumber is a lot less interesting than the leading light of the Internet. Andreessen says he won't miss the attention. "We'll get less sexy as a company. That'll mean fewer appearances on CNN. That's fine." 

GREAT EXPECTATIONS 

As Netscape tries to transform itself from an Internet startup to an established provider of corporate software, Andreessen seems immune to the pressure. "This is going to sound weird," he says. "But the gut-wrenching tension has decreased over time because survival has become less of an issue. Before you ship a product, you're operating on borrowed time and borrowed money. You don't have a business. All you've done may go up in smoke. You may not be there in three months. That's gut-wrenching. There's a good amount of pressure now, but it's not the same. If you put $200 million in the bank and do a $400-million-a-year run rate, you're not going to disappear overnight. You may be gone in 12 months, but not overnight." 

Asked if he would still be at Netscape in 15 years--Gates, after all, has run Microsoft since 1975--Andreessen pauses. "Fifteen years is an awful long time. If we are an independent company then, there's a good chance of it. But I would hope the company can do without me--I mean that in a nonegotistical way. At some point companies have to go on without their founders." That does, of course, beg the question of what's next. But Andreessen is more focused on two other time frames: the here and now, and the way far out. What's next is an open question. 

As for the way far out, he has plenty to say about it. One of his favorite novels is The Diamond Age, by Neal Stephenson. Its plot is built around nanotechnology, the science of manipulating individual atoms to build devices and circuits. Although nanotechnology is being taken more and more seriously, its possibilities seem positively chimerical. At its extreme, nanotechnology would allow us to create stuff--anything from a chair to a blimp--out of practically nothing. "So many of the things people do are going to be unnecessary when matter can be rearranged arbitrarily," says Andreessen. "I mean, this gets into all kinds of bizarre stuff. Immortality. There's no fundamental reason why the breakdown of cell structures is inevitable." 

He pauses to sip some iced tea, waiting to see if I've heard correctly. "There's no reason..." I begin. 

"There's no reason death should happen," he rushes on. "There's no reason decay shouldn't be totally repairable. There's no reason you shouldn't be able to design exactly the body you want." 

"Well," I say, "there's no reason I should be unhappy." 

"That raises a very philosophical question," he says. He seems exhausted, or perhaps disappointed by my skepticism. He slows down for a second. "Well, at the very least the concept of manufacturing goes away." 

It's one of those asides that make hanging out with Andreessen so interesting. But it also makes you wonder if this guy's too far out, too undisciplined, too young to keep Netscape in the big time. It makes you wonder if he can play in the same league as Gates. 

But Andreessen hasn't shown much interest in being like Bill--or Steve. When Jobs and Gates introduced their breakthrough products--Jobs's "insanely great" personal computer, and Gates's DOS operating system--they were already running their companies. Andreessen, on the other hand, doesn't want to run Netscape. That's Barksdale's job, and it leaves Andreessen free to do what he does so well: get people inside and outside the company inspired by the new products he thinks they need. He's also free to learn about the industry he's helping transform. 

At Apple, Jobs flamed out spectacularly after a brilliant start, giving way to John Sculley when the company needed a real manager. It's hard to see Andreessen following a similar path, since he hasn't shown much need to prove himself at things he despises, like football, or managing. 

As for Gates, well, Andreessen isn't sure the man in Seattle has a clear sense of the road ahead. "Microsoft is doing things that are not rational for a company in a leadership position," he says. "They define their culture as one where they deliberately try to destroy competitors as their primary way of doing business. The generally accepted strategy for a market leader is to grow the market, not to wipe out the remaining 10%. It may take years, but fundamentally their strategy is flawed." 

By then, Andreessen figures he may have other options. "You know," he says, "within 30 years someone's going to make a shitload of money in nanotech." 

REPORTER ASSOCIATE Shaifali Puri 

ANDREESSEN'S READING LIST 

It says a lot about how he's changed. He used to read history and philosophy. Now he's into digital Webzines, business tomes--and hours of E-mail. 

Inside the Tornado Geoff Moore 

The Prize Daniel Yergin 

Marketing High Technology: An Insider's View Bill Davidow 

Relationship Marketing: Successful Strategies for the Age of the Customer Regis McKenna 

Only the Paranoid Survive Andy Grove 

Hypercompetitive Rivalries Richard D'Aveni, Robert Gunther 

The Diamond Age Neal Stephenson 

MUSIC HATH CHARMS 

Andreessen began to appreciate classical music in college. "When you're paid $4.50 an hour, it's hard to justify buying crappy rock music CDs," he says.

In the Lab 

Membrane May Help Man Meet Machine 

By Dean Takahashi 
Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal 
902 words 
31 January 1997
The Wall Street Journal 
B1 
English
(Copyright (c) 1997, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

Jay Groves wants to make it clear that there are limits to his laboratory breakthrough. "Our goal," he says emphatically, "is not to stick a chip in your brain." 

His disclaimer says a lot about the implications of a discovery on the far frontiers of computer science and chemistry: a way to attach a living cell to a computer chip via a microscopic membrane. 

So-called biochips have been a staple of science fiction for years. William Gibson, author of the 1984 cyberpunk novel "Neuromancer," envisioned chips that could be plugged into someone's brain so that they could instantaneously catch up on their history or literature. 

But in real life, living cell membranes are notoriously slippery, amorphous things that can't be connected to the surface of a solid silicon chip. So three Stanford University researchers -- Mr. Groves, a graduate student, chemistry professor Steven Boxer and electrical-engineering researcher Nick Ulman -- have created a synthetic cell membrane to serve as a layer between chip and cell. Minute electrical fields help the silicon chip adhere to one side of the membrane, while a living cell sticks to the other side of the membrane. 

"The synthetic membrane fools the living cell into thinking [it's touching] another living cell," said Dr. Boxer, who teaches at the university in Palo Alto, Calif. "So the living cell will go ahead and attach itself." 

The researchers, no fans of science fiction, say they can't yet see the day when a "Six Million Dollar Man" starts flexing bionic muscles. Indeed, they are far from perfecting the tools that would allow cells and chips to "speak" to each other across the synthetic membrane. 

But their work does offer some more-realistic possibilities. For example, doctors looking for leukemia cells in blood could pour a blood sample over a chip with a patchwork of membranes and get their answer within seconds, as the cells in the blood were automatically sorted and identified. Since millions of cells could fit on the surface of a thumbnail-size chip, theoretically, millions of tests could be done simultaneously. 

Other independent researchers who have reviewed the Stanford process speculate that it could allow drug companies to perform, say, 10,000 blood tests for AIDS in the time it currently takes to do one, measuring changes in light or electrical forces. Another idea is to test new drugs on living cells, with the chip assessing the drug's effects on the cells. 

News of the Stanford research, which will be detailed in an article appearing in the journal Science today, has excited some researchers who ponder the "man-machine interface." Wentai Liu, a professor of electrical engineering at North Carolina State University in Raleigh, N.C., has helped eye specialists at Johns Hopkins University create chips with light sensors that can help blind patients detect shades of light. When inserted in the eye near the optic nerve, his chips send an electric signal over the tiny space between nerve and chip. A direct link between cell and chip could make the communication more precise. 

Dr. Liu considers the cell-membrane research a significant step forward in the science of bionics, or putting humans and machines together. "It's an elegant solution that could prove useful in our work," Dr. Liu said. 

The Stanford team's techniques "certainly offer a more stable way of attaching chips with hard surfaces to gunky biological cells," said Eric Drexler, a researcher at Palo Alto-based Foresight Institute who has long sought ways to develop tools to manipulate molecules and atoms, a science calls "nanotechnology." 

The Stanford research project, which is Mr. Groves' doctoral thesis, took several years of trial-and-error and false starts. Mr. Groves is a 26-year-old graduate student who has to brush aside long brown hair to look into a microscope. He experimented with samples of lipid molecules, which are common to all living beings and are easily pulled out of cells. In his experiments, he repeatedly scratched deep cuts into samples of the membrane with tweezers, watching the way it flowed into structures resembling cell walls. 

The researchers then found that a synthetic cell membrane would attach itself to silicon chip surfaces -- but wouldn't stick to other materials used on chips, such as aluminum compounds. Thus, by controlling the patterns of aluminum compounds placed on the surface of a silicon chip, the researchers could dictate where the membranes' molecules gathered on the chip, even if the sections were each a hundred times smaller than a hair's width. 

The scientists envision chips with sensitivity to light or electrical charges that would analyze the cells attached to the membranes. For instance, a cell might trigger a change in electrical charge that a chip could detect; the chip would then communicate the exact location of the cell to a computer. Researchers could use such a chip to perform experiments on individual cells. 

Tools like these, says Dr. Drexler, the Foresight Institute researcher, will ultimately pay off in bionics applications, like replacing damaged neural connections with chips that conduct signals directly to the brain. Dr. Boxer, however, is more circumspect. 

"We're not interested in fantasy," he said. "We're practical and we try to make things work on the physical level, not the hypothetical."

-- Scientists have tried various methods to kill tumors by choking off their blood supplies. Now, researchers at Dallas' University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center are taking a novel tack: creating blood clots inside the tumor vessels. When given human tissue factor to stimulate clotting, 38% of the cancerous mice tested had complete tumor regression, according to the latest issue of Science. 

-- Western engineers have long praised Russian materials technology. To tap it, startup Synmatix Corp. in Southfield, Mich., is working with Los Alamos National Laboratory and three research labs in Russia to perfect a Russian process that yields superstrong metals. Squeezing metal powders with pressures of 400 tons per square inch breaks the particles into even finer pieces. When these ``nanopowders'' are formed into shapes, the parts are up to 10 times stronger than those made from ordinary metals and alloys.
