 Article 126 *******************    



Tools 

How one technology begat another; Important inventions of the past 130 years have built upon one another to form more and more innovations in our quickly developing world. 

351 words 
4 October 1999
Forbes ASAP 
23 
Volume ; Issue 
English
Copyright 1999 Forbes Inc. 

Every important technological invention of the last century has been itself at the nexus of numerous prior inventions, some great, some small. 

And it doesn't end there: These inventions become the grist for the next great creation. For example, in the most famous technological trajectory of the 20h century, the electromechanical switch led to the vacuum tube, which led to, among other things, the transistor, which begat the integrated circuit, which in turn evolved into the microprocessor, the most pervasive and influential product in the modern world. 

The proliferation of these technological inventions, and resulting links between them, uniquely characterizes this century, but it may not be true of the next. 

As the chart shows, the Era of Invention, roughly between 1870 and 1930, led to a brief era when the inventions were put to use, mostly for military purposes. Now we are in the Era of Improvement when core inventions have been refined to create, among other things, wonderful tools to entertain us, increase our productivity, and extend our lives. 

But even this Era of Improvement could slow. In the future, the harvest from core inventions may prove sparse, expensive, and hard to attain. Etching silicon wafers or devising fractional horsepower electric motors is going to look easy compared to storing information on the molecules in a crystal lattice or using a billion nanomachines to construct a human heart. 

But that doesn't mean the digital revolution is over. The real excitement these days lies not in the core technologies but how they are mixed and matched in systems. We not only won't run out of these new system inventions, but we may in fact be at the beginning of their golden age. Ahead lie dozens, even hundreds, of amazing new products born at the intersection of these systems. And that process will continue for generations-with any luck, until the next great technological explosion starts us off again. 

Document fb00000020011212dva400003


More Like This

 Article 127 *******************    



Ideas 

Eruptions from the Future; The next 50 years will raise enormous ethical questions. 

Alvin and Heidi Toffler 
1,450 words 
4 October 1999
Forbes ASAP 
185 
Volume 0; Issue 0 
English
Copyright 1999 Forbes Inc. 

A great deal of today's excitement over convergence is still what might be called "small think." As important as the convergence of computers, telephones, and television might be, or the convergence of, say, retailing and entertainment, or of agriculture and lasers, higher levels of convergence are potentially even more powerful. To reduce the concept to what's happening with computers and the media is to miss what could be the most profound consequences. 

Advances in computer power and communications are so dazzling today that they draw attention away from the tremendous advances under way in other fields of science and technology. The ability to regenerate human organs, once regarded as the wildest of science fantasies, is now within sight. Genetic manipulation of human evolution; the move toward nanotechnology; the reconceptualization of computing around DNA models; "artificial life"; the use of human brain waves to activate computers and vice versa; the possibility of storing information at the molecular level--all are making headlines. 

Of course, geneticists began using computers for number crunching and modeling many years ago. But it took nearly half a century for information technology and the biological revolution to cross the campus and start talking seriously to each other. Today the two fields are in a race toward a meeting of minds, suggesting that another tremendous burst of technology is about to occur. 

Following the digital revolution, according to Dr. Steven Metz, a researcher at the Strategic Studies Institute of the U.S. Army War College, will come "dramatically improved robotics, 'brilliant' machines capable of complex decision making, and nanotechnology in which the ability to manipulate and manufacture individual molecules allows the construction of tiny but complex machines. Eventually this may fuse with a concomitant biological revolution...leading to manufactured entities that are part machine, part living organism." This scenario is supported by advances in supramolecular chemistry, in which molecules are attracted to one another and self-assemble into groups or clusters with higher-level functions. 

In turn, such capabilities suggest that instead of an industrial economy built around manufacturing, the economy of the future may eventually center once more on cultivation--the growing not merely of food but of all sorts of "things" such as machines, organs, buildings, and computers. If this turns out to be true, we will have moved from agriculture to industry and on to what might be termed "hyperagriculture." Other technological convergences may prove equally formidable. 

There is, however, another, longer-term, higher level of convergence--the "metaconvergence" of technology with culture, including religion, epistemology, and the rest of intellectual life. 

THE HIGHER CONVERGENCE Technology has been fundamental to wealth creation for more than 2 million years. Hunters and gatherers used a wide variety of primitive tools, including knives, axes, and awls. Agrarian societies, relying on peasant labor, used hoes and sickles, querns for grinding corn, and many other agricultural, harvesting, and processing tools. 

In The Medieval Machine, Jean Gimpel describes the many devices used during the Middle Ages for "crushing olives, fulling cloth, tanning leather, making paper." These simple machines met with others that converted flowing water into a supplemental source of usable energy. Important as they were, however, these developments did not revolutionize the existing economies of their societies. 

A dramatically new system for creating wealth did not arise until centuries later. Then, a slow metaconvergence of technology with religious and cultural changes brought strikingly new ideas, new beliefs, new ways of thinking, new values, and new lifestyles to Europe. 

Consider the three-century interval between Johannes Gutenberg and Thomas Newcomen. About 1440, Gutenberg, a printer from Mainz, Germany, invented, apparently independently, a method of printing with movable type that was already in use in China. This new technology arrived when Germany and other parts of the Continent were seething with resentment against the gross corruption, cronyism, payoffs, and hypocrisy of the Catholic Church. By 1520 movable-type presses were mass-producing Protestant tracts, and by 1550 some 10,000 books promoting Protestantism were spreading across Europe. So influential was the new technology that some historians have claimed--with some exaggeration--"no books, no Reformation." But at the same time, the religious controversy created a market for printing that hastened the application of the new machines. 

Early in the 17th century, the reading public, which had grown considerably larger, was perusing Francis Bacon, who called for nothing less than an end to the Church-backed ideas of Aristotle that had dominated European culture. In time, Galileo, Descartes, Kepler, and Newton challenged Europe's deepest cultural assumptions and traditions. This gave birth to modern science, or "natural philosophy," with its emphases on mechanism, empiricism, quantification, deism, and, ultimately, secularism. 

In 1705, Newcomen introduced a precursor to the steam engine. As steam-powered machines and early factories spread, the physician-philosopher Julian Offray de La Mettrie applied Newtonian mechanistic thinking to the human body, by analogy describing it as nothing more than a complex machine. In doing so, he challenged the concept of an immortal soul, outraging his countrymen. 

New attitudes about time, space, logic, causation, and knowledge itself followed. Values changed, emphasizing more the here and now than the promised rewards of heaven. The most profound assumptions of the era were thus challenged by a host of convergences between technology and culture, religion, and intellectual thought. Together they added up to what we have described as "indust-reality," the reigning ideology of the emergent industrial civilization. 

In due course, this grand convergence of mutually stimulative changes--a metaconvergence--triggered explosive economic upheavals, forever changing Western ideas about how wealth is produced. Monarchies toppled; cities and nation-states grew in size and power. Population swelled as productivity increased. Mass media appeared, as did, later, mass education. The result, after bitter conflicts over everything from the philosophical to the physical and financial, was nothing less than the slow construction of an urban, industrial economy immensely more productive than the earlier ones. The industrial era had arrived. 

WHAT IS HUMAN? 

Today we are once again racing toward a metaconvergence--this time at superaccelerated speeds. Just as La Mettrie and other 18th-century philosophers used machine analogies to describe the world around them, thinkers today attempt to explain many things, from urban crises to mental illness, in terms of systems theory and analogies drawn from computers. In this way technological changes interact with changes in the intellectual life of society, which, in turn, affect values--which then feed back into the kinds of technology we are willing to finance, develop, and apply. 

Religion, too, forms part of this matrix. In contrast to earlier centuries, Catholicism and Protestantism today basically coexist in the West--but secularism is under widespread attack and religion, for good or ill, is resurgent. 

This resurgence will be fueled by the life-and-death moral issues raised by the genetics revolution. For example: Is eugenics morally acceptable? Is providing a financial reward morally appropriate for organ donors? If we cannot sell or "own" a human under the laws banning slavery, should we be permitted to sell or own parts of a human? If today's battles over race and abortion can arouse passions to the point of bombings and murder, what will happen when we can create humans with previously nonexistent physical characteristics? Is a person with three breasts, or a single, cyclopean eye, "human"? What about genetically altered brains and emotions? What is the legal definition of human? 

Public uncertainty over questions like these could provide a wide opening for Islam, Christianity, and other religions to seize the initiative and moderate or prevent "antihuman" uses of the new powers granted us by technology. But religion's history reminds us that belief--particularly over matters of life and death--all too easily mutates into fanaticism. It is possible that as biological and convergent technological advances slam into society, internal differences over theology and politics might shatter existing churches and spawn new sects and cults around the world, fragmenting once-monolithic belief systems as the Reformation did in its time. 

In short, technologies may converge with one another, but they also converge with elements of society and culture in ways that produce not simply profit for this or that company, or the restructuring of industries and economies, but, over time, the restructuring and convergence of whole civilizations. 

And all this is heading our way. Welcome to the 21st century! 

Alvin and Heidi Toffler's book, Future Shock, published in 1970, set the tone for how we think about technology's impact on society. Their most recent book, War and Anti-War, was published in 1998.

 Article 128 *******************    



Disciplines 

All sciences merge into three; All of the world's scientific disciplines have influenced what Forbes ASAP has identified as the three topic areas that will help us understand all things great and small-M Theory, genetic engineering, and the new earth science. 

415 words 
4 October 1999
Forbes ASAP 
207 
Volume ; Issue 
English
Copyright 1999 Forbes Inc. 

With understanding comes complexity, then order, then finally simplicity. 

Three thousand years ago, at the birth of Western science, the Greek philosopher-scientists saw the universe with elegant simplicity. It was composed of fire, water, earth, and air--or, in the prescient prediction of Democritus, invisible infinitesimals called atoms. 

Aristotle changed all of that. Wherever he looked, he saw Platonic ideals but complex, messy reality. And so he set out to codify and characterize the material world in all of its diversity. In the Renaissance, a new way of looking at nature emerged, combining observation, hypothesis, and experimentation. With this scientific method in hand, scientists were no longer merely list makers and recorders but creators. As the chart shows, this experimental mental method proved unbeatable. By the 19th century, major discoveries were coming by the decade, almost every one spinning out a new scientific discipline. 

Now, at the end of the 20th century, we seem to be heading back from whence we came-back to elegant simplicity. Our multitude of separate sciences are converging into three: M-theory, and what we are calling Biological Engineering and the New Earth Science. 

At universities all around the world, some of the best minds are studying the interaction among living things and the systems and structures that support them. Scientists are drawing upon such diverse disciplines as oceanography, chemistry, and zoology in their quest to study and understand this New Earth Science. 

Over on the other side of campus, computer scientists, biochemists, and biologists are working at the very frontiers of Biological Engineering. Already, we can create and manipulate living things through genetic engineering. On the horizon, man and machine will converge as we explore the fields of nanotechnology, cybernetics, and artificial intelligence. 

With M-theory, still other scientists are attempting to explain the universe--from its tiniest quarks to its biggest black holes--in one complete unified theory. The universe, they believe, is composed of membranes that look a lot like those found in living cells and that exist in numerous dimensions. No doubt, those early Greek philosophers would have admired the elegance of this model--the long--delayed meeting between Plato and Aristotle, even if it took 11 different dimensions to occur.

 Article 130 *******************    



Developments to Watch 

PLOTTING THE FINEST LINES ON COMPUTER CHIPS 

EDITED BY ELLEN LICKING 
268 words 
1 November 1999
Business Week 
84 
Number 3653 
English
(c) 1999 McGraw-Hill, Inc.

Researchers at Northwestern University announced in the Oct. 15 issue of Science that they have invented the world's smallest and most accurate plotter. Their so-called nanoplotter is capable of drawing minuscule lines--each is only about 30 molecules wide--with such precision that only 200 billionths of an inch separates them. 

If the technology can be commercialized, it might be used to pack more processing power onto a computer chip, boosting speed and performance. It could also lead to diagnostic devices that can load thousands of medical sensors onto an area smaller than the head of a pin. 

The Northwestern group, led by Chad A. Mirkin, built their tiny plotter from a common laboratory instrument called an atomic force microscope. Typically, such a scope is used to map the surfaces of molecules, but Mirkin turned it into a writing instrument by coating its tip with molecular ink. When the tip comes in contact with a thin sheet of gold, molecules of the ink are transferred to the gold's surface. 

Using the technique, the researchers were able to draw the university's initials in fine lines one molecule high. They further improved on the technology by placing multiple inks--different kinds of molecules--side by side with such accuracy that the chemical identity of each line was maintained. Mirkin's plotter is also fast. In lab tests, it took the nanoplotter just 10 minutes to write about 100 words in molecules. That gives new meaning to the power of the written word.

 Article 131 *******************    



LEISURE & ARTS 
Bookshelf 

This is Really Something --- By Jim Holt 

1,033 words 
10 November 1999
The Wall Street Journal 
A20 
English
(Copyright (c) 1999, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.)

Zero is a funny number. In fact, some people appear to be unsure about whether it is a number at all. Last year in Centre County, Pa., the county commissioners voted to abolish an unpopular tax on occupations by evaluating the tax rate officially at zero. Since this left the local school boards painfully short of income, they sued the county, arguing that zero was not a value. To substantiate the claim, their attorney had a former county assessor try to divide by zero on his pocket calculator. It displayed an E for "error." 

How this case was resolved I don't know. But it is not the first time that zero has been a source of confusion. Unlike the counting numbers (1,2,3 . . . ), which have the solidity of God-given things, zero seems strangely ambiguous, not to say paradoxical. Is it the absence of number or the number for absence? If it stands for nothing, then how can it be treated as a something? 

You may dismiss such difficulties as idle and metaphysical, yet they have had great practical consequences for Western civilization, as Robert Kaplan shows "The Nothing That Is: The Natural History of Zero." To the Greeks and Romans, the very idea of zero was inconceivable. They did not have a symbol for it in their number systems. Thus they could not make use of convenient "positional" notation, in which, for example, 307 stands for three hundreds, no tens, and seven ones. And that was not good for ancient science, let alone for everyday arithmetic. Just try adding or multiplying with Roman numerals -- it's fiendishly hard. 

So where did the familiar hollow circle that we use to denote zero come from? That's a story fraught with mystery, and Mr. Kaplan tells it well, blending rival historical accounts with his own conjectures. (He speculates, for instance, that the form of zero is derived from the impression left by a counting chip in the sand.) 

Hindu mathematicians were evidently the first to use the modern symbol for zero. Their notation was transmitted westward during the Middle Ages by Arab scholars. On its arrival in Europe, zero began to take on greater reality, evolving from a sort of punctuation mark within numerals into a freestanding number in its own right. Oddly enough, the rise of commerce had something to do with this. When double-entry bookkeeping was invented in Italy around 1340, zero came to be viewed as a natural dividing point between positive numbers (credits) and negative numbers (debits). 

Whether "discovered" or "invented," zero was a number to be reckoned with. Philosophical doubts about its nature gave way to virtuoso calculations by great mathematical figures like Fibonacci and Fermat. Zero proved to be the key to solving algebraic equations: If the equation could be put in the form ab=0, then it must follow that either a=0 or b=0. A closely related abstraction, that of the infinitely small -- "almost zero" -- enabled Newton to invent the calculus, which became the mathematical engine of the Scientific Revolution. 

Mr. Kaplan, a popularizer of mathematics who has taught at Harvard, is an erudite and often witty writer, but he can make you wince with his occasional patches of purple prose. ("We had put out a bowl of milk for the little Robin Goodfellow of zero and here he is grown burly. Is he about to reveal himself as the bat-winged destroyer of our reason?") His concluding reflections on nothingness, moreover, are marred by a good deal of guff: Zero is "a shadow in the slanting light of fear," God is zero, America is the land of zero and so on. I am glad it did not occur to him that in tennis the word for zero is "love." 

If the story of zero has a moral, it is that a new symbol -- a mere mark on paper -- can sometimes push our thoughts in new directions, lead to sweeping new discoveries. In this century, another such symbol was invented by the physicist Richard Feynman (1918-88). "Feynman diagrams," a shorthand way of representing how electrons and photons interact, enabled their creator to work out the clearest version of quantum electrodynamics (which happens to be the most accurate theory in all of physics). They also, by virtue of their intuitive simplicity and power, have become a universal tool for physicists trying to understand the world of elementary particles. 

Feynman was an endlessly fascinating character. Since his death he has been the subject of at least three biographies, not to mention a movie in which he was played by Matthew Broderick. Now, with "The Pleasure of Finding Things Out: The Best Short Works of Richard Feynman" joining all the other volumes by and about him, it would appear that practically every thought he ever had is in print. 

Fortunately, Feynman seemed incapable of having a thought that wasn't fresh and original. This sparkling collection includes his minority report on the space shuttle Challenger (remember him dunking the O-ring in a glass of ice water at the televised press conference to show what went wrong?); unpretentious, thinking-aloud pieces on the value of science and its relation to religion; and his famous 1959 talk, "There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom," which, by detailing ingenious ways of building microscopic computers and factories, helped launch today's nanotechnology revolution. 

There are also lots of entertaining reminiscences, my favorite being Feynman's account of giving his first formal lecture. He was only a graduate student, and his audience included Albert Einstein, Wolfgang Pauli and many other daunting figures, so he had reason to be petrified. But he managed to present his theory anyway. Then, recalls Feynman: "Professor Pauli stood up -- he was sitting next to Professor Einstein. He said, `I do not think this theory can be right because of this and this and that and the other thing and so forth, don't you agree, Professor Einstein?' Einstein said, `No-o-o-o,' and that was the nicest no I ever heard." 

(See related letter: "Go Figure" -- WSJ Nov. 18, 1999)

Books 

THE POLLYANNA PROPHECIES 

BY MICHAEL J. MANDEL 
1,239 words 
22 November 1999
Business Week 
26 
Number 3656 
English
(c) 1999 McGraw-Hill, Inc.

THE LONG BOOM A Vision for the Coming Age of Prosperity By Peter Schwartz, Peter Leyden, and Joel Hyatt Perseus Books 336pp $26 

Any period of sustained growth brings forth the utopian visionaries. The boom of the 1960s, for example, saw the U.S. ready to declare a war on poverty. 

This time around, utopia looks a lot like a global version of Silicon Valley--at least to the authors of The Long Boom. Elaborating on a widely quoted 1997 Wired magazine article, Peter Schwartz, Peter Leyden, and Joel Hyatt--a consultant, a journalist, and an entrepreneur, all based in the San Francisco Bay area--have produced a book that describes a modern-day paradise on earth. The Long Boom, write the authors, ``is a vision of...a better, more prosperous and peaceful world.'' The key is rapid global growth that continues for decades: ``High economic growth solves the core issues facing the world today.'' 

The book, according to the authors, is ``not a prediction.'' Rather, it is an exhortatory tract. Their hope is that by laying out a positive vision of the future, they will increase the chance that it will happen. 

From this perspective, The Long Boom is a welcome relief from the pessimism of conventional economics. Indeed, the volume's target of 4% to 6% global growth for at least the next 20 years is well within the realm of possibility. Annual global growth during the second half of the 20th century averaged about 3%, and the Internet and related technologies could add a percentage point, as BUSINESS WEEK wrote in a recent cover story, ``The Internet Age'' (BW--Oct. 4). Breakthrough innovations such as nanotechnology--the engineering of individual atoms--could boost world growth even more. 

But even a shining vision needs analysis and some hint that the authors have thought about what might go wrong. That is what is missing from this book. The Long Boom simply assumes that everything will go well for the next 20 years. Massive technological, economic, and political problems are written off with a sentence, if that. As a result, The Long Boom comes across as an overly cheerful sermon rather than a compelling blueprint. 

Take the economy. The word ``recession'' is not mentioned in the index. Instead, economic downturns are viewed as resulting mainly from a lack of sufficient business and consumer confidence, to be best solved by more upbeat thinking. Solvable problems turn into a crisis because of ``the anxiety that has mushroomed around them,'' write the authors. If the global community is ``edging toward a state of depression''--in both the emotional and economic senses--it's ``largely because of the lack of a positive vision.'' 

But Schwartz, Leyden, and Hyatt fail to acknowledge that periods of great technological change are often highly volatile, as workers and corporations lose their old nichesign has gotten funkier over the past few years as engineers try to achieve that elusive golden posture. One of the most popular ergonomic keyboards--Microsoft's Keyboard Elite-- helps somewhat, Johnson says, by giving you about four more inches of workspace. But the design pushes the mouse farther away than it should be, so she doesn't recommend the gadget, which costs about $50 per keyboard. 

Instead, Johnson suggests looking at either the contoured keyboard, which is made by Kinesis Corp., or one from obvious to them, but it's not clear how networks can stop an overhyped stock market from collapsing. 

Problems that have bedeviled smart and dedicated people for decades are summarily dismissed. Take the matter of economically underdeveloped countries. ``The old model was to let every country slowly make its way up the technology food chain.'' Their solution? ``We need to transfer know-how and technology''--as if the importation of advanced technologies into developing countries had never been tried before. 

For the most part, the book lacks historical perspective--unless you count the authors' use of ``future history.'' For much of the book, they write as if they were looking back from the 21st century, giving their arguments an undeserved aura of certainty. This conceit may confound serious readers, for it produces a bizarre blend of real and imaginary companies in the index. For example, New York Times is followed there by a listing for Nippon Nano, a fictitious Japanese nanotechnology giant supposedly operating in the middle of the next century. 

The authors' faith in the inevitable progress of technology is touching. Their vision of worldwide rapid growth requires a new source of nonpolluting energy to substitute for fossil fuels. Not to worry, they say: Most of the environmentally sound technologies needed ``are already here, already fully developed, and getting competitively priced.'' 

Well, maybe. Their choice for the energy technology of the Long Boom is the fuel cell, which turns hydrogen and oxygen into water and power. That's certainly a clean technology, and it's potentially quite efficient. There's a catch, though. As the authors admit, another source of power is needed to generate the hydrogen in the first place--and for the foreseeable future, that means the current power plants will keep running. 

Similarly, the authors argue that the average lifespan can be extended to age 120, but they decline to deal with any of the negative implications. What if scientists cannot cure Alzheimer's, so that we have a nation of barely functional centenarians? Or conversely, what if people keep working well into their second century, so that older generations end up much wealthier than younger ones? It would have been nice to hear a bit about how the authors would solve these problems. Instead, they devote a whole chapter to what they call ``wild science''--things like warp drives that can drive spacecraft faster than light. 

Their self-congratulatory naivete shows up most clearly in their vision of future politics--which follows the apolitical Silicon Valley model. ``A different political mentality seems to be emerging on the West Coast,'' they write. ``It's not about left or right; it's about what works.'' 

But this model is based on the assumption that everyone across the globe shares the same values. In a world populated by nationalist Serbs, religious fundamentalists, and radical environmentalists, one cannot assume a future with globally shared values. 

Indeed, though the authors stress how, in their research, they crisscrossed the world, their parochialism is breathtaking. ``The Americans have figured out an economic model that works marvelously,'' they write. ``The world needs to open up just enough to see that taking some new ideas and developing them further is in its best interests.'' 

The book also includes some jarring examples of national stereotyping. The French and the southern European cultures are ``romantics.'' Asians are ``fantastic at absorbing new methods and mastering set courses.'' The Japanese ``resist change at all costs for as long as possible, and then adopt new ideas wholesale.'' And the poor Russians have ``a remarkable capacity for suffering.'' 

Ultimately, The Long Boom is based on a pervasive sense that Silicon Valley today is the most important place in history. As the authors have one of their imaginary future characters say: ``The future is being born right around me, and for that I am ecstatic.'' That sentiment may sound appropriate in a religious text--but it's not a good road map for the future. 

Mandel covers the New Economy from New York.

Departments: Digital Rules 

Fat, Florid, Farcical 

BY Rich Karlgaard 
702 words 
29 November 1999
Forbes 
051 
English
(c) 1999 Forbes Inc.

such was the 207-page "finding of fact" delivered by Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson on November 5. Like author, like paper, we say. The jowly judge found Microsoft to be a monopoly. Okay--by definition any company with an advantage in the marketplace must do something you don't and therefore is a monopolist. Next. He called Microsoft "predatory." Well, yes, the Gates gang is a hardball competitor with a tin ear for niceties. Let's go on. He said Microsoft hurt consumers and slowed innovation. That's pure baloney! 

Judge Jackson--who writes in longhand and discovered PCs about 30 seconds ago--says Microsoft has been inquisitioning innovation and kicking customers in the groin since it acquired its monopoly status around 1990. All this assumes the bulbous barrister would recognize computer innovation if it fell on his head. Right. Look at the facts. Since 1990: 

-- Tim Berners-Lee completed his work on the World Wide Web. 

-- The Web browser was invented at the University of Illinois. 

-- Marc Andreessen and Jim Clark founded Netscape to commercialize the browser. 

-- Jeff Bezos started Amazon and launched the e-commerce revolution. 

-- Venture capital thrown at information technology startups rose fortyfold from $2 billion a year to $80 billion a year, when you include corporate VCs and angels. 

-- The Palm Pilot reached a million customers faster than any other computing device in history. 

-- Palm licensed its operating system to Nokia and Handspring, cutting Microsoft Windows CE off at the knees. 

-- Sun Microsystems invented the Java language and started a mass exodus of computer programming talent away from Microsoft toward Sun. The exodus continues. 

-- Sun won the Internet server battle and checked progress of Microsoft Windows NT in its tracks. 

-- AOL grew faster than Microsoft. 

-- AOL's stock rose faster than Microsoft's. 

-- AOL's home page kicked MSN nine ways till Sunday. 

-- So did Yahoo's. 

-- A Finnish entrepreneur tunneled under the Windows monopoly to create Linux. 

-- Linux acquired 12 million customers, all at the leading edge of Web-centric computing. 

-- Red Hat, a Linux support company, went public and attained a multibillion-dollar market cap. 

-- ASPs (application service providers), an idea developed with a goal to cripple Microsoft, became, this year, the hottest funded category of software. 

-- Steve Jobs returned to Apple and made it once again the fastest growing major PC company. 

Yeah, Microsoft has sure killed innovation during its decade of monopoly dominance. 

INFINITE AND ZERO, II 

Ten years ago Tom Wolfe wrote a memorable essay for Harper's called "Stalking the Billion-Footed Beast." It sought to explain why so few writers were attempting the Great American Novel. Wolfe blamed it on reality. Late-20th-century America had become such a rowdy, hog-stomping carnival that it swamped even the best novelist's imagination. 

Well, ditto the futurist's. A couple of months ago we noted a Science article about how scientists at UCLA and Hewlett-Packard had successfully tricked molecules into behaving as memory bits (see Digital Rules, Aug. 23). While the work was early and crude--the researchers had merely demonstrated "read-only" memory thus far--it nevertheless predicted the dawn of a new era of molecular computing. Coming soon: chips the size of a white blood cell and whole computers no bigger than a speck of dust. Now comes a winter issue of Science. The crunchy problem of "read-write" molecular gates will take many years to solve, right? Wrong. "We just did it," say researchers at Yale and Rice Universities. Ah, but circuits, they're always the showstopper, unshrinkable past a point. Wrong again. Think nanotubes of atoms, coated in titanium, a billionth of a meter thick, say the Yale and Rice teams. 

No joke--computation is racing toward infinite power at zero cost. Faster than we ever thought. Moore's Law will skip past silicon, giving the finger as it goes. A single molecular chip will rival the human brain by 2020, all of New York City's brains by 2040, and the world's by 2050.

 Article 134 *******************    



The Wall Street Journal Millennium (A Special Report): Industry & Economics --- Think Small: Imagine changing a chair into a table at the flick of a switch; Welcome to nanotechnology

By Timothy Aeppel 
1,781 words 
31 December 1999
The Wall Street Journal 
R40 
English
(Copyright (c) 1999, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.)

Call it the lure of the Lilliputian. 

When it comes to making things, smaller is usually better. Think of cell phones or heart defibrillators. Smaller things are simply more useful and cheaper, usually. Even big things benefit from miniaturization; consider the electronics inside sport-utility vehicles. 

But gazing into the next century, smallness is broaching a frontier that makes the tiniest dimensions used in today's factories seem like football fields or rows of Greyhound buses. The terminology is fuzzy, like the emerging science it describes, but those studying it often lump it under the banner of "nanotechnology." A nanometer is a unit of measure equal to a billionth of a meter -- the level at which individual atoms can be recognized. 

Viewed broadly, nanotechnology is mankind's effort to measure and fiddle with things at this minuscule level, and one obvious place to begin using such capabilities is computers. The smallest memory devices today are still designed and built at dimensions far above the nano-level, and some experts warn that this technology is nearing its limits. However, building things like a wristwatch-size device that can carry an entire Library of Congress -- one of the totems of computer visionaries -- will require new breakthroughs in miniaturization. Nanotechnology might be the path. 

Fine, you're thinking, but what does this have to do with making SUVs? 

You'd be surprised. There are serious scientists and engineers suggesting that through nanotechnology, we could eventually, sometime in the next century, turn our production philosophy on its head. Rather than hacking our much-desired little things out of bigger lumps of materials like steel or silicon, we could simply build them from the ground up, one atom at a time. 

"Nanotechnology is where microtechnology was 15 or 20 years ago," says Robert M. Mehalso, president of Microtec Associates, a Rochester, N.Y., technology consulting firm. "In other words, it's still in its infancy." 

The most extreme view of nanotechnology opens startling possibilities. Manufacturing would no longer consist of cobbling together things from component parts like spark plugs and sheets of steel. Instead, you'd create factories at the molecular level able to churn out virtually any product desired from materials ubiquitous in the atmosphere, like dirt and water. Proponents of this view, often associated with the author K. Eric Drexler, envision minuscule robots called assemblers that could build almost anything, including other assemblers. 

"When people really get the concept, they look at matter in a different light," says Christine Peterson, executive director of the Foresight Institute, a Palo Alto, Calif. think tank set up by Mr. Drexler to study nanotechnology. 

Far from being the solid stuff of everyday life, matter in the world of nanotechnology becomes dynamic. Ms. Peterson says nanotechnology might make it possible, for instance, to build things like a chair with a switch that enabled it to change shape into a table. It may sound like science fiction, but if you can arrange atoms one way, you should be able to shift them into other structures. Not only would the chair be able to change shape, it would do so extremely fast. And it wouldn't have to melt into a lump of muck first; it would go straight from one to the other. 

Such a world would need factories only for big items and other commodities. For smaller goods, each home might have its own black box -- able to download computer instructions that would enable it to produce any product desired by the owner. Pollution from factories and poverty would vanish, and the products themselves would be perfect in every way. Of course, the same technology also could be used to build horrible weapons; that's one reason that proponents say we should think about the implications of nanotechnology before the capabilities start to emerge. 

Provocative visions like these have led to something of a rift within the scientific community over nanotechnology. Many universities are doing research in the area, as are big companies like International Business Machines Corp. The Pentagon, too. There's obviously huge potential for such science to revolutionize the way we live and work. But there's a danger of letting our imaginations run away with us, warns Harold Craighead, a professor of applied and engineering physics at Cornell University, who also heads the school's Nanobiotechnology Center. 

"If people get the expectation that [science-fiction-like] things are achievable in the near term, they'll end up disappointed," he says. Moreover, some of the wilder notions may never be achieved. There are too many questions still hovering over the field, he says, such as how you power and control nanosized devices. 

What's needed is a great deal of old-fashioned scientific research. But Mr. Craighead wryly notes that it's often the flashier stuff that gets attention, even at Cornell. A few years ago, for instance, several of his students caused a stir by crafting a tiny guitar out of silicon; each of its six strings were no more than 100 atoms wide. The entire musical instrument would fit inside one human blood cell. Of course, though the strings could be made to resonate, no musician could hold it or play it. 

"The fact that it's shaped like a guitar means nothing for making devices that are useful at that size," he says. But it does serve a purpose: For people who don't know about this field, he adds, it shows that we can make extremely tiny, recognizable things. Cornell researchers more recently made a harp using the same technique. 

So how far away are the practical breakthroughs? Ralph Merkle anticipates "intermediate capabilities that will give us some commercial applications" of nanotechnology within a decade. A computer expert and engineer, Mr. Merkle previously studied nanotechnology at Xerox Corp.'s Palo Alto Research Center in California. But he recently took a job at Zyvex LLC, a start-up in Richardson, Texas, that is vowing to commercialize a system for building things from atoms. 

"There are those who say 10 years is a hugely ambitious goal, but we're going to give it a shot," he says. 

For most of the scientific community, the goals are more modest. That's certainly true for T. Ross Kelly, a professor at Boston College. Because nanotechnology has yet to form an identity of its own as a separate science, research is being done by specialists in a variety of disciplines -- physics, engineering, biology and, as in the case of Mr. Kelly, organic chemistry. 

Mr. Kelly normally works on things like new antibiotics. But motors fascinate him. His office on the outskirts of Boston is lined with a collection of strange devices he has collected over the years that seem to defy physical laws, such as a "memory wire" that automatically straightens out -- no matter how much it's twisted -- when it's placed into a cup of water. About five years ago, he started trying to make a chemically powered, molecular motor. "Not for any applied reason," he says, "but because I think it's cool." 

He finally succeeded, creating a motor consisting of just 78 atoms. It caused a stir among nanotechnology enthusiasts and was written up in the magazine Nature. It looks a bit like a waterwheel, but it only pivots 120 degrees before grinding to a halt. It has no known use, but that didn't stop people from calling to ask if his device could someday power a robotic assembler. 

His response: Only after a great deal more is learned about how to control and direct such a tiny motor. 

His next goal is to create a motor that can turn in full circles and operate continuously. Mr. Kelly thinks it's possible, but cautions that it's likely to "take years." 

Part of the problem for nanotechnology is its image as something of a fringe science. The science has spawned a following among futurists who have little scientific background and has even become a niche within science-fiction novels. A recent episode of "The X-Files" hinged on the idea of nano-devices somehow making their way into the blood of one of the regular characters, allowing a villain to control the person's health with a hand-held device about the size of a pocket calculator. 

Nanotechnology will certainly revolutionize some things. For instance, building things from atoms could yield new materials that are many times stronger or far lighter than anything known today. However, some processes may simply make more sense to do the old-fashioned way. It may be possible to make an oak shelf one atom at a time, for instance, but we may decide we already have the best possible way to create such a product. It's called a tree. 

There's also the economics. We've come to accept the notion that as things shrink, they grow cheaper. But at some point, that may no longer be the case. Even nanotechnology enthusiasts admit that without certain breakthroughs that allow atomic assembly to occur easily and quickly, it could be extremely costly to make things at the atomic scale. 

And finally, there's the question of whether it's desirable. A technology that produces almost everything at a nominal cost raises interesting questions for a society structured as it is at the end of the 20th century. 

For instance, most traditional jobs would disappear. Howard Baetjer Jr., an economist at Towson University in Maryland, has pondered such things and says it shouldn't be much different from the shift away from agriculture that occurred earlier in mankind's development. Once, most people in the world labored in the fields, but only a tiny fraction do today. 

"What are all those people doing?" says Mr. Baetjer. "They're manicuring ladies' toes at boutiques in New York, they're producing Sega computer toys, and producing magazines that appeal to tastes and interests that nobody could have imagined some time ago." 

In other words, our economy is a fluid thing that will open doors few people can imagine viewed from the limited perspective of the present. Who knows? Maybe the advent of plentiful and perfect products will revive interest in handmade items. It's conceivable the ultimate sign of prosperity in a nanotechnological future will be eating off dinner plates you made yourself, with lopsided handles and fingerprints visible in the glazed surfaces. 

--- 

Timothy Aeppel is a staff reporter in The Wall Street Journal's Pittsburgh bureau.

Politics & Policy 

Clinton Plans Major Initiative on Scientific Research 

By John D. McKinnon 
Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal 
1,310 words 
31 December 1999
The Wall Street Journal 
A12 
English
(Copyright (c) 1999, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.)

WASHINGTON -- President Clinton plans to launch a major initiative to promote scientific research, hoping to cement his administration's legacy of federal help in fostering the New Economy. 

The initiative will include proposed funding boosts for basic research in several areas, including information technology, health care and supercomputers. 

The proposal, to be unveiled with the president's 2001 budget request in February, could give the administration's top technophile, Vice President Al Gore, something to crow about at a time when his prospects in the presidential race remain uncomfortably uncertain. 

Mr. Gore "has got the emerging champion in him, especially in medical research and the Internet, two issues he's passionate about," says Ray Merenstein, vice president of Research! America, an advocacy group for health-care science based in Alexandria, Va. If the administration comes out with a big funding proposal, "he can say, 'I fought for that,' and make himself look good." 

Indeed, even some Republicans and other critics of Mr. Gore tacitly concede that boosting government science funding is a big potential plus for him. Congress last year agreed to fund an administration high-tech budget proposal but pointedly changed the program's name because it had become closely identified with the vice president, some observers say. 

The new tech package will be one of a half-dozen major initiatives to be included in the White House's budget blueprint for fiscal year 2001, according to people familiar with internal administration deliberations. 

The administration's proposal will reflect a little-noticed but growing consensus among lawmakers, policy officials and others about the proper role for Washington to play in scientific research. Early in the Clinton administration, the White House tried to promote practical applications of technological breakthroughs, through the Department of Commerce and other agencies. 

Many Republican resisted the effort, worried about getting the government so directly involved in bringing new technologies to market. And when the economy improved and private industry began spending more on research and development, the urge eased for government involvement. 

"The debate has swung toward basic research as the place for the federal government to have the best impact," says Kei Koizumi, director of R&D budget and policy for the American Association for the Advancement of Science. With the technology-fueled boom of the late 1990s, "the focus is on ensuring that this continues with future technologies." 

The new White House effort will increase funding on a broad range of recent federal efforts in scientific research. It also will propose to expand work in cutting-edge areas such as supercomputers and nanotechnology, which involves tiny systems such as engineered cells. 

In a speech earlier this month, President Clinton appeared to lay the groundwork for the initiative, crediting technology with much of the economy's stellar performance and calling for more public investment. "If we want our current prosperity to continue into the 21st century, we must . . . clearly continue to encourage the creation and the spread of new technologies in our own economy," he declared. 

Earlier this year, a presidential advisory panel warned that the U.S. is "gravely underinvesting" in basic long-range information-technology research, such as supercomputers for modeling weather systems and ultrafast software. The panel recommended roughly doubling over the next five years the $1.3 billion that the government spent on information-technology research last year. 

When the panel studied research and development data, members concluded that applied research was being funded reasonably well, thanks to rapid expansion of industry R&D. Industry research increased by 11% from 1998 to 1999 alone, by one measure, while federal research rose by only 1%. In fact, total U.S. R&D could rise to a record share of the economy next year, surpassing the 1964 high mark of 2.87%. 

But industry R&D -- especially in computer-related fields -- tends to focus on short-term, applied concepts, driven by competitive pressures. Considering both government and private-sector research, the panel concluded that fundamental scientific research "had maybe even been going down a bit relative to overall [information technology] budgets," explains Irving Wladawsky-Berger, general manager of International Business Machines Corp.'s Internet Division in Somers, N.Y., and co-chairman of the President's Information Technology Advisory Committee. 

Administration officials refused to release exact numbers for the administration's 2001 information-technology proposal, but "it will be forward looking and meet expectations," says Henry Kelly, assistant director for technology at the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy. Members of the advisory panel "will be pleased," he added. 

The panel recommended a $472 million spending increase for this fiscal year ending Sept. 30, 2000, while the White House asked for a $366 million increase and Congress approved a $235 million increase. For next fiscal year, the advisory panel had recommended a $733 million increase over the 1999 level. 

Mr. Gore's office and campaign didn't respond to requests for comment on the budget proposal or his role in shaping it. 

Much of the recent funding effort has focused on what Mr. Kelly calls the "hideously complex" problems posed by future generations of supercomputers. "Instead of having one or two processors, you're talking about thousands or millions of chips," Mr. Kelly explains. "They have to be able to operate together and share memory." 

The new darling of this year's administration research initiative may prove to be so-called nanotechnology, involving manipulation of extremely small systems -- such as cells -- for some kinds of production, and even computing. Practical applications of this would include the design and manufacture of pharmaceuticals, for example. 

When officials gave White House Budget Director Jack Lew a briefing book earlier this year on R&D funding proposals, they highlighted the nanotechnology sections by putting tiny print on the cover and giving Mr. Lew a large magnifying glass to help him read it. 

"There's been huge excitement in the last few years" over nanotechnology, Mr. Kelly adds. "We're trying to make sure that's adequately reflected in our budget." 

Other specifics reportedly under consideration include plans for biotechnology; energy-saving cars, trucks and other materials, a favorite of the vice president; and health-care research. While campaigning, Mr. Gore has proposed significantly boosting cancer research through human-genome research. 

Another likely theme of the research-funding proposal: an attempt to present a research budget that provides more balance than recent spending plans have between different areas of science. In recent years, Congress has favored health research at the expense of some other high-tech categories that the administration prefers. Budgets have risen sharply for the National Institutes of Health, for example, while some administration officials feel that physics and computer science have deserved more. 

In his speech this month, Mr. Clinton said he wanted to "make this point very strongly . . . that we have a balanced research portfolio." He added, "advances in health care, for example, are often dependent on breakthroughs in other disciplines such as the physics needed for medical imaging technology." 

But the challenge of focusing research money is especially hard at a time when tight spending caps set during the era of budget deficits continue to limit federal spending. "We've made a big effort to not miss any important bets, and it's a painful process as you can imagine," says Mr. Kelly. 


---

 Article 136 *******************    



Health & Science: Clinton to Propose More Funding for Research 

By Glenn Burkins 
Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal 
685 words 
24 January 2000
The Wall Street Journal 
B14 
English
(Copyright (c) 2000, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

PASADENA, Calif. -- President Clinton this week will ask Congress to approve a nearly $3 billion spending increase to boost science and technology research -- money he says is essential for the U.S. to maintain its technological edge. 

The president announced his proposal Friday at the California Institute of Technology, one of the nation's premier research schools, and is expected to mention the technology initiative during his annual State of the Union address Thursday. 

Playing to a packed crowd in a Caltech auditorium, Mr. Clinton said the $2.8 billion in new spending would include a $1 billion increase in biomedical research for the National Institutes of Health and a $675 million increase for the National Science Foundation. Additional funding also would go to expand research in areas ranging from information technology to space exploration to the development of cleaner sources of energy. 

Several of the steps echo campaign proposals made by Vice President Al Gore, who long has been identified with the administration's efforts to promote research and technology. 

If approved by Congress, the new money would be designated for fiscal year 2001, which begins Oct. 1. 

Neal Lane, President Clinton's top science adviser, said the additional funding would go a long way toward pushing the nation toward the administration's goal of having government and private enterprise together invest 3% of the country's gross domestic product on research and development. He said that number is approaching 2.9%, up from 2.6% when the president was elected. 

Mr. Lane predicts strong bipartisan support for the administration's proposal, even as conservative Republicans call for tax cuts and less federal spending. The government spends about $40 billion a year to assist R&D, mainly through a collection of agencies and programs called the 21st Century Research Fund. 

"It's so clear from every study, every kind of data, economic analysis, that the federal investment in science and technology is about as good an investment as you can possibly make with the American taxpayers' money," Mr. Lane said. "So I think if there is any area of government where you can expect the two parties to come together, this is it." 

A Friday letter to the president from House Science Committee Chairman F. James Sensenbrenner Jr. (R., Wis.) reflected the less-partisan atmosphere surrounding scientific issues. Mr. Sensenbrenner commended the White House for its "new emphasis on basic over applied research." He also said that in the recently adopted 2000 budget, "cooperation between Congress and the administration yielded many significant victories" in science funding. However, Mr. Sensenbrenner also warned the president against "budgetary game-playing" that he said had weakened some previous White House proposals by tying research increases to taxes Mr. Clinton wanted. 

Included in the proposed $2.8 billion increase is $497 million for a national nanotechnology initiative, an attempt to make machines and computers as small as molecules. Mr. Clinton said such research could lead to computers capable of storing the contents of the Library of Congress in a device the size of a sugar cube, or to the development of material 10 times stronger than steel but at a fraction of the weight. 

The proposed $1 billion increase for the National Institutes of Health would go to support research in areas such as diabetes, brain disorders, cancer and the development of an AIDS vaccine. The center's current budget is about $18 billion. 

The National Science Foundation would see the largest increase in its history, up 17% from its current $3.9 billion budget. 

In the runup to the administration's budget release next month, officials also confirmed plans to close the so-called "digital divide" by providing subsidies to help low-income families buy computers and get Internet access. 

In his Saturday radio address, Mr. Clinton proposed creating teams of Medicare-fraud fighters across the country as well as investing in new technologies to track abuse in the program. 

--- 

John D. McKinnon contributed to this article.

 Article 137 *******************    


Special Section/The Capitalist Century 

Inventing Tomorrow Today Sure, the future will be different. But who knows exactly how? These five young innovators have a clue. 

Nicholas Stein 
1,966 words 
6 March 2000
Fortune Magazine 
F-34+ 
Issue: March 6, 2000 Vol. 141 No. 5 Special 70th Anniversary Issue 
English
(Copyright 2000) 

During seminal historical moments, like the dawn of a new millennium, it is often tempting to imagine how our lives might be different ten, 20, even 50 years hence. It is a natural urge, and one easily satisfied by a few sage words from some forward-thinking think tank. In the weeks leading up to Y2K, we learned about biotech, nanotech, and dozens of other words ending in "tech." We heard possiblys, maybes, and almost certainlys. But the scientists and technologists working to transform these probabilities into realities remained, as ever, in the background. 

FORTUNE set out to find a few brave souls out there who weren't just talking about what the future might be like but were actually inventing it--people whose work today will transform radically the way we live our lives tomorrow. But there was one problem: Unlike the 1,500 or so professional members of the World Future Society, most of the brightest young innovators in science and technology don't really advertise. After a few hundred phone calls, we found them anyway. 

In a nondescript office building in New Jersey we discovered Pankaj Merchia, who combined his knowledge of medicine and computer science to create a device that may radically alter the practice of medicine. At the V.A. Hospital in New Orleans, we came upon Timothy Hammond, a kidney specialist who found solutions to his patients' problems in the unlikeliest of venues--outer space. In the bowels of NASA's Ames Research Center at Moffett Field, Calif., we unearthed K.R. Sridhar, whose invention may allow us to breathe on Mars--and in some other unfriendly places, like the middle of a raging fire. In Hawaii, we found another NASA affiliate, Lewis Pinault, who may one day help us navigate the legal and political quagmires associated with space exploration. A member of our group does come from one of the more famous bastions of futurethink, MIT's Media Lab. That's where we met Neil Gershenfeld, who is building brains for musical instruments, chairs, and other formerly inanimate objects. 

Each innovator is young, studious--and surprisingly modest. Perhaps they will change under the glare of the media spotlight. Perhaps they will take out an ad in Futurist magazine. We suspect that they won't. 

K.R. SRIDHAR: FINDING OXYGEN ON PLANET MARS 

"If the pioneers who landed on the East Coast had to load up their wagons with all the food, water, and air they needed to travel west, they would only have gotten as far as the Appalachians," jokes K.R. Sridhar, director of the Space Technologies Laboratory and professor of aerospace and mechanical engineering at the University of Arizona. "They had to learn to live off the land." Sridhar has spent the past ten years figuring out how we can do the same in space. 

Sridhar is at the forefront of the effort to adapt the environments of other planets to make them habitable. The key is breathing: How can we get good air to breathe in those hostile environments? Working with NASA's Johnson Research Center, Sridhar has developed the Oxygen Generator System (OGS). The device--a black dome the size of a grapefruit and weighing about two pounds--is designed to convert the carbon dioxide of Mars' atmosphere into oxygen, which could both sustain the lives of human explorers and serve as rocket fuel for return missions to Earth. The 2003 Surveyor Lander to Mars will test both and may also--courtesy of Sridhar's lab- -create drinking water, electricity, and heat. 

Sridhar's work has terrestrial applications as well. Within five years, he believes, the OGS could be worn by firefighters storming smoke-filled buildings, environmental workers containing toxic spills, soldiers in biological war zones, and emphysema sufferers, who may trade in cumbersome oxygen tanks for OGS fanny packs. 

PANKAJ MERCHIA: THE END OF DIAGNOSTIC ERRORS 

When the Clinton Administration recently announced it would spend $30 million to reduce medical errors, it may have imagined a future like this: Every time a doctor evaluates a patient, a few clicks on a customized Casio handheld computer--linked to a database compiled by Harvard and Johns Hopkins medical school faculty--gives him focused recommendations about what drug and dosage to prescribe, reactions with other medications, and other available treatments. The doctor can print the prescription on a special printer, so tiny that it plugs into the back of the PDA. When he drops the PDA into its cradle to recharge the battery, it also gets an update from the database. Thanks to this marriage of medicine and technology, the number of U.S. deaths (100,000) and serious injuries (one million-plus) attributed to medication errors drops by nine-tenths. 

That's exactly what Pankaj Merchia, a 26-year-old Harvard M.D. and computer science whiz, has dreamed up. Merchia graduated at 18 from Johns Hopkins with bachelor's degrees in biomedical engineering, electrical and computer engineering, and mathematics. After a year in India studying medicine and Eastern philosophy, he combined a medical degree at Harvard with doctoral work in computer science and medical informatics at MIT. He even spent a year in the health-care practice at McKinsey. 

His background seems perfectly choreographed for such an ambitious enterprise. He calls his device the MDPad. In conjunction with the medical center at Johns Hopkins, Merchia has applied for funding from pharmaceutical companies so that he can give away 100,000 MDPads to physicians over the next two years. He hopes to generate additional revenue by selling ads that will pop up on the MDPad screen and by charging fees for premium services to medical specialists. 

LEWIS PINAULT: DEVELOPING A LEGAL SYSTEM FOR SPACE 

A former management consultant at BCG, Gemini, and Coopers & Lybrand, Lewis Pinault has just written a book, "Consulting Demons," about his experience. He also holds a bachelor's degree in political science from MIT and a master's from the London School of Economics. So his talk of the legal and geopolitical implications of mining asteroids and of building settlements on Mars is not the rambling of a Trekkie. Establishing property rights and liability on the moon, asteroids, and planets in our solar system may just be the legal quandary of the future. 

Companies and governments are busy sending more and more communications satellites into orbit. Every launch increases the likelihood of a collision, and yet no law exists to divide up the space in which they travel--or to assign blame or damages should any impact occur. Satellites also provide visual details of the movements of troops and fishing boats, the location of forced labor camps, the probability of volcanic activity, and other crucial information. Despite industrial and governmental pressures to restrain or to release this data, no uniform agreements exist. All of which makes questions of the ownership of planets, asteroids, and the air above Earth seem slightly less esoteric. 

Pinault was a delegate to last summer's U.N. Conference on Space in Vienna. Despite having his studies partially financed by NASA, he advocates an internationalist perspective on space settlement rather than one focused mostly on protecting U.S. interests. An International Space Authority, similar to the U.N., could pool the funds of member states to allow for international space exploration. 

Pinault sees the moon as the first extraterrestrial settlement, with water and metals mined from asteroids as building materials. A moon settlement could then be used as an extraterrestrial way station for the exploration and colonization of Mars and other planets. 

NEIL GERSHENFELD: THE PERFECTION OF PAPERR 

As the leader of the Physics and Media Group at MIT's Media Lab, Neil Gershenfeld has made it his mission to invent technology that brings people together. He has developed computers that make car seats smart enough to know who is sitting on them, so that they don't deploy potentially lethal adult-sized airbags for young children. He has placed penny-sized computers in the bottoms of medication bottles so that they glow when it is time to take a pill, and also inform a doctor, via a network connection, when a prescription must be refilled. But what distinguishes Gershenfeld from many of his peers is his refusal to discard wonderful old technologies just because newer ones come along--one reason he's studying what makes those Stradivarius violins, which were built more than 300 years ago, really sing. 

In his book "When Things Start to Think," Gershenfeld discusses the technophile notion that we must choose between books and computers: "I was struck by how strange it is to replace paper with displays that are guaranteed to be bulkier, take more power, and look worse. If paper is such a good system, why not continue to use it?" The only advantage liquid-crystal panels have over paper, says Gershenfeld, is their ability to change. So instead of trying to build an LCD screen to look and function like paper, Gershenfeld and his Media Lab colleague Joe Jacobsen developed electronic paper and electronic ink. These charged, microencapsulated particles have the look and feel of the real thing, but tiny electrodes implanted in the paper can switch old words and images for new ones. Another invention, radio paper, is even more ingenious. A radio receiver imbedded in the paper and powered by a microscopic solar panel downloads data and changes the "ink" to something new. Imagine a newspaper that updates itself automatically and instantaneously. It might really be the perfection of paper. If it works, Gershenfeld will have pulled off the death of print as we know it--a murder we can all celebrate. 

TIMOTHY HAMMOND: GROWING ORGANS IN OUTER SPACE 

On the CV linked to his Website at Tulane's Environmental Astrobiology Center, Dr. Timothy Hammond lists his activities and interests: "Being beaten at one-on-one basketball by 14-year-old son; admiring 18-year-old daughter's new clothes when she's home, wondering where she is when she's not; walking the dogs." He forgot to indicate that he might one day be recognized for ending drug testing on animals, and for growing human organs in space. The Australian-born kidney specialist, transplant physician, and head of research at New Orleans' V.A. Hospital simply wanted to find a better way to repair the damaged kidneys of his patients. 

He found the answer in space. Tissue cells of kidneys and brains undergo developmental changes when the stresses to which they are exposed change. On Earth, technicians trying to create tissue cells can momentarily "suspend" growing cells above the pressure-filled culture in which they reside--but gravity eventually wins out. Hammond theorized that gravity-free space would reduce stress to zero, optimizing the environment for duplicating tissue cells. 

In 1997, Hammond's theory was tested aboard the space station Mir, where kidney cells extracted from rats in his lab were kept under weightless conditions for four months. A year later, on the space shuttle Columbia, the experiment was repeated with human cells. Implications: Space may be the place to develop small pieces of kidney tissue that could be used instead of animals when pharmaceutical companies test drugs. It may even be possible to grow entire human organs in space--and tailor them to recipients' genetic specifications. The understanding gained from space research can then be used to duplicate these experiments on Earth. Fine, says Hammond-- as long as all this stuff can someday help his patients. 

See also 17 accompanying stories pages F-1 through F-90 of same issue

 Article 138 *******************    



Industrial Management: COMMUNICATIONS 

A BIG FUTURE FOR TINY BIRDS Satellites the size of a deck of cards may transform communications, defense, and astronomy 

By Alan Hall in New York 
1,411 words 
20 March 2000
Business Week 
130H 
Number 3673 
English
(c) 2000 McGraw-Hill, Inc.

Russia's launch of the basketball-sized Sputnik I in 1957 stunned the world and ushered in the Space Age. But Sputnik was a behemoth compared with the two tiny satellites that sent their first signals back to Earth on Feb. 8. If Sputnik went ``beep,'' these tiny birds went ``chirp, chirp.'' Each one is smaller than a deck of cards and weighs less than half a pound. Yet the signals from these fledgling spacecraft, dubbed picosats, may herald another new era in communications and space research. 

The experimental spacecraft, the tiniest ever placed in orbit, were built by Aerospace Corp. in El Segundo, Calif., for a consortium funded by the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA). Connected by a gossamer tether to prevent them from drifting apart, the two picosats talked to each other for a week--and to a third on the ground, mounted in the center of a dish antenna at SRI International in Menlo Park, Calif. Then the AA batteries that were the only source of power gave out. Their low-power radios were adapted from ordinary cordless phones. So the signals could be picked up only when SRI's big data-comm dish was aimed squarely at the satellites. To help track the pair, the tether contains gold wires, which return a strong radar echo. 

Everything went so smoothly that today's huge, multibillion-dollar satellites could be getting lots of company in the not-too-distant future. Aerospace executives and others envision a chorus of inexpensive, mass-produced spacecraft that can be spewed from motherships on demand--and even soar off into deep space. ``They are a harbinger of all kinds of miniature satellites,'' says Ernest Y. Robinson, a top engineer at Aerospace and co-author of a seminal paper that launched the concept of tiny spacecraft at a meeting of the International Astronautical Federation Congress in 1993. Since then, satellites have come to be classified by weight--picosats tipping the scales at less than 1 kilogram, or 2.2 pounds, for example (page 130J). 

Packed into a mother satellite constructed by Stanford University space-engineering students, the picosats were lofted on a four-stage booster from Vandenberg Air Force Base on Jan. 26. The orbiting twins epitomize NASA's goal of building spacecraft that are smaller, lighter, and cheaper. And they are the closest thing yet to a satellite-on-a-chip. 

Picosats rely on an advanced technology known as MEMS, for microelectromechanical systems. These systems integrate semiconductor circuits and minuscule mechanical devices carved from wafers of silicon. Earthbound examples of MEMS include air-bag release triggers in cars and tiny squirt-gun valves in ink-jet printer heads. 

The primary goal of the present mission is to test various MEMS devices designed to be switched on and off remotely, via radio commands from the ground or a mothersat. This is a crucial step in demonstrating that MEMS chips are reliable enough to replace the bulky electronics boxes now used in satellites for navigation and orbital control, sensing, and computation. Last July the space shuttle Columbia carried into orbit a package of some 30 MEMS satellite components made by Rockwell Science Center in Thousand Oaks, Calif., including sensors and gyroscope controls. The tiny devices were closely monitored, and their performance was compared with that of the shuttle's own instruments. They passed with flying colors, leading to the current experiment. MINIATURE THRUSTERS. Last year's shuttle mission was also the first test in space of a novel propulsion system designed to allow tiny satellites to maneuver in orbit. The device, developed by TRW, Aerospace, and California Institute of Technology under a $3.5 million DARPA contract, literally puts thousands of tiny rockets on a computer chip. Each thruster is a pinhead-size silicon box filled with a propellant. On command, microscopic heating elements in the boxes ignite the propellant. The burning fuel bursts through the thin outer face of the box, providing a tiny rocket-like thrust. Although each MEMS rocket can be used only once, arrays of thousands of thrusters might keep a small satellite on course for several years. Engineers estimate that almost a million thrusters could be packed into an area of just 16 square inches. 

The thrusters will also be used to ``de-orbit'' little satellites at the end of their useful lives. That's so they won't add to the space junk already in orbit--floating debris that can pose a collision hazard. ``That's a problem you have to address from Day One,'' says Siegfried Janson, an Aerospace scientist who invented the term nanosatellite. 

When all MEMS components have been checked and rechecked and finally judged ``space worthy,'' engineers intend to combine them in slightly larger satellites. These nanosat-size birds would be big enough to do really useful work in space and curb the need for costly monsters as big as cars. The mighty mites would be powered by solar cells and have the navigational skills to roam around space much more freely. 

That's what appeals to the Defense Dept. Military planners and intelligence chiefs see great potential for nanosat spies. Mother birds packed with various types of special-purpose nanosats could be parked in orbit until their chicks are needed. Or it might be possible to shoot nanosats into low-earth orbit from a cannon or from high-flying jet planes. Either way, the Pentagon could quickly put a swarm of satellites over tomorrow's Kosovo, avoiding the protracted countdown required to launch a big rocket. The Lilliputian spies would provide a bird's-eye view of a battlefield--including details that might be missed by spy sats at much higher perches. 

But Robinson and Janson see many other uses. A few small sats could pick up messages as they pass over one point on the globe, then download them to receivers at other locations. Or they could imitate, on the cheap, the Teledesic concept of a globe-circling constellation, with at least one sat always overhead. Janson says nanosats in contact with 10-meter-diameter ground antennas could handle data at the rate of 1 megabit a second. ``One day we may see nanosatellite clusters used for Internet-in-the-sky applications,'' he says. PONY EXPRESS. Scientific applications also abound. For astronomers, an array of nanosats could form a giant radio telescope in space that would dwarf ones on earth. Although nanosatellites will be limited to carrying optic systems with lenses no bigger than about 10 centimeters in diameter, that's sufficient to provide a ground resolution in the tens of meters. Suddenly, it could be affordable to continuously monitor agricultural, mineral, and water resources. 

For its part, NASA already is planning to use nanosats to study the interaction of the earth's magnetic field with charged particles from the sun. In addition, future planetary probes might create a Pony Express relay back to Mother Earth by dropping off a trail of nansosats as they plunge into space. That would reduce the onboard power needed for distant probes to send reports back to earth, freeing up space for additional scientific instruments. 

Scientists predict that the first full-fledged military and commercial nanosats will zip through the heavens in 8 to 10 years. It could happen sooner, thanks to the recent boost from President Clinton. His 2001 budget request includes an 84% increase in nanotechnology funding, with the Pentagon slated to get $110 million for projects such as MEMS--a 57% boost over its current allowance. 

So construction of the huge International Space Station continues, and plans are still being drawn up for human bases on the moon and Mars. But a large part of the future may lie with the lightweights--minuscule spacecraft that will fit in an astronaut's pocket. 


Little Giants
The electronics industry is blazing new trails in space. As satellites shrink,
scientists see a wide range of sizes coming into use.
