 Article 211 *******************    



Desert Blooms; A lot of online stock pickers have been losing their shirts lately. Not these guys. 

Adam Leitzes and Joshua Solan, with Ben Berentson 
1,565 words 
3 December 2001
Forbes Best of The Web 
13 
Volume 168; Issue 14 
English
Copyright 2001 Forbes Inc. 

In difficult markets, smart stock pickers prevail. Here are five online mavens thriving in adversity. 

Brandon GoyetteScreen Name: WallstarbSite: www.FinancialChat.comLocation: Nashua, N.H. 

Goyette is the ringleader of Financial Chat, home to the ActiveTrader chat room. He oversees a diverse and occasionally rowdy group, and actively participates in the never-ending discussions. "I only have one pair of eyes," says Goyette. "But with 1,000 other pairs of eyes looking at every conceivable stock, news service and media outlet, it is very seldom that we as a group do not know why a stock is moving. More important, we often know why a stock should move before it moves."Goyette, 31, trained at Goldman Sachs as a stock analyst and then did fixed-income arbitrage for hedge fund Mariner Investment Group before starting his own hedge fund, Delta Investment Group, in January 2000. So far this year his fund is up 95%. His investment philosophy is "to be where others are not." He attempts to uncover small-cap diamonds in the rough before anyone on Wall Street takes notice. A computer science and finance graduate of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, Goyette focuses on small tech companies and insists that they have sound financials. He places a particular emphasis on companies with high cash and low debt. He wants to ensure that his investments have enough capital and financial flexibility to tide them through the many months it can take before Wall Street begins to pay attention. 

These days Goyette's investment approach has him focusing on nanotechnology, the science of manufacturing devices at the subatomic level. "Nanotechnology, while still in its infancy, holds the promise to revolutionize many different aspects of our economy and civilization," says Goyette. 

Favorite Stocks: Nanophase Technologies 

and Nanogen 

as pure plays in the nanotechnology space. Says Goyette, "Once the average person has heard of nanotechnology, everyone will be scrambling for these types of stocks." 

Vaughn Okumura 

Site: www.VTOReport.comLocation: Pearl City, Hawaii 

At 36, Vaughn Okumura, CPA, is retired and living in Hawaii. His story is the same as many successful individual investors: a few hard knocks at the start, "visions of losing it all" as a stock account balance was depleted and a final, miraculous recovery. Okumura says his holdings went from $35,000 to $7,000 to $2 million after taxes in a matter of ten years. 

He started his site, VTO Report, in 1998 to satisfy his passion for stock investing and to teach others his technical analysis techniques. Fundamentals? "It's like driving forward by looking in the rearview mirror," he says. On the other hand, "the chart of a stock will give you clues as to how a particular company is operating long before it reports its earnings." 

Okumura's most successful strategy outlined on the site is his short-term trades of the Nasdaq 100 index. Up 10% year-to-date and 184% since 1997, the portfolio uses a simple relative strength indicator to determine when the market is overbought and oversold. His site's "smooth" stocks are consistent winners over many years. "Most investors buy stocks in a downtrend thinking they've uncovered a bargain," he cautions. "But in most cases, they end up with a portfolio of clunkers." 

Favorite Stocks: Harley-Davidson 

, Stryker 

and Sysco 

. "All three have been in nonvolatile uptrends over the last 15 years." 

Peter Suzman 

Site: www.SiliconInvestor.comLocation: Newton, Mass. 

As a Harvard Law graduate and the founder of a company that constructs and administers stock-option plans for public companies, Peter Suzman is not your typical Internet message board participant. He's also not your typical biotech stock expert. Suzman has emerged as a leader on community-investment site Silicon Investor, where he has contributed thousands of messages on the subject of biotechnology. Says Suzman, "This is the century when many, if not most, common diseases will be cured or controlled." 

Biotech is perfectly suited to Suzman's long-term buy-and-hold strategy. "One unusual aspect of the drug industry is that things happen very slowly, so you can analyze many years ahead," explains Suzman. "One knows the universe of new drugs that might be approved over the next five years, and also when every existing drug is going off patent." Of course, many biotech stocks plummet when drugs they are backing fail. Suzman alleviates the impact of such occurrences with a diversified portfolio of biotech stocks. 

With more than 90% of his portfolio invested in biotech, Suzman devotes hours to reading annual and quarterly reports of biotech companies, health care conference proceedings, medical journal articles and clinical trial results. He also utilizes Silicon Investor to query industry scientists, all of whom disguise their identities with pseudonyms. Suzman claims his strategy has generated annual returns in excess of 60% in 1998, 1999 and 2000. This year, however, Suzman is down 10% so far. 

Favorite Stocks: Sepracor 

. "The best short- and medium-term pipeline in biotech. SEPR makes patented improvements in existing drugs, which is a lot less risky than developing brand-new drugs." He also likes Intermune 

and Aviron 

. 

Bill Ginsberg 

Screen Name: shortboySite: www.shortboy.comLocation: New York, N.Y. 

Bill Ginsberg requested that his picture not be included with this article and you won't find much about his background on his site. As a short-seller Ginsberg is fearful of being labeled a bad guy or somehow as unpatriotic because he benefits when stock prices go down. 

In 1996, after dropping out of Columbia Business School and trying his hand at trading options on the floor of the American Stock Exchange, Ginsberg decided to start trading his own capital. At first he thought his 76% return from short-selling in a strong bull market was a fluke. But his 49% return in 1997 and 40% return in 1998 convinced Ginsberg that he was good at identifying peaks of major stock run-ups. 

Ginsberg took his strategy to the Web by launching Shortboy.com in 1999. At first, he divulged his picks to anyone who would listen. On July 1, 2001, Ginsberg began charging $119 per quarter for real-time access to his picks (delayed content is still available free of charge). So far, more than 200 people have subscribed to the service. 

Ginsberg's approach is simple: short high-cap, highly liquid stocks whose gains have been overextended. He follows closely the bullishness and bearishness indicator published by Investors Intelligence, which paints a picture of the market sentiment of investment advisers. When more than 55% of advisers indicate they are bullish, Ginsberg believes the market is due for a fall. When bearishness outweighs bullishness, Ginsberg gets out of the market because he doesn't like to go long on stocks. 

Favorite Stocks: Short, Kohl's 

(KSS) and Laboratory Corp. of America 

(LH). "Kohl's has a P/E of 40. Any disappointment and it will drop to 30. LH has a P/E ratio around 37, and has risen from a price of $10 in three years." 

Kevin Fravel 

Site: www.marketocracy.comLocation: Bristol, Pa. 

Thanks to the Fed's barrage of rate cuts, housing is one of the few remaining vibrant sectors in the economy. That's been a boon to Kevin Fravel's housing-stock-heavy mutual fund portfolio, posted at Marketocracy.com. His Kevin D. Fravel Fund is up 50% since the beginning of the year and 73% since its inception in September 2000. "Inflation and interest rates are all historically low, and those are what drive the home-building industry," says Fravel, a 33-year-old computer programmer. 

Fravel, a devotee of Benjamin Graham, screens for stocks on Multex.com, looking for ones that have a price/book ratio below 1, a P/E ratio below 10, positive revenue growth and increasing operating margins. Fravel also goes to Morningstar.com to check company ratings, looking for strong financial stability. 

He particularly likes smaller, less well-known regional home builders, figuring they are attractive takeover targets for the bigger players. D.R. Horton's 

October takeover of Schuler Homes 

gave Fravel a nearly 48% gain. 

Favorite Stocks: Dominion Homes 

, the largest homebuilder in central Ohio: "The company has a more than $210 million order backlog. It's a takeover target." Hovanian Enterprises 

: "A family-run company. It's trading at its book value of $12 and has a P/E of 5." 

Adam Leitzes and Joshua Solan are the authors of the soon-to-be-published Bulls, Bears, and Brains: Investing with the Best and Brightest of the Financial Internet. John Wiley & Sons, $24.95.

 Article 212 *******************    



Second Sight; "I bought it on eBay as a lark, in the days when we all felt rich enough to do such flippant things..." 

Michael S. Malone 
2,377 words 
3 December 2001
Forbes ASAP 
92 
Volume 168; Issue 14 
English
Copyright 2001 Forbes Inc. 

Seen Through A Relic, A Haunting New Vision 

I bought it on eBay as a lark, in the days when we all felt rich enough to do such flippant things. I don't even remember my winning bid, which says something as well. 

It came in a 3-foot-square cardboard box, which I opened on the driveway to minimize the mess, as workmen behind me sanded and hammered away while restoring my house. It was wrapped in a black foam sheet encased in wadded pages of the Connecticut Post. One headline read: "Gates Tells Congress to Trust High Tech." 

I knew what I'd find when I pulled away the sheet: a Norden bombsight. But I was still taken aback by its presence. 

It was black as anthracite, with dust in every corner and curve. The dust of an English runway, perhaps? Or sand from North Africa? Or just the cobwebs of the garage in which it had sat for the past half century. 

It seemed less menacing than coldly malign, in the way only a relic weapon could. Twenty pounds, not much bigger than a football, with a cylinder at one end, a sphere at the other. It was seeded with knurled knobs and toothed gears and dials. From one side hung an old cloth-insulated wire terminating in a plug. Underneath, it held a small pane of glass the size of a cigarette pack. On top, a second pane opened like a porthole into the sphere. And at the center, covered with a rotting rubber eye protector, was a lens. 

I set the contraption atop the recycling bin and stepped back to regard it. On that bright Northern California day, my prize seemed like a dark emissary from a forgotten time. From one angle it looked like a clock mechanism, from another an automobile transmission, from yet another a bomb. 

It was, in fact, all three. Mounted in the nose of a B-17 bomber, attached with gyroscopes and rotating mirrors and cables to the airplane around it, and manipulated by a deft bombardier, the Norden was the most dangerous weapon in the world in the early years of World War II. Once it was set on a target and adjusted for airspeed and other variables, the Norden literally took over for the pilot, flying the plane on a strict approach path and telling the bombardier when to push the button to release the ton of bombs or incendiaries from their racks 10 feet behind him. 

So technologically innovative and important was the Norden--neither the Germans nor the Japanese had anything close--that bombardiers were sworn to do whatever was necessary to keep one from falling into enemy hands. If the bomber was going down, they were to pull out their .45-caliber automatic and shoot it or pull a switch that ignited an explosive mounted inside the device. Or, if all else failed, they were to obey orders to ride the plane right down into oblivion in the German soil. 

My father sat at just such a Norden bombsight on a rickety seat in the Plexiglas nose of the "Badland Bat," a B-17 in the 615th Squadron of the 401st Bomb Group of the 8th Air Force. As the barrels of the twin .50-caliber machine guns overheated from his futile attempts to shoot attacking German fighters, as flak burst just beyond the glass and dying men in planes around him screamed into his headset, and as his heart pounded and his hands shook from the fear and the adrenaline, he would bow his head as though in prayer and bring his eye against the frozen eyepiece. Twisting one knob, then another, he would align the reticulated crosshairs on the target. Then, so freighted with helplessness and horror that the passing seconds seemed like days, he would hit the switch that surrendered control of his life and those of the seven men around him to that little black machine. 

The target itself had been identified that morning at a pre-dawn briefing in a Quonset hut thick with the smell of cigarettes and coffee. The target always had a name--Schweinfurt, Cologne, Berlin--and a title: ball bearing factory, pillboxes, industrial district. But on the map and in the crosshairs, it was merely an image of a bridge or a crossroads, or the shape of a building. There were no people in sight; just the abstract shapes of targets and then the sudden blooming of explosions as the Badland Bat left a swath of fire across miles of German farms, villages, and towns. 

Thirty times over Germany and France between January and June 1944, my father bowed to his bombsight. Looking back, he would tell me that for all the terrors of being fired on by antiaircraft guns and being strafed by German fighters as his bomber flew into and out of target zones, the worst moments were always those seconds when he handed his life over to the Norden--the long moments as he waited for it to tell him to rain death over the countryside. 

My father was a young man of intelligence and imagination, and those combat experiences made him brazen. He sneaked off the base the night before D day to meet a girl in a local pub. They also made him cold-hearted. When a rookie crew arrived to replace his hut mates who had died in an earlier raid, my father teased them, saying they'd taken the doomed side of the shelter; they died the next day on their very first mission. But, after four months and 25 missions, the experience began to break his mind. In letters home, the cocky young man who'd circumvented the censors by disguising his number of raids as his birthday ("I am 21 years old today") found himself toward the end struggling to compose a single coherent sentence ("I can't seem to keep my mind on one thought anymore."). 

Unlike every second man in the 8th Air Force, my father made it home alive and unhurt. Five years later, he was back overseas, this time on a Pacific island and with a new job in Air Force intelligence. Through another lens--this one shielding his eye with a smoky black filter--he watched purple lightning crackle across the mushroom clouds rising from Eniwetok atoll. 

My father returned to Germany, and I was a Cold War baby, born in Munich, then a city of vacant lots scraped smooth of the rubble of a once thriving metropolis. It had become a Norden world, with all of us hurtling forward on autopilot over a menacing landscape. Only a few people, like my father as he took the .32-caliber automatic out of the closet and headed for the Czech border, still felt they had some control of their fate. 

But soon he lost even that. At the end of the decade we came home. My father found himself closing his military career in Washington, D.C., shuttling back and forth as liaison officer between the war rooms of the Pentagon and the White House, and between the CIA and the FBI. It was a position of enormous responsibility but little control. My father began to sit alone at lunch in the garden of the Hirshhorn Museum to calm himself; on Friday nights he would drink to fend off his sense of the inevitable. 

He reached his nadir in October 1962. On duty nights, my mother would awaken me at midnight, and we would drive from Falls Church, Virginia, into D.C., me in pajamas and wrapped in a blanket in the back seat. It was usually so lonely and dark that I would sleep the whole way. But this night was unlike any other. Every light in every building on Pennsylvania Avenue and along the Capitol Mall seemed to be on. Yet the streets were eerily empty. 

We picked up my father. He usually drove us home, but on this night he was incapable of holding the steering wheel. His voice had a tone I'd never heard before--a tone it probably hadn't taken in almost 20 years. The Cuban Missile Crisis had begun. Until we arrived, my father had sat alone, in the basement of the Office of Special Investigations before a bank of Teletype code machines, reading with growing horror as they spit out the beginning of the end of the world. The ICBMs armed, Strategic Air Command at fail-safe, airborne divisions kneeling beside runways. He had known as much as anybody on earth about what was unfolding, but he could do nothing about it. The bombsight had taken over. And for hours he sat helpless at ground zero, waiting to die, knowing his family would die as well. 

Suddenly, it was over. Everything seemed to change. Although it was still a Norden world, the target had receded for the moment. My father had almost been a victim. A heart attack a few weeks later nearly killed him. But he survived, retired from the military with honor, and took a job with NASA, heading west with his family to California. 

We arrived in Silicon Valley just in time to join the illusion. The Valley had been born of war. Military contracts had built Hewlett-Packard and Varian; the nuclear age had given birth to the Valley's largest employer, Lockheed Missile and Space. So, too, had defense orders underwritten the success of the Valley's first modern company, Fairchild Semiconductor. All had grown rich building successive generations of weaponry; they would grow richer yet. 

About the time we arrived, in an event all but forgotten even by historians of technology, the Federal Communications Commission announced that all future televisions would offer not only VHF tuners but UHF tuners as well. It was a little event of immeasurable consequences. Chasing million-unit orders, the chip industry for the first time turned away from the bombsight business and toward the department store. 

It has never turned back. I remember the day my father brought home a Hewlett-Packard 35 calculator and pronounced it a miracle. I remember standing in line, with the boys who would later create the personal computer revolution, in the lobby of a NASA building waiting my turn to play Lunar Lander on a computer terminal. I saw the first Atari video game in the hallway of a comedy club down the street from my house. And I walked with my father through a trade show in San Francisco and saw the Apple I, built by two of my neighbors. 

America no longer seemed a Norden nation. Our fate was no longer on autopilot. Sometimes we even imagined we were flying the plane. My father cheated death for a quarter century. Each time his heart would fail, some new technology appeared on the scene to save him. He traveled the world, programmed his computer, drank beer, and gave his time to charitable work, acting as though death couldn't catch him. And when he did die--from a fall off a ladder, not from his scarred old heart--even his death seemed an act of will, not helplessness. 

It was now a willful world, as though an alchemy of technology and desire would deliver to us all of our dreams. Our enemies were less defeated than enlisted into a cybernetic common cause. The autopilot retreated so far into memory that two generations forgot it. We replaced it with nostalgia for the future: the aching desire for that distant, more perfect place we could already imagine, promised to us by our technologies. Our perpetual unhappiness was that we were not there yet, a there that raced ahead from calculators and PCs to bioengineering and nanomachines. Somewhere, no doubt just ahead, lay the fulfillment of all our desires. The beauty of it was that now, it seemed, anyone could plot his own flight path and pilot his own life. 

It was in that prelapsarian time, just ended yet long ago, that I carried the Norden bombsight out to the sunny driveway, past the workmen and the new Jaguar, all of them paid for with overvalued stock options. I set it atop the recycling bin, filled with newspaper headlines about the latest stock market record high: "eBay's Whitman America's Richest Woman CEO." I looked through the eyepiece...and saw nothing. Nothing except a mirror reflection of the brilliant day before me. 

Disappointed, I put the device back in its box, another bauble from the bubble, and returned it to the garage, between the old, unread art books and the new, unused porcelain--an evil old black toad forgotten in the shadows. 

Then late on a mid-September afternoon I took the box out again. Our family had just returned from church, where we had, with other Silicon Valleyites in the pews around us, wept and prayed for the thousands murdered by terrorists. As I bowed my head, I felt the distant mechanical shudder of the autopilot kicking in once more. Driving home, I could see my neighbors putting out American flags and glancing up nervously at any sound overhead. We put out a flag of our own. Then, without thinking, I went into the garage and brought out the Norden. 

I did the same as before, though the day was overcast, and the papers in the new recycling bin carried headlines proclaiming "WAR." The Norden was dustier and darker than ever. The eyepiece seemed harder and more brittle. Once again, I bowed and put my face to it. Looking through the lens I saw this time what I had missed before. What my father had seen before me. 

I saw my family and home caught squarely in the bombsight's crosshairs. 

Michael S. Malone is editor at large of Forbes ASAP. He is the author of nine books, including Going Public, The Big Score, and Infinite Loop. His next book, a collection of his writings called The Valley of Heart's Delight, will be published in 2002.

Developments to Watch 

NANO PEAPODS THAT PACK A PUNCH 

Edited by Otis Port 
235 words 
21 January 2002
BusinessWeek 
75 
Number 3766 
English
(Copyright 2002 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

Some molecules naturally go together like peas in a pod. Spherical clusters of carbon atoms called buckyballs, for example, are quite comfortable inside straw-shaped nanotubes, also composed of pure carbon. And these nano peapods may function as superfast transistors. That's the promise revealed by intricate measurements of nano peapods by a research team led by Ali Yazdani, a University of Illinois physicist. 

Using a scanning tunneling microscope, the team mapped the movement of buckyballs and electron waves shown in this image inside the lilliputian peapods. They found that changing the location of the peas can transform the pod from a conductor into a semiconductor or an insulator. As a result, in future nanochips, carbon peapods may become a versatile component that can be tuned to play different roles. 

Yazdani's team used peapods produced by David Luzzi's materials science group at the University of Pennsylvania, which first created these complex structures in 1998. Since then, several peapod varieties have been produced. And in Japan, scientists have stuffed metal atoms inside the pealike buckyballs in nanopods. ``There's a whole gamut of things you can put inside'' to modify a peapod's electrical properties, says Yazdani. Studying them all will keep him busy for years. 

Photograph: A POD OF BUCKYBALLS PHOTOGRAPH BY D. HORNBAKER & A. YAZDANI (UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS)

 Article 214 *******************    



Technology Journal 

H-P, UCLA Receive a Patent for New Technology 

By David P. Hamilton 
Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal 
549 words 
24 January 2002
The Wall Street Journal 
B8 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

Hewlett-Packard Co. and the University of California at Los Angeles said they have received a broad patent for technology that could one day be used to build molecular-scale electronic devices. The patent's claims haven't yet been checked out by outside scientists. 

H-P researchers say the work covered by the patent represents a major step toward building so-called nanoscale electronic devices, which could lead to vastly more dense and powerful computer chips that operate at the scale of individual atoms and molecules. 

"We're trying to create a technology that would recreate integrated circuits," said Philip Kuekes, a computer scientist at H-P. Dr. Kuekes submitted the patent application together with fellow H-P researcher R. Stanley Williams and UCLA chemist James Heath. 

Nanotechnology, the name widely applied to such microscopic inventions, is a hot topic among researchers in Silicon Valley and other scientific centers, though most commercial applications are still years off. 

The new patent lays out several methods for "growing" crossed arrays of tiny wires on a silicon substrate, each wire only a few atoms thick. The junctions of such "nanowires" can serve as electronic switches, according to the patent, and could act as much smaller analogues to existing circuit components such as diodes or transistors. By contrast, today's computer chips are manufactured in a long series of excruciatingly detailed steps, and remain far bulkier than such nanoarrays. 

In addition, the scientists claim a method for building insulating patches into the array of wires, allowing them to subdivide the array into regions that can operate as separate electronic devices. As a result, the researchers say they can build complex logic circuits with this technology, although they haven't yet demonstrated that ability. Logic circuits are the basic computational elements of a computer chip. 

Dr. Williams said the team hasn't yet published any of the work covered by the patent to retain a competitive advantage over other scientists. Now that the patent is public, he said, "we certainly anticipate a significant number of publications this year." 

Patent claims involving basic science are often difficult to verify independently. While examiners at the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office attempt to ensure that work is unique and not duplicative of existing "prior art," they are rarely experts in the given field. For true scientific verification, many scientists still look to the peer review -- independent appraisal by other experts -- that is required for publication in leading scientific journals. 

Even an issued patent may not be the last word in intellectual-property rights. Other patents in this field are also likely to issue, and any conflicts will have to be resolved by negotiation and cross-licensing or in the courts. Dr. Williams did say that H-P doesn't intend to enforce its patent in research applications. 

In addition, the U.S. government is now funding follow-up work at both H-P and UCLA through the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency. As a result, the government is likely to retain some rights to future applications and could step in if patent holders are restricting access to a technology considered fundamental to progress.

 Article 216 *******************    



Spending on Science and Technology to Climb 

255 words 
5 February 2002
The Wall Street Journal 
No Page Citation 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

WASHINGTON -- The Bush administration's budget contains billions of dollars in new spending on science and technology projects, a sharp reversal from last year's budget request, which all but ignored both areas. 

Among the biggest winners is the Networking and Information Technology Research and Development Program, a multiagency effort led by the White House's Office of Science and Technology Policy, whose funding increased 3% to $1.9 billion. The budget also proposes $679 million for the White House's National Nanotechnology Initiative, which supports research devoted to creating tiny but powerful computers, a 17% increase. 

And the White House is seeking to boost the budget for the National Science Foundation by nearly $250 million, to $5.03 billion from $4.79 billion. 

All told, the White House is planning to devote $57 billion to science and technology efforts, a record that represents a sharp 9% increase over last year. The spending accounts for about half of the $111.8 billion in new federal research and development funds, which was up 8% from the fiscal 2002 budget. 

Not all tech programs were so lucky. In his new budget request, the White House proposes to eliminate all funding for the Technology Opportunities Program, a Clinton-era program designed to help bridge the so-called digital divide by bringing computers and Internet access to poor and underserved areas. 

The program, which began in 1994, gave out grants that had to be matched by local government or private-sector funds.

 Article 217 *******************    



Bush Offers $2.13 Trillion Budget, Launching Era of Deficit Spending --- Popular Domestic Initiatives Face Security Squeeze In Wake of Sept. 11 

By John D. McKinnon and Shailagh Murray 
Staff Reporters of The Wall Street Journal 
1,849 words 
5 February 2002
The Wall Street Journal 
A2 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

WASHINGTON -- President Bush formally launched a new era of deficit spending, sending a $2.13 trillion budget to Congress that requires tens of billions of dollars of federal borrowing to pay for vast increases in the military and homeland-security spending. 

At the same time, Mr. Bush is trying to contain the potential political fallout for a return to red ink by tightly squeezing many domestic programs. But he risks another backlash by attempting to curb popular programs such as education. 

Mr. Bush also used his budget blueprint to attempt to jump-start the stalled debate on Capitol Hill over an economic-stimulus bill, urging Congress to adopt nearly $90 billion worth of tax cuts and spending increases this year and another $70 billion-plus next year. But enthusiasm for such a measure is waning on Capitol Hill, and the plan could die this week. 

As a candidate in the 2000 elections, Mr. Bush pushed broadly popular domestic initiatives such as education spending and a prescription-drug benefit for seniors. And he vowed fiscal responsibility, promising to weed out needless defense spending and protect the mounting Social Security surpluses that many in both parties wanted to reserve for debt reduction. 

But in the wake of Sept. 11 attacks -- as well as the recession -- Mr. Bush's priorities now are topsy-turvy, as yesterday's new submission show: 

-- The administration forecasts budget deficits for at least three years, with red ink for the fiscal year starting Oct. 1 totaling $80 billion. 

-- Contrary to campaign pledges to wall off the Social Security trust fund from other uses, Mr. Bush proposes using all the Social Security surpluses -- and then borrowing from the general public beyond that -- to fund the government for the next two years, and to spend well over $100 billion of Social Security funds in each of the following three years. (Social Security benefits won't be affected during this time; the Treasury intends to repay the trust fund for monies used elsewhere.) 

-- A year ago, the administration projected it could slash the amount of federal debt outstanding by $1.6 trillion by 2007. Instead, the figure goes up by $58 billion. 

-- Instead of shrinking the federal government, the number of federal employees would go up this year and next year, reversing a years-long trend. Federal spending as a percentage of the economy is expected to rise to near 20% for the first time since 1996. 

Administration officials were quick to defend their changed fiscal stance with a simple answer: "It's a budget to win a two-front war," White House budget director Mitchell Daniels said. Bush aides have noted that, as a candidate, Mr. Bush said that he would alter his antideficit stance in the event of recession or war -- both of which have come to pass. 

While defense spending would get the biggest increase in two decades, and homeland-security spending would rise to $37.7 billion, many social services would get squeezed. Some job-training programs branded as "underperforming" would face lower budgets, as would some regulatory agencies. A favorite Clinton-era community-development program, the Community Development Financial Institutions fund in the Treasury Department, would see its budget cut to $68 billion from $81 billion. 

In all, eight agencies would take actual cuts or receive increases of 1% or less in discretionary spending, including the politically sensitive departments of Agriculture and Labor, as well as the Army Corps of Engineers. 

In a further sign of how far its priorities have shifted, the tax-cutting Bush administration even quietly proposed increasing a few fees in the name of battling terror, including fees on cruise-vessel passengers and ski resorts and timber operations on federal land. 

Some programs that officials of the Office of Management and Budget had criticized just a few months ago got whopping increases. The government's information-technology spending, for example, would rise to more $50 billion from $45 billion, a sharp reversal from last year's budget request, which all but ignored both areas. 

Among the biggest winners is the Networking and Information Technology Research and Development Program, a multiagency effort led by the White House's Office of Science and Technology Policy, whose funding increased 3% to $1.9 billion. The budget also proposes $679 million for the White House's National Nanotechnology Initiative, which supports research devoted to creating tiny but powerful computers, a 17% percent increase. And the White House is seeking to boost the budget for the National Science Foundation by nearly $250 million, from $4.79 billion to $5.03 billion. 

All told, the White House is planning to devote $57 billion to science and technology efforts, a record high that represents a sharp 9% increase over last year. The spending accounts for almost half of the $111.8 billion in new federal R&D funds, which was up 8% from the 2002 budget. Much of the increase is being portrayed as a way to shore up homeland security. 

While the bulk of the spending battles will be fought out over the remaining eight months of the fiscal year, the first major fight President Bush faces is over his push to revive an economic-stimulus bill that Congress couldn't pass last year. 

In hopes of reaching a compromise with Democrats, Mr. Bush put forward a set of broad principles that emphasized help for individuals, particularly lower-income workers. His guidelines included speeding up parts of last spring's tax-cut bill; giving tax refunds similar to last year's for low- and moderate-income people; helping displaced workers with extended benefits; reforming the minimum tax on corporations; and creating incentives for businesses to buy new equipment. 

Senate Majority Leader Tom Daschle has introduced his own, scaled-back version designed to find common ground between the two parties. But legislators from both parties are cooling to both the White House and Senate Democratic plans. 

Now that the economy appears to be pulling out of recession, many politicians aren't sure the package is needed. Also, the Enron scandal has made them nervous about touching the corporate alternative-minimum tax, or AMT, a centerpiece of the White House and House GOP stimulus plans. Were the tax to be repealed outright, Enron stands to collect $254 million in back AMT payments. Mr. Bush yesterday proposed only to "reform" the tax prospectively. 

Many Democrats, meanwhile, have come to believe that no stimulus is better than whatever package the House and Senate negotiate -- legislation that would almost certainly include long-term tax cuts, either for individuals or corporations. "My hope is we don't get anything but unemployment-benefit increases," said Rep. Jim McDermott (D., Wash), a senior member of the House Budget and Ways and Means committees. 

A series of procedural votes are now scheduled for tomorrow in the Senate on the competing stimulus proposals. The most likely result is that neither side will secure enough votes to pass its proposal, and the issue will die for the year. 

While Congress may be cool toward short-term economic stimulus, legislators are eager to boost farm aid, and Mr. Bush's budget endorses a major increase in agriculture spending over the next decade -- a major issue in some Midwestern states that have become fertile ground for Democrats. 

The administration also proposes to tighten down on what it called both congressional pork-barrel spending and waste in the executive branch. The budget chapter on the Department of Housing and Urban Development notes, for example, that: "Carmel, Indiana, with a poverty rate of 1% and median household income over $77,000, received $1 million for its parks. On the other hand, Gary, Indiana, with a 26% poverty rate and a median income less than a third of Carmel's, did not receive a project grant." 

Mr. Daniels, the White House budget director, admitted there will be heavy resistance to such proposed cuts on Capitol Hill, particularly in an election year. He warned that the president would push back hard against excess spending on the domestic side. He noted that Mr. Bush threatened vetoes on three spending bills last year. "It may well come to that this year," Mr. Daniels said. 

Overall, discretionary spending apart from defense and homeland security would increase by only about 2% in 2003. Even including homeland-security needs, overall nondefense spending would increase just 3.2% in 2004 and less than 2% for the next three years. 

Republicans and Democrats alike were distancing themselves from the administration's deficits. Some Republicans and conservative Democrats were discussing a budget resolution that shows government spending in balance for next year. 

"Short-term deficits are acceptable during a war or recession. However, I'm concerned that the president's budget puts us dangerously close to a return to long-term deficit spending," said Democratic Rep. Dennis Moore of Kansas, co-chairman of the so-called Blue Dog Democrats' Budget and Social Security Task Force. 

"Enron's not revealing its true debt, and the U.S. government is not revealing its true debt," said Democratic Sen. Kent Conrad of North Dakota, chairman of the Senate Budget Committee. 

In fact, the current budget plans don't include costs from some expensive proposals for reforms. While President Bush's budget again proposed Medicare changes, including a prescription-drug benefit, it included nothing for the costlier remaking of Social Security. Its 10-year cost has been estimated at $1 trillion -- about the amount of the surplus the White House projects will exist during the next decade. 

It also doesn't include the cost to fix the individual alternative-minimum tax, which will trap as many as 40 million taxpayers during the next decade. That fix could exceed $300 billion. 

The administration's total tax package would cut taxes by about $591 billion over 10 years, although some Democrats said that figure understated the amount -- for example, by leaving out some tax-break extensions that would be effective in 2002.

              Is Stimulus Still Possible?
  President Bush and congressional leaders say they still want a
stimulus package, though many lawmakers are having second thoughts.
Here's what a package would likely include:
  The Emerging Senate Consensus
  -- $300 rebate checks (one-year cost: $14 billion)
  -- Two-year 30% depreciation bonus (two-year cost: $77 billion)
  -- State Medicaid assistance (one-year cost: $5 billion)
  -- Extended unemployment benefits (one-year cost: $8 billion)
  -- Increase in small-business expensing limits, to $40,000 from
$25,000
  Sticking points
  -- Bush and Republicans still want: Acceleration of individual-tax
rate cuts; changing the corporate alternative-minimum tax.
  -- Democrats still want:Subsidies to help unemployed people to buy

 Article 218 *******************    



E-Commerce (A Special Report): Consumer's Guide --- Inspired Choice: For many would-be artists, the Web is their Muse 

By Alexandra Kaptik 
1,785 words 
11 February 2002
The Wall Street Journal 
R9 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

WHEN WILLIAM KRETZ, a software engineer from Arlington, Texas, decided to begin writing fiction as a hobby, he headed for that old favorite haven of would-be artists: the coffeehouse. But his destination wasn't some smoky beat refuge or even a glossy Starbucks. Instead, it was a Web site called Coffeehouse for Writers. 

The site is one of several online rallying points for budding authors, offering homespun free advice as well as some how-to courses that charge fees. For the 29-year-old Mr. Kretz -- who was struggling to write a first-person fictional account of the inner life of a problem-ridden superhero -- the big draw was that other denizens of the site kept prodding him to improve his writing while pushing him not to give up. 

"I would never have thought I could do it without their encouragement," says Mr. Kretz. When he posted his first chapter on a message board sponsored by the site, www.coffeehouseforwriters.com, he expected rave reviews but didn't get them. "I was stunned -- I really thought my work was nearly perfect," he says. But the reaction of the writers convinced him that his first draft was "a literary piece of garbage" with far too many unnecessary words. 

To help him along, one writer took on the task of nitpicking his entire work, one chapter at a time. "By chapter nine, I learned how to reword sentences on my own," says Mr. Kretz, who is currently submitting his first short story to a magazine. 

So it goes as more aspiring artists, writers and musicians turn to the Internet to spark their creativity, to nurture their imagination, to goad themselves to greatness. In the tangible world, finding one's muse is often a matter of rare luck. But the Web can be a muse itself, allowing far-flung people to share ideas conveniently and instantly -- and at the very moment they feel most stumped or discouraged. 

Got writer's block? So do a lot of other Hemingway hopefuls. Need a rhyme for a song lyric? You are not alone. Whether you are looking for a list of grammatical misdeeds to avoid, or just need a pat on the back to fend off loneliness and help you keep going, there are sites out there promising to help. And people from all walks of life are using these online resources to revive a dormant talent or try something new. 

Some of the sites make money by evaluating submissions, offering workshops or selling ads. Others are run by people who do it as a labor of love; they are looking for inspiration themselves or just want to inspire others. 

The Coffeehouse falls somewhere in between. It charges fees for some of its online offerings, from a $25 weekend workshop to a $225 three-month course called "Writing Fiction 101." These workshops are conducted solely by e-mail, usually with at most nine students, who get writing tips and at least two assignments a week from a "facilitator." Karen Hertzberg of Oconomowoc, Wis., who started the Coffeehouse in 1999, says that the site "may not be raking in all kinds of money, but it is financially sound and not in debt -- a rather amazing feat in this unstable dot-com economy." She says that her real motivation in launching the Web site was to share what she has learned about the craft "and allow others to do the same." 

Whatever the motives, the sites are a godsend for many. Take Scott R. Taylor, the 52-year-old owner of a small thermoplastics-manufacturing business in Bartlesville, Okla. He was looking for more fulfillment than he could get from his doctorate in organic chemistry. So he resumed his passion for songwriting, which was born in his youth when he sang in church and school choirs. 

Mr. Taylor began writing songs again seven years ago, and the Internet has been invaluable. For starters, he says, the Net is an "incredible source of information." He also uses message boards to get critiques and encouragement from other musicians. "It's a whole new medium for letting people hear complete songs . . . lyrics and music," Mr. Taylor says. He even wrote one song in collaboration with a person he met on a music site, Just Plain Folks. 

Mr. Taylor now dashes off at least a dozen songs a year. Most are in the country-music vein, such as his barstool lament with the refrain, "I'm just two six-packs from lovin' you." At one point he assembled a full compact disk ("Dreamin' I'm a Cowboy") and offers it for sale online. 

Among the various Web sites aimed at songwriters, Mr. Taylor favors Just Plain Folks (www.jpfolks.com/home.html) for its discussion forums and songwriting-collaboration chats. He'll visit the site of musician Irene Jackson (www.irenejackson.com) to dole out advice and get some for himself on the live songwriting chats she hosts. And he sometimes turns to the site called Songwriting Education Resource (www.craftofsongwriting.com), where a "writer's room" coaches would-be songwriters on how to sell their work. On a more humble scale, there's the site www.rhymezone.com, which provides rhyming words for stumped lyricists. 

For many writers, all the muse they need can be found in the most-common online tool: the search engine. "The Internet is a tremendous research tool," says David Lemberg, a 52-year-old chiropractor in New York. He has been working on a novel and screenplay about healing ethnic hatred in the Middle East. "I've never traveled in the Middle East," Mr. Lemberg says, but "whenever I need any bit of information that's not in my head, I use the Copernic search engine and, boom -- I find exactly what I'm looking for." 

A different search engine, Google, is the major muse of Roger A. Wehage, a 58-year-old engineer from Dunlap, Ill., who also writes fiction. "I've been writing technical junk ever since college -- thousands of mathematical equations, stuff you'd fall asleep reading in a minute," Mr. Wehage says. But when it's time to nail down the technical twists in a plotline, he turns to the Web. 

One time, for example, "I was researching a story involving highly intelligent crawling and flying robots the size of sugar ants and fruit flies," he recalls. Mr. Wehage scoured dozens of Web sites to learn what he could easily absorb on "nanotechnology, molecular biology, robot theory; you name it. . . . I looked up hundreds of references on the things that make ants and flies do all the things their tiny little bodies do. And I let my imagination run wild. It's amazing, I tell you. I don't have to be an expert in any of these areas to write great fiction. I just need enough knowledge so what I write is credible." 

Before he tried his hand at fiction, Mr. Wehage says, he used to watch television for hours on end -- a habit he has since dropped. "Cutting out television has given me more time to spend on the Internet," he says. 

Other authors find inspiration at sites geared specifically to their craft. Among these, one of the most comprehensive is Writer's Write (www.writerswrite.com). It has links to reference resources including dictionaries, encyclopedias, government agencies and science and health sites that writers might want to visit. There is information on writers' conferences, job listings and media-industry news. There's also a monthly feature called Internet Writing Journal, a free online magazine that interviews established novelists, screenwriters and journalists to glean advice on how to improve writing skills. In one recent issue, Lawrence Block, author of the books "Hope to Die" and "Hit List," offered guidance to new writers on how to create realistic dialogue for characters. 

People who run Web communities say there is a clear need to bring artists together online. "I think many songwriters feel very isolated. They wonder if there's anyone else out there doing what they do," says Jodi Krangle, Toronto, who in 1995 began Muse's Muse -- a site dedicated to songwriting. It's a place "for songwriters to get their lyrics critiqued and a meeting place for people to get together, talk over issues and even do a little co-writing," she says. The Web site (www.musesmuse.com) has message boards and live chats as well as listings about festivals, showcases and conferences; songwriting classifieds for bands looking for fresh music or new singers; publishing tips; staff-written articles and Muse Radio shows, which are broadcast on the Internet, promoting independent songwriters and their work. To make money, the site accepts advertising for musician directories, instrument accessories and recording software. 

Taking a different tack, Mary Ann Halpin uses her Web site (www.maryannhalpin.com) to reach out to burned-out professionals. A photographer in Los Angeles, she describes herself as the muse of creativity -- it's even printed on her business cards. Ms. Halpin spends part of the year 80 miles north of the city, offering in-person creative-renewal seminars in Pine Mountain Club, Calif. She set up the seminars after finishing a photography book left her depressed and feeling worn-out four years ago. "Creativity shouldn't be forced," she says. "It should flow like a river." 

--- 

Ms. Kaptik is an editor for The Wall Street Journal Online in New York.

 Article 219 *******************    



Small-Stock Focus: Overture Services, JMAR Surge As Small Caps Post a Slight Gain 

By Larry Bauman 
Dow Jones Newswires 
810 words 
13 February 2002
The Wall Street Journal 
C8 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

NEW YORK -- Small-capitalization stocks inched higher in thin trading, extending their winning streak to three straight sessions. 

The overall Nasdaq market, however, fell moderately on the weakness of technology and telecommunications stocks. 

Concerns about accounting irregularities, which have weighed on the stock market for the past week, have started to abate. But traders said that while that relieved some of the downward pressure on stocks, investors were hard-pressed to find a compelling reason to be aggressive buyers. 

The Russell 2000 index of small-capitalization stocks rose 0.69 point, or 0.15%, to 472.01, and the Nasdaq Composite Index, at 1834.21, fell 12.45 points, or 0.67%. 

"What we need is clarity on the economy," said Jeff Petherick, portfolio manager at NorthPointe Capital. Until there is evidence that the economy has started to recover from recession, he said, the stock market "will be in a state of funk." 

Overture Services, a Pasadena, Calif., online search engine formerly known as GoTo.com, leapt $5.91, or 27%, to 27.66. The company -- whose stock had been hurt by fears about its ability to renew contracts -- announced it reached a deal with Microsoft Corp.'s MSN network to provide paid search listings. Goldman Sachs added the stock to its recommended list, upgrading it from market outperformer. 

JMAR Technologies jumped 54 cents, or 20%, to 3.28. The San Diego provider of precision micro- and nanotechnology products said that its JMAR Research unit was awarded an Army contract valued at as much as $34.5 million. The contract is for the company's one-nanometer point source X-ray lithography system to produce high-performance semiconductor integrated circuits. 

Glenayre Technologies climbed 34 cents, or 16%, to 2.44, following Monday's 14% gain. The Charlotte, N.C., telecommunications-equipment company reported fourth-quarter break-even results, exceeding analysts' projections. The company said that it still expects to report a first-quarter loss of one to three cents a share, but that it expects to achieve "sustained profitability" in the third quarter, which it said is "a quarter ahead of current expectations." 

NetScreen Technologies gained 2.15, or 13%, to 18.50. Wachovia Securities raised its rating on the Sunnyvale, Calif., provider of Internet security systems and appliances to strong buy from buy. 

United Surgical Partners International, a Dallas operator of surgical facilities, rose 2.10, or 12%, to 19.95. The company said that it expects 2002 earnings of 52 cents to 55 cents a share, compared with analysts' forecasts of a 50-cent-a-share profit. United Surgical, which went public in June, hasn't released 2001 results yet. 

Pegasus Solutions added 1.08, or 7.1%, to 16.20. CIBC World Markets raised its rating on the Dallas provider of hotel reservation transaction-processing services to strong buy from buy. 

Borland Software was up 59 cents, or 4.3%, to 14.39. The Scotts Valley, Calif., software-development-products company reaffirmed the first-quarter and full-year 2002 guidance it provided in late January. The company said that it reiterated its forecasts because the stock fell 17% Monday, and that it couldn't account for the stock's weakness. 

Aeroflex, a Plainview, N.Y., maker of modules and integrated circuits, tumbled 3.50, or 22%, to 12.40. The company posted quarterly earnings that were down from the year-earlier period on a 21% decline in sales, but which beat analysts' projections. But the company said that its third-quarter profit will fall short of expectations. 

Immersion, a San Jose, Calif., developer of sensory technology for video games, fell 92 cents, or 20%, to 3.58. The company reported a fourth-quarter loss of 16 cents a share, compared with its year-earlier 29-cent-a-share loss. J.P. Morgan and Bear Stearns lowered their ratings on the stock. 

LCC International dropped 1.26, or 18%, to 5.74. The McLean, Va., telecommunications engineering and consulting company posted a fourth-quarter loss of four cents a share, compared with its year-earlier 11-cent-a-share profit, with its revenue declining 46%. Raymond James & Associates and Credit Suisse First Boston lowered their ratings on the stock. 

The American depositary receipts of Amarin, a London pharmaceutical company, slid 1.94, or 13.7%, to 12.18 after posting quarterly earnings, excluding items, of 28 cents per ADR, compared with its year-earlier 30-cent-a-share profit. Excluding items, the company's loss was $1.39 per ADR. 

Salix Pharmaceuticals was down 1.56, or 9%, to 15.70. The Raleigh, N.C., drug developer posted a fourth-quarter loss that was much wider than its year-earlier loss, but narrower than analysts had predicted.

 Article 221 *******************    



Developments to Watch 

NANO DEVICES THAT ARE ON SALE NOW 

By Otis Port Edited by Catherine Arnst 
264 words 
18 March 2002
BusinessWeek 
93 
Number 3774 
English
(Copyright 2002 McGraw-Hill, Inc.) 

Nano-this and Nano-that: Nano prefixes seem destined to become as common as dot-com suffixes. While many startups are only talking about building devices with nanometer-scale elements--100 to 1,000 times thinner than a human hair--one is now selling actual products. NanoOpto in Somerset, N.J., has just unveiled a trio of optical components for use in fiber-optic networks. 

NanoOpto's components are designed to polarize, split, and recombine the beams of laser light flowing through optical fibers. But they don't look like any lens or optical prism you've ever seen. Under a microscope, they could be waffles for hungry microbes. The rectangular shapes rising from the grid are only about 100 nanometers wide, or less than the wavelengths of laser light that they process. That's important, says NanoOpto Chairman Stephen Y. Chou, who's also a Princeton University professor, because sub-wavelength optical elements don't operate on the same principles as macro-size optics. So they don't have to be aligned as precisely as conventional devices. Chou expects this to create major savings for companies that build fiber-optic equipment. 

How does NanoOpto make its teensy waffles? With a miniature waffle iron. Chou developed a method that essentially molds the pattern on a wafer four inches across, one wafer per minute, which then gets cut into thousands of components. Soon, the company will begin producing multi-tier waffles so one component can perform multiple functions.

 Article 222 *******************    



The Property Report 

Plots & Ploys 

By Peter Grant 
821 words 
20 March 2002
The Wall Street Journal 
B8 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

[What's Brewing in the Real Estate Market] 

Paramount Price 

A GERMAN investment company that has a history of paying top dollar for Manhattan office property has beaten a crowded field of competitors to win the bidding for the office tower at 745 Fifth Ave., people with knowledge of the deal say. 

Paramount Group, the investment arm of Germany's Otto family, has agreed to pay more than $270 million for the 500,000-square-foot building, more than $10 million over what experts thought the property would fetch. At that price, the property's annual cash return amounts to just below 7.5%, people say. The deal is a further sign that the market for midtown Manhattan property is one of the hottest in the country. 

Paramount has been an active buyer of Manhattan property since the 1970s, during both booms and busts. In 1998, Paramount paid one of the highest prices ever for a New York office building, shelling out $283 million, or $525 a square foot, for 712 Fifth Ave. 

Officials with Paramount and Goldman Sachs Group, which is representing the seller of 745 Fifth, Rodamco N.A., declined to comment. 

Vacant Look 

THE WIDESPREAD belief that the recession is over has brought little joy to the office leasing market. Many analysts are predicting the vacancy rate will continue to rise for the rest of this year, with little improvement next year. Steve Sakwa of Merrill Lynch, for example, predicts the rate will top out around 16.5% by year end, just 2.6 percentage points lower than the peak of the last recession. "We don't think market conditions are going to get a lot better until 2004 at the earliest," says Mr. Sakwa. 

Why the gloomy forecast? Demand for office space follows employment growth, and many economists don't think there is going to be much of that for at least a year. Economy.com Inc., a business consulting firm, projects slight job growth beginning in the third quarter of this year. "But we really don't get cooking until the middle of 2003," says Craig Thomas, a senior economist with the firm. 

New supply also is a problem. About 84 million square feet of new space was in the pipeline at the end of last year and will be delivered in 2002 and 2003, according to Merrill Lynch. Though that is down about 40% from space on the way at the end of 2000, it still represents a 2.8% increase in the nation's inventory at a time of anemic demand. 

There is one encouraging statistic, though: These days an average office worker occupies 180 square feet, down from 210 square feet in 1991, says Stuart Seeley, of UBS Warburg. That means there is little space in existing offices to put new workers when hiring begins. "We're already stacked up like cordwood," he says. "The spring-back in demand for space is likely to be a hair more robust." 

NASA Plan Grounded 

THE GLUT of office space in Silicon Valley is putting a crimp in the National Aeronautics and Space Administration's dream of developing a sprawling research and education complex on part of Moffett Field, a former naval air station in the San Francisco Bay area. 

The centerpiece of the complex is the proposed Carl Sagan Center for the Study of Life in the Cosmos, a 110,000-square-foot laboratory where government, private-sector and university scientists would study such subjects as astrobiology and nanotechnology. This research would be used in the search for life by robotic space missions. 

But NASA needs the private sector to bankroll the $41 million development. The agency had been hoping to persuade a Silicon Valley company to do this by giving it, in return, development rights to 20 acres adjoining the site. But this incentive has lost its luster because of the millions of square feet of empty space on the market. "Office space out here is cheap and plentiful," says Mike Marlaire, assistant development director for NASA Research Park. 

Mr. Marlaire says NASA is still hopeful that it will be able to cut a deal with a company that will enable it to open the Carl Sagan Center in two or three years. "Anyone who knows what's planned out here realizes it's a gold mine," he says. 

Building Block 

The Orlando, Fla., office market, where the vacancy rate rose to 17.7% at the end of 2001 from 12.3% a year earlier, is about to get some bad news from Siemens Westinghouse Power Corp. The company has hired Alter Group, of Chicago, to build it a second building in an office park near the University of Central Florida, this one 226,000 square feet. Siemens will vacate leased space when it moves into the new building, people involved in the project say.

 Article 224 *******************    



Boom Town: Little White Ones You Might Hear From the Techies 

By Kara Swisher 
873 words 
1 April 2002
The Wall Street Journal 
B1 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

The weather has turned springlike earlier than usual this year in Silicon Valley, bolstering a feeling of new promise that has been creeping into the tech industry. 

The word in the press, at industry parties (they have returned!) and conferences (they never went away) is that Silicon Valley is back, and ready to roar again. Earnings warnings are down, venture funding is up, layoffs and bankruptcies have slowed. 

Only 18 Internet companies were shuttered in February, says Webmergers.com, which tallies dot-com shutdowns. That's "the lowest casualty rate since August 2000," it says, and is part of a decline in failures since a peak in May last year. 

But if Silicon Valley is back, so are its rationalizations about what's really going on here. Several years ago, at the start of the boom in early 1998, I wrote about the "Lies of Silicon Valley," that listed favorites such as "It's not about the money" and "We sell solutions, not products." Those seemingly harmless circumlocutions set the tone for some poor choices that followed. 

Now, along with the feeling of new promise, the Valley offers some new mendacities as well. Among them: 

Failure has been a good thing. This has become an overriding theme among the digerati since the downturn began in the spring of 2000. You can't finish your seared sea bass in a local restaurant without some venture capitalist or entrepreneur or banker telling you that they have learned a lot more from their loser investments than from any success. 

That's all well and good, as long as you were not the one getting burned. Most of the big players here, in fact, cashed out early and often, leaving too many others, mostly individual investors and lesser employees, out of luck. "Failure is humbling, character-building and can teach you, but it is definitely not good," says Julie Wainwright, former CEO of Pets.com, one of the first high-profile dot-com failures. 

It's true that experimentation is at the heart of the Silicon Valley experience, but in the last cycle those with the most responsibility didn't pay the price for the risks associated with innovation. 

We've learned our lessons. This gem goes hand in hand with the delusion in Silicon Valley that failure is always a virtue. Most here -- often the most egregious of offenders -- will give sincere speeches about the ways in which they will change and the things that will be different from here on out, with a focus on the bottom line, mature management and cautious growth. In a running joke of his, Amazon founder Jeff Bezos says he now spells profit with an "f" rather than as "prophet." 

Let's hope that will bring the sector to the Promised Land, although recent hype over the world-changing virtues of high-speed wireless and nanotechnology (a genre that includes microscopic machines and such) sounds suspiciously familiar. 

The next hot thing is. . . . Silicon Valley never loses its hunger for what's coming next. A big fight is poised to break out this spring over small devices that e-mail and phone at the same time. A range of products will be released, and they are all certainly intriguing. But their utility is still unproven. 

What should be hot is making all that has been dreamed up over the past several years work well for consumers, giving them confidence to use the new technologies more. 

Tech is poised to soar. That's the feeling here and on Wall Street, as stocks of many major tech companies rebounded nicely from lows of last year. It's a good thing, but many experts still think growth is going to be a lot slower through the year than in past years. 

In a report in February, Morgan Stanley, which surveyed tech trends and spending patterns of 225 chief information officers, estimated that 2002 information technology growth will be flat at about 2%, better than 2001's estimated 1.5%, but no where near the 12% of 2000. 

According to the report, new projects for the year are likely to be smaller in size and scope, with application integration and security software leading the list of priorities. Not too exciting, but much needed. 

"Tech spending will lag the recovery in capital spending and the economy in general," says Chuck Phillips, the tech analyst at Morgan Stanley who wrote the report. But "generally, we have a slightly more positive bias than we did three months ago." 

Silicon Valley is back. Um, it never went away. That made the spate of woe-is-me wailings of 2001 almost as unbearable to listen to as the hype of 1999. The endless self-promoting billboards are gone, along with the greedheads. Good and steady entrepreneurs and promising young companies can ply their talents in a much saner environment. 

That's a forecast we call can live with. 

--- 

Has Silicon Valley really learned its lesson from the downturn or is it still fooling itself about its prospects? Write me at kara.swisher@wsj.com and come see the online debate Friday at WSJ.com/BoomTown.

Business The Bucks of Summer 

Davide Dukcevich 

Another April, and new hope for baseball fans that their team can win it all. Forbes.com's special report, the Business of Baseball, checks the game's financial box score. Think today's top players are richer than Croesus? Our decade-by-decade time line of stars' salaries reveals them to be falling behind chief executives'--though both groups tend to get their biggest paychecks after their careers have peaked. 

We name the only baseball club owner to make the starting roster of our first pro-sports All Star Owners team. And for memorabilia buffs, we scout the rally potential of rookie cards of yesteryear. 

LifestyleThe V-12 Coming to a VW Near You 

Michael Frank 

On page 286 of this issue, we explain why the technology in the new V-12 engine powering the world's most expensive sports car may end up in Volkswagens. What does it feel like to drive such raw power? Forbes.com has taken the first test drive (on American soil) of the new Lamborghini Murcielago. Read our exclusive review of the car and its engine.

In ``Nanotechnology'' (The Tech Outlook, BusinessWeek 50, Apr. 8), Mitsubishi's $100 million nanotech venture fund is sponsored by Mitsubishi Corp., not Mitsubishi Electric Corp.

BusinessPost Your Ideas--And Why Not?Paul Maidment 

A powerful aspect of the Web is that it lets a publication's readers talk back to the editors and to one other. We sensed that would be a great way to tap into a particularly Forbesian kind of ingenuity: simple but unconventional ideas to improve business. Last issue we launched a column--both in print and online--that is designed to promote just that: "Why Not?" by Yale professors Ian Ayres and Barry Nalebuff. They asked you to tell them what's right or wrong with their ideas and to propose your own. Two weeks in, the Why Not? Forum on Forbes.com is well established as a gathering place for original ideas for products and services. The conversation continues. Come join.LifestyleMiami: Hot or Not?Christina Valhouli 

It might have great sunsets and proximity to Latin America, but if Miami wants to attract big companies it has to solve its problems of crime, poor education and outdated infrastructure. Forbes.com reports on a city struggling to shed its image as a neon-lit tourist destination full of cruise ships, drug lords and rap stars.

 Article 230 *******************    



Big Hopes Surround a Small Idea --- Science of Nanotechnology Is Gaining Enthusiasts, But Funding Is Still Scarce 

By Beth M. Mantz 
Dow Jones Newswires 
1,063 words 
1 May 2002
The Wall Street Journal
B11F 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

NEW YORK -- During the past year, scientists, entrepreneurs and futurists have barely been able to contain their excitement about nanotechnology. But for all the buzz, this emerging science has yet to attract wide investment from the world of venture capital. 

Nanotechnology isn't an industry. Rather, it is the study and manipulation of matter at a scale measured by nanometers, units so minuscule it would take about 150,000 to span the diameter of a human hair. At this level, these structures take on new properties and could be used to develop products in industries ranging from materials and chemicals to electronics, and from telecommunications to medicine. The scientific promise is so great the federal government's National Science Foundation predicts nanotechnology will represent $1 trillion in goods and services by 2015. 

But the commercial applications of nanotechnology are still too far away for venture capitalists to open their wallets. 

"Up until now, [nanotechnology] has been a number of science projects, not a series of businesses ... and the payoff was five to 10 years off," said Chris Anzalone, partner at Washington, D.C., venture-capital and investment-banking firm Galway Partners and acting chief executive of NanoInk Inc., a start-up that is developing tools to build nanoscale structures one atom at a time. 

Venture capitalists want to invest in a business with products coming down the pike, a near-term expectation of revenue, and return on investment flowing back to limited partners. Only recently have these science projects begun to graduate from the research labs to businesses. 

"We are in the early days of nanotechnology," said Mel Melsheimer, president and chief operating officer of publicly traded venture firm Harris & Harris Group, which has already invested in early-stage nanotechnology companies. "As a VC, we have chosen to move forward, but most VC firms are evaluating and are interested to see success over time." 

That isn't to say investment hasn't found its way into nanotechnology, especially since former President Clinton established the National Nanotechnology Initiative in 1999. The federal government and academic institutions are increasingly loosening the purse strings. In fiscal year 2001, the federal government spent $463.9 million on nanotechnology programs. For fiscal 2002, ending in October, Congress will have appropriated about $604.4 million, which includes National Aeronautics and Space Administration and the U.S. Department of Agriculture programs. 

In his proposed budget for fiscal year 2003, President Bush requested a total allocation of $710 million for federal investment in nanoscale science, engineering, technology, and associated programs. 

Some corporations have invested in nanotechnology. International Business Machines Corp., Hewlett-Packard Co., Motorola Inc., Dow Chemical Co., Corning Inc. and 3M Co. each have launched significant nanotech initiatives. 

Venture capitalists have played a relatively small role, although the amount of actual investment from the venture-capital community is hard to track. 

NanoBusiness Alliance says start-up companies received $100 million in funding from corporations, angel investors and venture firms in 1999. A NanoBusiness Alliance report published last year estimates direct venture investment rose to $500 million in 2000 and then jumped to $800 million in 2001. It predicts such funding will reach $1 billion this year and $1.2 billion next year. Currently, corporations are responsible for most funding, but venture firms will take over as the field matures, said Mark Modzelewski, executive director of the organization. 

Meanwhile, VentureOne, which tracks the venture-capital community, disputes these findings, but agrees venture funding is on the rise. According to its research, only four venture-backed nanotechnology companies received a total of $23.9 million in financing in 1999, nine start-ups received a total of $137.4 million in 2000 and 14 got $82.3 million in 2001. Five companies have received $53 million so far this year. 

In the near term, venture capitalists remain cautious. New York private-equity firm Lux Capital is a prime example. Managing partner Josh Wolfe, who is considered a nanotechnology expert, expects nanotechnology will hasten huge changes in technology and business. Yet, to date, his firm hasn't invested in any nanotechnology-based companies. He expects the firm will fund its first nanotechnology company this quarter. 

"Nanotechnology is a wonderful thing. It can harness new properties that don't exist in nature at higher scales, but it is unpredictable," Mr. Wolfe said. 

Venture capitalists want a return on their investment within a fairly short period and few nanotechnology start-ups can deliver, said NanoBusiness's Mr. Modzelewski. Additionally, those companies that could generate products and revenue sooner rather than later are often engaged in materials, powders and plastics, businesses that tend not to attract venture capitalists because of competition with big corporate players. 

Those businesses that are expected to generate the sizable venture-capital returns -- those involved in the applications of nanotechnology -- are still in their relative infancy, said Mr. Modzelewski. They include drawing nanoscale semiconductor chips that could double the speed of computing at half the cost, developing new methods to screen for biological proteins or new drug targets, creating nanoscale drug-delivery systems, engineering "nanobots" that would combat disease at the molecular level, developing molecular electronic devices, and designing nanoscale optical components for telecom gear, to name a few. While some select start-ups involved in applications have secured venture capital, most of these fledgling firms haven't. 

If any one area is reeling in venture capital, it is the nanotechnology "picks and shovels" businesses, those companies developing tools that can pick or move atoms one at a time or devising software for modeling nanotechnology. Others are creating nanomaterials that will form the building blocks for applications in electronics, optics, telecommunications and life sciences. 

Though venture investment to date has been small, venture investors appear to be breaking up into two groups, say observers. One group contains those investors who focus solely on the science and thus enter very early and take on the most risk. 

The other group is more savvy. Appreciating the risk of investing in a science rather than a business, these investors look for a start-up's associations with proven pioneers, such as Nanosys Inc.'s Larry Boch, who has founded more than a dozen companies, many of which have become public companies.

Technology 

Scientists Say They Have Bonded Viruses, Synthetic Materials --- `Novel' Process by Researchers In Texas Could Be Used To Form New Substances 

By Robert Tomsho 
426 words 
3 May 2002
The Wall Street Journal 
B5 
English
(Copyright (c) 2002, Dow Jones & Company, Inc.) 

Researchers at the University of Texas, harnessing nature's microscopic building powers, said they have devised a way to use noninfectious viruses to grow a hybrid material that could be used to make semiconductors and various other electronic, magnetic and optical devices. 

Their study, detailed in an article in the journal Science, involved making naturally occurring molecules adhere to minute particles of man-made materials and then self-assemble into whole new structures. Researchers elsewhere said the bonding between organic and inorganic materials is a potentially important advance in the fast-growing field of nanotechnology, which involves the manipulation of atoms and molecules to form new substances. 

"This is a novel approach," said Harold Craighead, a professor of applied and engineering physics at Cornell University, in Ithaca, N.Y. 

Chad Mirkin, director of the Institute of Nanotechnology at Northwestern University, in Evanston, Ill., described the work as "pretty impressive," adding that the Texas researchers had managed to persuade molecules to "do things that Mother Nature hasn't refined." 

Lead researcher Angela Belcher, an assistant professor of chemistry and microchemistry at the University of Texas, in Austin, said the group set out to mimic the processes that nature uses to make substances such as shell or bone. Ms. Belcher said the process used in the study could eventually be used to grow and arrange parts for a variety of devices, displays and sensors. 

In nature, some molecules naturally snap together when exposed to each other. In the study, molecules of the genetically engineered virus were repeatedly exposed to so-called quantum dots, a microscopic substance used in semiconductor manufacturing. In the presence of a chemical solution and a magnetic field, the molecules began bonding to the dots and each other. Multiplying, they eventually assembling themselves into liquid crystal structures several centimeters long. 

Nanotechnology derives its name from a unit of measure called the nanometer, a billion of which make up a meter. 

Predicting that the field could involve more than $1 trillion in goods and services by the year 2015, the federal government plans to invest more than $600 million in related research this year. Dozens of universities and technology concerns have opened related research facilities and competition for scientists in the field is keen. Indeed, in the fall, Ms. Belcher and much of her research team at the University of Texas will move to the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, in Cambridge.
